- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Synchrophasor VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the instantaneous magnitude, phase, and frequency values of the fundamental frequency component as defined in the P class of performance in IEEE Std C37.118.1-2011. This VI resamples even-time-spaced voltage and current

### FPGA Phasor Measurement for P Class VI

**Owning Palette:** Synchrophasor VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the instantaneous magnitude, phase, and frequency values of the fundamental frequency component as defined in the P class of performance in IEEE Std C37.118.1-2011. This VI resamples even-time-spaced voltage and current signals into even-angle-spaced signals. The input signals must have a sample rate of 50 kHz. The output rate is 25 samples per cycle. You must manually select the polymorphic instance to use.

**Related Information**
Handshaking Protocol

Example

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

FPGA Phasor Measurement for P Class (50 Hz)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/FPGA_Phasor_Measurement_for_P_Class__50_Hz.gif']

|  | number of channels specifies the number of channels of the input data. The default is 1. This VI supports up to 16 channels. |
| --- | --- |
|  | signal in specifies the voltage signals or current signals whose sample rate must be 50 kHz. When number of channels is greater than 2, the multichannel signal in must be interleaved. Note Because the input data type is <±,24,1> fixed-point and different analog acquisition modules have different fixed-point data types, the raw data from the data acquisition module must be divisible by a certain number so that the full range of the divided result can be represented as <±,24,1> fixed-point numbers without overflow. For example, the data type of NI 9225 is <±,24,10>, which means the raw data must be divisible by 512. Alternatively, you can right-shift the data by 9 bits and then convert the data type to <±,24,1> fixed-point. |
|  | timestamp specifies the timestamp, in seconds, of each sample of the input signal. The timestamp is the number of seconds since the International Atomic Time (TAI) epoch of 00:00:00, January 1, 1970, UTC time. The timestamp is not adjusted for leap seconds. |
|  | reference channel index specifies the channel of the input data that this VI uses as a reference to detect the resampling time and frequency. The default is 0. This VI usually uses a voltage channel as the reference channel. reference channel index must be less than number of channels. |
|  | input valid? specifies whether the next data point has arrived for processing. The default is TRUE. Use a handshaking protocol to wire the output valid? output of an upstream node to this input to transfer data from the upstream node to this node. |
|  | ready for output? specifies whether downstream nodes are ready for this node to return a new value. The default is TRUE. Use a handshaking protocol to wire the ready for input? output of a downstream node through a Feedback Node to this input of the current node. Note If this input is FALSE during a given cycle, the output valid? output returns FALSE during that cycle. |
|  | output data returns the instantaneous magnitude, phase, frequency, and timestamp values. The output rate is 25 samples per cycle. magnitude returns the instantaneous magnitude, in RMS value, of the fundamental frequency at even-spaced angle intervals. Note If you scaled the input data from the data type of the analog input (AI) module to <±,24,1> fixed-point, you must scale the output back using the same scale factor. phase returns the instantaneous phase, in pi radians, of the fundamental frequency at even-spaced angle intervals. timestamp returns the timestamp, in seconds, of each sample of the output signal. The timestamp is the number of seconds since the International Atomic Time (TAI) epoch of 00:00:00, January 1, 1970, UTC time. The timestamp is not adjusted for leap seconds. frequency returns the instantaneous frequency at even-spaced angle intervals. |
|  | magnitude returns the instantaneous magnitude, in RMS value, of the fundamental frequency at even-spaced angle intervals. Note If you scaled the input data from the data type of the analog input (AI) module to <±,24,1> fixed-point, you must scale the output back using the same scale factor. |
|  | phase returns the instantaneous phase, in pi radians, of the fundamental frequency at even-spaced angle intervals. |
|  | timestamp returns the timestamp, in seconds, of each sample of the output signal. The timestamp is the number of seconds since the International Atomic Time (TAI) epoch of 00:00:00, January 1, 1970, UTC time. The timestamp is not adjusted for leap seconds. |

|  | output valid? returns TRUE when this node has computed a result that downstream nodes can use. Use a handshaking protocol to wire this output to the input valid? input of a downstream node to transfer data from the node to the downstream node. |
|  | error returns error information about the phasor measurement process. |
|  | ready for input? returns TRUE when this node is ready to accept new input data. Use a handshaking protocol to wire this output through a Feedback Node to the ready for output? input of an upstream node. Note If this output returns FALSE during a given cycle, LabVIEW discards any data that other nodes send to this node during the following cycle. LabVIEW discards this data even if the input valid? input is TRUE during the following cycle. |

FPGA Phasor Measurement for P Class (60 Hz)

Example

Refer to the Synchrophasor Measurement.lvproj in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Synchrophasor directory for an example of using the FPGA Phasor Measurement for P Class VI.

Parent topic:

Synchrophasor VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=fpga-reclosing-logic-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00059: FPGA Reclosing Logic VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `fpga-reclosing-logic-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/fpga-reclosing-logic-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Protection VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Controls the automatic reclosing and lockout of a circuit breaker based on the reclosing settings that you specify. This VI also displays the reclosing status. Example reset? specifies to reset the outputs of this VI to their default

### FPGA Reclosing Logic VI

**Owning Palette:** Protection VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Controls the automatic reclosing and lockout of a circuit breaker based on the reclosing settings that you specify. This VI also displays the reclosing status.

Example

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/FPGA_Reclosing_Logic.gif']

|  | reset? specifies to reset the outputs of this VI to their default values and to reset the reclosing logic to be ready for a new round of judging process. The default is FALSE. |
| --- | --- |
|  | reclosing settings specifies the settings of the reclosing logic to decide how and when to reclose the circuit breaker. number of reclosing attempts specifies the number of reclosing attempts in a reclosing sequence. The default is 3. You can specify up to seven reclosing attempts. If the number of reclosing attempts exceed the number that you specify, the reclosing logic enters into a lockout state. dead times specifies the time period, in milliseconds, from the instant when trigger shot? turns to TRUE, to the instant when a following reclosing attempt starts. The default values of dead times for the default three reclosing attempts are 200, 11000, and 10000. If dead times are set to 0, the time period is only one tick. close pulse duration specifies the duration of the close pulse signal, in milliseconds, that recloses the circuit breaker. The default is 100. Refer to manuals of circuit breakers for the minimum required pulse duration. If close pulse duration is set to 0, the duration is only one tick. reclaim time specifies the time period, in milliseconds, from the instant when start reclaim timing? turns to TRUE, to the instant when reclaim time expired? returns TRUE. The default is 3000. If reclaim time is set to 0, reclaim time expired? returns TRUE at the instant when start reclaim timing? turns to TRUE. enable synchronism check? specifies whether to receive synchronism check results. The default is FALSE. When you set enable synchronism check? to TRUE, this VI returns a close pulse if the synchronism check passes during a dead time. So this VI does not need to wait for the dead time to expire for reclosing the circuit breaker. If you set enable synchronism check? to FALSE, this VI ignores synchronism check passed?. enable lockout reset? specifies whether to reset the reclosing logic when lockout reset time expires. The default is FALSE, which specifies that the lockout state ends until you send a manual close command to close the circuit breaker. lockout reset time specifies the time period, in milliseconds, from the instant when the reclosing logic enters into a lockout state, to the instant when this VI resets the reclosing logic. The default is 5000. If lockout reset time is set to 0, the lockout duration is only one tick. |
|  | number of reclosing attempts specifies the number of reclosing attempts in a reclosing sequence. The default is 3. You can specify up to seven reclosing attempts. If the number of reclosing attempts exceed the number that you specify, the reclosing logic enters into a lockout state. |
|  | dead times specifies the time period, in milliseconds, from the instant when trigger shot? turns to TRUE, to the instant when a following reclosing attempt starts. The default values of dead times for the default three reclosing attempts are 200, 11000, and 10000. If dead times are set to 0, the time period is only one tick. |
|  | close pulse duration specifies the duration of the close pulse signal, in milliseconds, that recloses the circuit breaker. The default is 100. Refer to manuals of circuit breakers for the minimum required pulse duration. If close pulse duration is set to 0, the duration is only one tick. |
|  | reclaim time specifies the time period, in milliseconds, from the instant when start reclaim timing? turns to TRUE, to the instant when reclaim time expired? returns TRUE. The default is 3000. If reclaim time is set to 0, reclaim time expired? returns TRUE at the instant when start reclaim timing? turns to TRUE. |
|  | enable synchronism check? specifies whether to receive synchronism check results. The default is FALSE. When you set enable synchronism check? to TRUE, this VI returns a close pulse if the synchronism check passes during a dead time. So this VI does not need to wait for the dead time to expire for reclosing the circuit breaker. If you set enable synchronism check? to FALSE, this VI ignores synchronism check passed?. |
|  | enable lockout reset? specifies whether to reset the reclosing logic when lockout reset time expires. The default is FALSE, which specifies that the lockout state ends until you send a manual close command to close the circuit breaker. |
|  | lockout reset time specifies the time period, in milliseconds, from the instant when the reclosing logic enters into a lockout state, to the instant when this VI resets the reclosing logic. The default is 5000. If lockout reset time is set to 0, the lockout duration is only one tick. |
|  | start reclaim timing? specifies whether the reclaim timer starts counting. The default is TRUE. When start reclaim timing? turns to TRUE, the reclaim timer starts counting for a time period that you specify in reclaim time if start reclaim timing? stays TRUE. At the instant when the reclaim time expires, reclaim time expired? returns TRUE. In most cases, you connect circuit breaker status to start reclaim timing?. After the circuit breaker closes and remains closed for a time period that you specify in reclaim time, reclaim time expired? returns TRUE, which resets the reclosing logic. |
|  | trigger shot? specifies whether to start the reclosing shot. You can add logical operations for deciding under what conditions the reclosing shot starts. For example, you can define that the shot starts at the instant when the trip signal asserts, the circuit breaker opens, or both the trip signal asserts and the circuit breaker opens. |
|  | force to lockout state? specifies whether to force the reclosing logic to a lockout state. The default is FALSE. You can add logical operations for deciding under what conditions the reclosing logic changes to the lockout state. For example, you can define that the reclosing logic changes to the lockout state when a manual close command closes the circuit breaker during a reclosing sequence. |
|  | block close pulse? specifies whether to block the close pulse. The default is FALSE. Blocking the close pulse does not affect the process of the reclosing sequence. |
|  | synchronism check passed? specifies whether the synchronism check passes. The default is FALSE. The dead state ends before dead times expires when synchronism check passed? is TRUE. If you set enable synchronism check? to FALSE, this VI ignores synchronism check passed?. |
|  | reclosing status returns the status of the reclosing process. sequence in progress? returns TRUE when the reclosing sequence is in progress. lockout? returns TRUE when the reclosing logic moves to a lockout state. When shot count exceeds number of reclosing attempts you specify or when force to lockout state? is TRUE, the reclosing logic moves to the lockout state. reclaim time expired? returns TRUE when the reclaim time expires, which represents a successful reclosing operation. At the instant when the reclaim time expires, the reclosing logic returns to the reset state. |
|  | sequence in progress? returns TRUE when the reclosing sequence is in progress. |
|  | lockout? returns TRUE when the reclosing logic moves to a lockout state. When shot count exceeds number of reclosing attempts you specify or when force to lockout state? is TRUE, the reclosing logic moves to the lockout state. |
|  | reclaim time expired? returns TRUE when the reclaim time expires, which represents a successful reclosing operation. At the instant when the reclaim time expires, the reclosing logic returns to the reset state. |
|  | shot count returns the current reclosing shot number. |
|  | dead state? returns TRUE when the reclosing sequence is in a dead state. If any one of dead times expires or synchronism check passed? is TRUE during the dead time, dead state? turns to FALSE. |
|  | close pulse? returns a close pulse signal that recloses the circuit breaker. Use close pulse duration to specify the pulse duration of the close pulse signal. |

Example

Refer to the 79 Autoreclosing.lvproj in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Protection\79 Autoreclosing directory for an example of using the FPGA Reclosing Logic VI.

Parent topic:

Protection VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=fpga-resampling-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00060: FPGA Resampling VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `fpga-resampling-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/fpga-resampling-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: FPGA VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Resamples even-time-spaced voltage and current signals into even-angle-spaced signals. The input signals must have a sample rate of 50 kHz. The output signals are synchronous with the line frequency with a constant number of samples per cyc

### FPGA Resampling VI

**Owning Palette:** FPGA VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Resamples even-time-spaced voltage and current signals into even-angle-spaced signals. The input signals must have a sample rate of 50 kHz. The output signals are synchronous with the line frequency with a constant number of samples per cycle. Use the Resampling for Power Quality VI if you want to perform resampling on the host computer. You must manually select the polymorphic instance to use.

This VI supports measuring single-bus single-line and single-bus multiple-line systems.

Details Examples

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

FPGA Resampling (50 Hz)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/FPGA_Resampling__50Hz.gif']

|  | number of channels specifies the number of channels of the input data. The default is 7. This VI supports up to 32 channels. |
| --- | --- |
|  | signal in specifies the voltage signals or current signals whose sample rate must be 50 kHz. When number of channels is greater than 2, the multichannel signal in must be interleaved. Note Because the input data type is <±,24,1> fixed-point and different analog acquisition modules have different fixed-point data types, the raw data from the data acquisition module must be divisible by a certain number so that the full range of the divided result can be represented as <±,24,1> fixed-point numbers without overflow. For example, the data type of NI 9225 is <±,24,10>, which means the raw data must be divisible by 512. Alternatively, you can right-shift the data by 9 bits and then convert the data type to <±,24,1> fixed-point. |
|  | timestamp specifies the timestamp, in seconds, of each sample of the input signal. timestamp is optional for power quality measurement. |
|  | reference channel index specifies the channel of the input data that this VI uses as a reference to detect the resampling time and frequency. The default is 0. This VI usually uses a voltage channel as the reference channel. reference channel index must be less than number of channels. |
|  | input valid? specifies whether the next data point has arrived for processing. The default is TRUE. Use a handshaking protocol to wire the output valid? output of an upstream node to this input to transfer data from the upstream node to this node. |
|  | ready for output? specifies whether downstream nodes are ready for this node to return a new value. The default is TRUE. Use a handshaking protocol to wire the ready for input? output of a downstream node through a Feedback Node to this input of the current node. Note If this input is FALSE during a given cycle, the output valid? output returns FALSE during that cycle. |
|  | samples per cycle specifies the number of samples per cycle to resample. samples per cycle must be even and within the range of 128 to 512. More samples per cycle requires more real-time computation resources of further processing on the resampled signal. Choosing low number of samples per cycle might make the signaling voltage measurement incompliant with IEC 61000-4-30:2008. The default is 192. For example, in 50 Hz systems, IEC 61000-4-30:2008 requires that the frequency of signaling voltage must be at most 3,000 Hz. If number of samples per cycle is 128, which means the sample rate after resampling is 6,400 Hz, the measured signaling voltage is right at the edge of the passband of resampling filters. The results might not be accurate enough. |
|  | resampled data returns information about the data after resampling. signal returns the resampled signal at even-spaced angle intervals. When number of channels is greater than two, the multichannel resampled signal is interleaved. Note If you scaled the input data from the data type of the analog input (AI) module to <±,24,1> fixed-point, you must scale the output back using the same scale factor. timestamp returns the timestamp, in seconds, of the resampled signal. frequency returns the instantaneous frequency at the timestamp of the resampled signal. |
|  | signal returns the resampled signal at even-spaced angle intervals. When number of channels is greater than two, the multichannel resampled signal is interleaved. Note If you scaled the input data from the data type of the analog input (AI) module to <±,24,1> fixed-point, you must scale the output back using the same scale factor. |
|  | timestamp returns the timestamp, in seconds, of the resampled signal. |
|  | frequency returns the instantaneous frequency at the timestamp of the resampled signal. |
|  | resampling info returns information about the measurement configuration for the resampled data that you can pass to further analysis. nominal frequency returns the nominal line frequency of the input signal. resampling valid? returns TRUE when the resampled signal is valid. resampling valid? returns FALSE when the detected frequency is out of range. samples per cycle returns the number of samples per cycle used for the resampling process. |
|  | nominal frequency returns the nominal line frequency of the input signal. |
|  | resampling valid? returns TRUE when the resampled signal is valid. resampling valid? returns FALSE when the detected frequency is out of range. |
|  | samples per cycle returns the number of samples per cycle used for the resampling process. |
|  | output valid? returns TRUE when this node has computed a result that downstream nodes can use. Use a handshaking protocol to wire this output to the input valid? input of a downstream node to transfer data from the node to the downstream node. |
|  | error returns error information about the resampling process. |

FPGA Resampling (60 Hz)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/FPGA_Resampling__60Hz.gif']

|  | number of channels specifies the number of channels of the input data. The default is 7. This VI supports up to 32 channels. |
| --- | --- |
|  | signal in specifies the voltage signals or current signals whose sample rate must be 50 kHz. When number of channels is greater than 2, the multichannel signal in must be interleaved. Note Because the input data type is <±,24,1> fixed-point and different analog acquisition modules have different fixed-point data types, the raw data from the data acquisition module must be divisible by a certain number so that the full range of the divided result can be represented as <±,24,1> fixed-point numbers without overflow. For example, the data type of NI 9225 is <±,24,10>, which means the raw data must be divisible by 512. Alternatively, you can right-shift the data by 9 bits and then convert the data type to <±,24,1> fixed-point. |
|  | timestamp specifies the timestamp, in seconds, of each sample of the input signal. timestamp is optional for power quality measurement. |
|  | reference channel index specifies the channel of the input data that this VI uses as a reference to detect the resampling time and frequency. The default is 0. This VI usually uses a voltage channel as the reference channel. reference channel index must be less than number of channels. |
|  | input valid? specifies whether the next data point has arrived for processing. The default is TRUE. Use a handshaking protocol to wire the output valid? output of an upstream node to this input to transfer data from the upstream node to this node. |
|  | ready for output? specifies whether downstream nodes are ready for this node to return a new value. The default is TRUE. Use a handshaking protocol to wire the ready for input? output of a downstream node through a Feedback Node to this input of the current node. Note If this input is FALSE during a given cycle, the output valid? output returns FALSE during that cycle. |
|  | samples per cycle specifies the number of samples per cycle to resample. samples per cycle must be even and within the range of 128 to 512. More samples per cycle requires more real-time computation resources of further processing on the resampled signal. Choosing low number of samples per cycle might make the signaling voltage measurement incompliant with IEC 61000-4-30:2008. The default is 192. For example, in 50 Hz systems, IEC 61000-4-30:2008 requires that the frequency of signaling voltage must be at most 3,000 Hz. If number of samples per cycle is 128, which means the sample rate after resampling is 6,400 Hz, the measured signaling voltage is right at the edge of the passband of resampling filters. The results might not be accurate enough. |
|  | resampled data returns information about the data after resampling. signal returns the resampled signal at even-spaced angle intervals. When number of channels is greater than two, the multichannel resampled signal is interleaved. Note If you scaled the input data from the data type of the analog input (AI) module to <±,24,1> fixed-point, you must scale the output back using the same scale factor. timestamp returns the timestamp, in seconds, of the resampled signal. frequency returns the instantaneous frequency at the timestamp of the resampled signal. |
|  | signal returns the resampled signal at even-spaced angle intervals. When number of channels is greater than two, the multichannel resampled signal is interleaved. Note If you scaled the input data from the data type of the analog input (AI) module to <±,24,1> fixed-point, you must scale the output back using the same scale factor. |
|  | timestamp returns the timestamp, in seconds, of the resampled signal. |
|  | frequency returns the instantaneous frequency at the timestamp of the resampled signal. |
|  | resampling info returns information about the measurement configuration for the resampled data that you can pass to further analysis. nominal frequency returns the nominal line frequency of the input signal. resampling valid? returns TRUE when the resampled signal is valid. resampling valid? returns FALSE when the detected frequency is out of range. samples per cycle returns the number of samples per cycle used for the resampling process. |
|  | nominal frequency returns the nominal line frequency of the input signal. |
|  | resampling valid? returns TRUE when the resampled signal is valid. resampling valid? returns FALSE when the detected frequency is out of range. |
|  | samples per cycle returns the number of samples per cycle used for the resampling process. |
|  | output valid? returns TRUE when this node has computed a result that downstream nodes can use. Use a handshaking protocol to wire this output to the input valid? input of a downstream node to transfer data from the node to the downstream node. |
|  | error returns error information about the resampling process. |

FPGA Resampling Details

**Related Information**
Handshaking ProtocolLine and Bus Configurations


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the FPGA Resampling VI:

- labview\examples\Electrical Power\Protection\Recloser Controller\Recloser Controller.lvproj
- labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (SAR)\Power Quality Applications (cRIO) (SAR).lvproj
- labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)\Power Quality Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma).lvproj

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=fpga-sampling-rate-ratio-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00061: FPGA Sampling Rate Ratio VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `fpga-sampling-rate-ratio-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/fpga-sampling-rate-ratio-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Synchrophasor VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the ratio of the nominal sample rate to the actual sample rate of the data acquisition module as the correction parameters for frequency calculation. Example nominal Fs specifies the nominal sample rate, in hertz, of the

### FPGA Sampling Rate Ratio VI

**Owning Palette:** Synchrophasor VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the ratio of the nominal sample rate to the actual sample rate of the data acquisition module as the correction parameters for frequency calculation.

Example

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/FPGA_Sampling_Rate_Ratio.gif']

|  | nominal Fs specifies the nominal sample rate, in hertz, of the data acquisition module. The default is 50000 hertz. |
| --- | --- |
|  | timestamp specifies the timestamp of each acquired sample from the data acquisition module. |
|  | timestamp valid? specifies whether the timestamp is valid. Connect the GPS locked status to timestamp valid? if the timing source is GPS. The default is TRUE. |
|  | nominal Fs/actual Fs returns the ratio of the nominal sample rate to the actual sample rate of the data acquisition module. This VI updates nominal Fs/actual Fs every second. |

Example

Refer to the Synchrophasor Measurement.lvproj in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Synchrophasor directory for an example of using the FPGA Sampling Rate Ratio VI.

Parent topic:

Synchrophasor VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=fpga-threshold-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00062: FPGA Threshold VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `fpga-threshold-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/fpga-threshold-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Protection VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Detects the input energizing quantity G for deciding whether G turns to a pick-up signal according to pick-up settings. You can use this VI to specify the threshold for any over/under protection. For example, you can use the Over Thre

### FPGA Threshold VI

**Owning Palette:** Protection VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Detects the input energizing quantity **G** for deciding whether **G** turns to a pick-up signal according to **pick-up settings**.

You can use this VI to specify the threshold for any over/under protection. For example, you can use the Over Threshold instance when you build overcurrent and overvoltage protection relays. You can use the Under Threshold instance when you build undercurrent and undervoltage protection relays.

Example

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

FPGA Threshold (Over Threshold)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/FPGA_Threshold__Over.gif']

|  | number of channels specifies the number of channels of the input data. The default is 1. This VI supports up to 16 channels. |
| --- | --- |
|  | G specifies the value of the input energizing quantity. G and pick-up settings must have the same scale. When number of channels is greater than 2, the multichannel G must be interleaved. |
|  | pick-up settings specifies the pick-up setting value and the reset threshold level for defining whether G rises to be a pick-up signal for over protections, such as overcurrent and overvoltage protections. pick-up settings and G must have the same scale. Gs specifies the pick-up setting value. reset level specifies the reset threshold level where pick-up signal? is reset in over protections, such as overcurrent and overvoltage protections. reset level must not exceed Gs. You also can use reset level to prevent unnecessary oscillations. |
|  | Gs specifies the pick-up setting value. |
|  | reset level specifies the reset threshold level where pick-up signal? is reset in over protections, such as overcurrent and overvoltage protections. reset level must not exceed Gs. You also can use reset level to prevent unnecessary oscillations. |
|  | input valid? specifies whether the next data point has arrived for processing. The default is TRUE. Wire the output valid? output of an upstream node to this input to transfer data from the upstream node to this node. |
|  | pick-up signal? returns TRUE when the input energizing quantity G exceeds the pick-up setting value Gs. To prevent unnecessary oscillations, pick-up signal? turns from TRUE to FALSE when G falls below reset level. When number of channels is greater than 2, the multichannel pick-up signal? is interleaved. |
|  | output valid? returns TRUE when this node has computed a result that downstream nodes can use. Wire this output to the input valid? input of a downstream node to transfer data from the node to the downstream node. |
|  | error returns error information about pick-up settings. |

FPGA Threshold (Under Threshold)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/FPGA_Threshold__Under.gif']

|  | number of channels specifies the number of channels of the input data. The default is 1. This VI supports up to 16 channels. |
| --- | --- |
|  | G specifies the value of the input energizing quantity. G and pick-up settings must have the same scale. When number of channels is greater than 2, the multichannel G must be interleaved. |
|  | pick-up settings specifies the pick-up setting value and the reset threshold level for defining whether G falls to be a pick-up signal for under protections, such as undercurrent and undervoltage protections. pick-up settings and G must have the same scale. Gs specifies the pick-up setting value. reset level specifies the reset threshold level where pick-up signal? is reset in under protections, such as undercurrent and undervoltage protections. reset level must not be less than Gs. You also can use reset level to prevent unnecessary oscillations. |
|  | Gs specifies the pick-up setting value. |
|  | reset level specifies the reset threshold level where pick-up signal? is reset in under protections, such as undercurrent and undervoltage protections. reset level must not be less than Gs. You also can use reset level to prevent unnecessary oscillations. |
|  | input valid? specifies whether the next data point has arrived for processing. The default is TRUE. Wire the output valid? output of an upstream node to this input to transfer data from the upstream node to this node. |
|  | pick-up signal? returns TRUE when the input energizing quantity G falls below the pick-up setting value Gs. To prevent unnecessary oscillations, pick-up signal? turns from TRUE to FALSE when G exceeds reset level. When number of channels is greater than 2, the multichannel pick-up signal? is interleaved. |
|  | output valid? returns TRUE when this node has computed a result that downstream nodes can use. Wire this output to the input valid? input of a downstream node to transfer data from the node to the downstream node. |
|  | error returns error information about pick-up settings. |

Example

Refer to the Recloser Controller.lvproj in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Protection\Recloser Controller directory for an example of using the FPGA Threshold VI.

Parent topic:

Protection VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=fpga-vis.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00063: FPGA VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `fpga-vis.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/fpga-vis.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Electrical Power VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use the FPGA VIs to measure electrical power data on FPGA targets. Palette ObjectDescriptionFP

### FPGA VIs

**Owning Palette:** Electrical Power VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the FPGA VIs to measure electrical power data on FPGA targets.

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| FPGA Resampling | Resamples even-time-spaced voltage and current signals into even-angle-spaced signals. The input signals must have a sample rate of 50 kHz. The output signals are synchronous with the line frequency with a constant number of samples per cycle. Use the Resampling for Power Quality VI if you want to perform resampling on the host computer. You must manually select the polymorphic instance to use. This VI supports measuring single-bus single-line and single-bus multiple-line systems. |

| Subpalette | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Protection VIs | Use the Protection VIs to implement protection devices on FPGA targets. Protection devices measure input energizing quantities, set thresholds of pick-up signals, and delay for certain time intervals before trip signals assert. Protection devices also control the automatic reclosing and lockout of circuit breakers. |
| Synchrophasor VIs | Use the Synchrophasor VIs to perform synchrophasor measurement on FPGA targets. The Synchrophasor VIs are in compliance with IEEE Std C37.118.1-2011. |

Parent topic:

Electrical Power VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=frequency-domain-calibration-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00064: Frequency Domain Calibration VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `frequency-domain-calibration-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/frequency-domain-calibration-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Scaling & Calibration VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calibrates a phase spectrum by applying gains and phase delays at all frequency bins, based on actual ranges. You can use the Get Range Utilization VI to calculate range utilization. You must specify frequencies to calibrat

### Frequency Domain Calibration VI

**Owning Palette:** Scaling & Calibration VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calibrates a phase spectrum by applying gains and phase delays at all frequency bins, based on **actual ranges**. You can use the Get Range Utilization VI to calculate range utilization. You must specify **frequencies to calibrate** and **ranges to calibrate** in ascending order.

This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems.

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Frequency_Domain_Calibration.gif']

|  | frequencies to calibrate specifies piecewise frequencies that you want to calibrate phase spectra. You must specify calibration points in ascending order, for example, 25, 50, 75, and 100. The maximum value for 50 Hz nominal frequency is 2,500 Hz and 3,000 Hz for 60 Hz nominal frequency. |
| --- | --- |
|  | ranges to calibrate specifies piecewise calibration points in range percentage. You must specify calibration points in ascending order, for example, 25, 50, 75, and 100. |
|  | spectra in specifies 10/12-cycle complex spectra up to the specified harmonic. The real part of the first vector represents the DC component. Each element of spectra in corresponds to one channel. Voltage spectra are in volts and current spectra are in amperes. f0 specifies the start frequency of the spectrum. df specifies the frequency resolution of the spectrum. magnitudes specifies the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. phases specifies the phase of the spectrum. channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. For line voltages, Voltage Phase A (L1), Voltage Phase B (L2), and Voltage Phase C (L3) equal Phase AB, Phase BC, and Phase CA respectively. |
|  | f0 specifies the start frequency of the spectrum. |
|  | df specifies the frequency resolution of the spectrum. |
|  | magnitudes specifies the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. |
|  | phases specifies the phase of the spectrum. |
|  | channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. For line voltages, Voltage Phase A (L1), Voltage Phase B (L2), and Voltage Phase C (L3) equal Phase AB, Phase BC, and Phase CA respectively. |
|  | gains specifies gains characteristics of different channels for frequencies and ranges that you define. The first dimension represents channels, the second dimension matches frequencies to correct, and the third dimension matches range percentages. |
|  | phase delays specifies phase delays characteristics of different channels for frequencies and ranges that you define. The first dimension represents channels, the second dimension matches frequencies to correct, and the third dimension matches range percentages. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the electrical power system. Use the Initialize System VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | actual ranges specifies actual range utilization in percentages, which is the waveform amplitude relative to RMS full range, in percentages. Each element of actual ranges corresponds to one element of spectra in. This VI interpolates the gains and phase delays of a specific actual range using the closest two ranges to calibrate. |
|  | spectra out returns calibrated complex spectra. f0 returns the start frequency of the spectrum. df returns the frequency resolution of the spectrum. magnitudes returns the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. phases returns the phase value of the spectrum. channel returns the channel of the spectrum. |
|  | f0 returns the start frequency of the spectrum. |
|  | df returns the frequency resolution of the spectrum. |
|  | magnitudes returns the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. |
|  | phases returns the phase value of the spectrum. |
|  | channel returns the channel of the spectrum. |
|  | system info out returns information about the basic configuration of the electrical power system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Frequency Domain Calibration Details

In the single-bus single-line measurement, when you specify the **wiring** input of the Initialize System VI as **3xUph + 2xI** or **3xUline + 2xI** and specify the **spectra in** input of this VI as a current spectrum, the Frequency Domain Calibration VI calculates the spectrum of Current Phase B (L2) using the calibrated spectrum of Current Phase A (L1) and Current Phase C (L3).

**Related Information**Delta Connections
Line and Bus Configurations


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Frequency Domain Calibration VI:

- Calibration for Power Quality Measurement (Host) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Calibration
- Calibration for Power Quality Measurement (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Calibration (DAQmx)

Parent topic:

Scaling & Calibration VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=frequency-half-cycle-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00065: Frequency (Half Cycle) VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `frequency-half-cycle-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/frequency-half-cycle-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: U, I & Freq VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the half, single, and 10/12-cycle frequency of a voltage or current waveform, by using the zero crossings. You can use the Zero Crossing VI to calculate zero crossing and samples since the last cross. This VI refreshes the

### Frequency (Half Cycle) VI

**Owning Palette:** U, I & Freq VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the half, single, and 10/12-cycle frequency of a voltage or current waveform, by using the zero crossings. You can use the Zero Crossing VI to calculate **zero crossing** and **samples since the last cross**. This VI refreshes the half and single-cycle frequency every half-cycle. If the value is flagged, this VI returns the last non-flagged frequency. You can use **half-cycle frequency** with the 10 Seconds instance of the EN50160 Aggregation VI to calculate frequencies according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008.

This VI supports measuring one channel on one line at one time.

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Frequency__Half_Cycle.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | waveform specifies voltage or current one-channel waveform that represents 10-cycle time interval for a 50 Hz power system or 12-cycle time interval for a 60 Hz power system. Voltage waveform is in volts and current waveform is in amperes. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | zero crossing specifies the zero crossing index. |
|  | samples since the last cross specifies the number of samples since the last zero crossing. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the electrical power system. Use the Initialize System VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | voltage flag? specifies whether a interval is flagged. An interval is flagged during a dip, swell, or interruption, if any half-cycle voltage RMS is out of dip or swell thresholds during this interval. If the interval is flagged, the frequency is determined by the last non-flagged frequency. The default is FALSE. |
|  | one-cycle frequency returns frequency value measured over one cycle, starting at fundamental zero crossing and refreshing each half-cycle. timestamps returns timestamps of the end of each signal half-cycle. frequencies returns the frequency value measured over one cycle. |
|  | timestamps returns timestamps of the end of each signal half-cycle. |
|  | frequencies returns the frequency value measured over one cycle. |
|  | half-cycle frequency returns frequency value measured over half cycle, starting at fundamental zero crossing and refreshing each half-cycle. timestamps returns timestamps of the end of each signal half-cycle. frequencies returns the frequency value measured over half cycle. |
|  | timestamps returns timestamps of the end of each signal half-cycle. |
|  | frequencies returns the frequency value measured over half cycle. |
|  | averaged frequency returns frequency calculated as the ratio of the number of integral cycles counted during the basic calculation interval, which is 10 or 12 cycle, divided by the cumulative duration of the integer cycles. If the interval is flagged, the frequency is determined by the last non-flagged frequency. |
|  | system info out returns information about the basic configuration of the electrical power system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Frequency (Half Cycle) Details

**Related Information**
Line and Bus Configurations


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Frequency (Half Cycle) VI:

- Magnitude & Frequency (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (DAQmx)
- Magnitude & Frequency VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)
- Magnitude & Frequency VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (SAR)

Parent topic:

U, I & Freq VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=frequency-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00066: Frequency VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `frequency-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/frequency-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Power Measurement VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the fundamental frequency of a voltage or current signal. Wire data to the waveform input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. Example Use the pull-down menu to select an inst

### Frequency VI

**Owning Palette:** Power Measurement VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the fundamental frequency of a voltage or current signal. Wire data to the **waveform** input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

Example

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Frequency (1 Channel)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Frequency__1_Channel.gif']

|  | waveform specifies the voltage or current signal. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | frequency returns the fundamental frequency, in hertz, of the voltage or current signal. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Frequency (N Channels)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Frequency__N_Channels.gif']

|  | waveforms specifies the voltage or current signal for each channel. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | frequencies returns the fundamental frequencies, in hertz, of the voltage or current signals. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Example

Refer to the Basic Power Measurement (DAQmx) VI in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Measurement directory for an example of using the Frequency VI.

Parent topic:

Power Measurement VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=fundamental-phase-calibration-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00067: Fundamental Phase Calibration VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `fundamental-phase-calibration-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/fundamental-phase-calibration-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Scaling & Calibration VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calibrates the phase of a fundamental vector based on actual ranges. You can use the Get Range Utilization VI to calculate actual ranges. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. Details Examples ra

### Fundamental Phase Calibration VI

**Owning Palette:** Scaling & Calibration VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calibrates the phase of a fundamental vector based on **actual ranges**. You can use the Get Range Utilization VI to calculate **actual ranges**.

This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems.

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Fundamental_Phase_Calibration.gif']

|  | ranges to calibrate specifies piecewise calibration points in range percentage. You must specify calibration points in ascending order, for example, 25, 50, 75, and 100. |
| --- | --- |
|  | one-cycle fundamental vectors in specifies one-cycle fundamental vectors. Each array element represents one measurement channel and contains one period fundamental vectors from 10-cycle time interval for a 50 Hz power system or 12-cycle time interval for a 60 Hz power system. Voltage vectors are in volts and current vectors are in amperes. timestamps specifies the timestamp of the end of each signal cycle. vectors specifies the vector value for each signal cycle. channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. For line voltages, Voltage Phase A (L1), Voltage Phase B (L2), and Voltage Phase C (L3) equal Phase AB, Phase BC, and Phase CA respectively. |
|  | timestamps specifies the timestamp of the end of each signal cycle. |
|  | vectors specifies the vector value for each signal cycle. |
|  | channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. For line voltages, Voltage Phase A (L1), Voltage Phase B (L2), and Voltage Phase C (L3) equal Phase AB, Phase BC, and Phase CA respectively. |
|  | phase delays specifies phase corrections in degrees for fundamental frequencies and ranges that you specify. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the electrical power system. Use the Initialize System VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | actual ranges specifies actual range utilization in percentages, which is the waveform amplitude relative to RMS full range. Each element of actual ranges corresponds to one element of one-cycle fundamental vectors in. This VI interpolates the phase delays of a specific actual ranges using the closest two ranges to calibrate. |
|  | one-cycle fundamental vectors out returns one-cycle fundamental vectors after calibration. Each array element represents one measurement channel and contains one period fundamental vectors from 10-cycle time interval for a 50 Hz power system or 12-cycle time interval for a 60 Hz power system. Voltage vectors are in volts and current vectors are in amperes. timestamps returns the timestamp of the end of each signal cycle. vectors returns the vector value for each signal cycle. channel returns the channel of the output vector. |
|  | timestamps returns the timestamp of the end of each signal cycle. |
|  | vectors returns the vector value for each signal cycle. |
|  | channel returns the channel of the output vector. |
|  | system info out returns information about the basic configuration of the electrical power system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Fundamental Phase Calibration Details

In the single-bus single-line measurement, when you specify the **wiring** input of the Initialize System VI as **3xUph + 2xI** or **3xUline + 2xI** and specify the **one-cycle fundamental vectors in** input of this VI as a current fundamental vector, the Fundamental Phase Calibration VI calculates the vector of Current Phase B (L2) using the calibrated vectors of Current Phase A (L1) and Current Phase C (L3).

**Related Information**Delta Connections
Line and Bus Configurations


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Fundamental Phase Calibration VI:

- Calibration for Power Quality Measurement (Host) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Calibration
- Calibration for Power Quality Measurement (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Calibration (DAQmx)

Parent topic:

Scaling & Calibration VIs

- source_description: Owning Palette: Power & Energy VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the fundamental power values from the voltage and current spectra for a single-cycle block. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. You must use the D to Y Conversion VI to convert line voltage

### Fundamental Power Values (1 Cycle) VI

**Owning Palette:** Power & Energy VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the fundamental power values from the voltage and current spectra for a single-cycle block.

Note

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Fundamental_Power_Values__1_cycle.gif']

|  | one-cycle fundamental phase voltage vectors specifies one-cycle fundamental vectors of voltage channels. Each array element represents one measurement channel and contains one-cycle fundamental vectors from 10-cycle time interval for a 50 Hz power system or 12-cycle time interval for a 60 Hz power system. Each array element of one-cycle fundamental phase voltage vectors and the corresponding array element of one-cycle fundamental current vectors must be in the same phase of the electrical power system. timestamps specifies timestamp of the end of each signal cycle. vectors specifies the one-cycle fundamental vector value. channel specifies the channel of the fundamental vector. |
| --- | --- |
|  | timestamps specifies timestamp of the end of each signal cycle. |
|  | vectors specifies the one-cycle fundamental vector value. |
|  | channel specifies the channel of the fundamental vector. |
|  | one-cycle fundamental current vectors specifies one-cycle fundamental vectors of current channels. Each array element represents one measurement channel and contains one-cycle fundamental vectors from 10-cycle time interval for a 50 Hz power system or 12-cycle time interval for a 60 Hz power system. Each array element of one-cycle fundamental current vectors and the corresponding array element of one-cycle fundamental voltage vectors must be in the same phase of the electrical power system. timestamps specifies timestamp of the end of each signal cycle. vectors specifies the one-cycle fundamental vector value. channel specifies the channel of the fundamental vector. |
|  | timestamps specifies timestamp of the end of each signal cycle. |
|  | vectors specifies the one-cycle fundamental vector value. |
|  | channel specifies the channel of the fundamental vector. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the electrical power system. Use the Initialize System VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | one-cycle fundamental power values returns one-cycle fundamental power values. Each array element represents one measurement channel and contains one-cycle power values from 10-cycle time interval for a 50 Hz power system or 12-cycle time interval for a 60 Hz power system. timestamps returns the timestamp of the end of each signal cycle. apparent power returns fundamental apparent power vectors. active power returns active apparent power vectors. reactive power returns reactive apparent power vectors. |
|  | timestamps returns the timestamp of the end of each signal cycle. |
|  | apparent power returns fundamental apparent power vectors. |
|  | active power returns active apparent power vectors. |
|  | reactive power returns reactive apparent power vectors. |
|  | system info out returns information about the basic configuration of the electrical power system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Fundamental Power Values (1 Cycle) Details

**Related Information**
Line and Bus Configurations


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Fundamental Power Values (1 Cycle) VI:

- Power & Energy (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (DAQmx)
- Power & Energy VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)
- Power & Energy VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (SAR)

## TOPIC 00069: Fundamental Vector VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `fundamental-vector-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/fundamental-vector-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Power Measurement VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the fundamental vector of a voltage or current signal. Wire data to the voltage waveform input or the current waveform input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. Use the Compl

### Fundamental Vector VI

**Owning Palette:** Power Measurement VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the fundamental vector of a voltage or current signal. Wire data to the **voltage waveform** input or the **current waveform** input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

Use the Complex To Polar function to convert the results of this VI to magnitude and phase components. Use the Complex To Re/Im function to convert the results of this VI to real and imaginary components.

Examples

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Note

voltage waveform

current waveform

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Fundamental_Vector__1_Channel.gif']

|  | voltage waveform specifies the voltage signal in volts. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | current waveform specifies the current signal in amperes. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | frequency returns the frequency of the output fundamental components in hertz. |
|  | voltage fundamental vector returns the fundamental vector of the voltage fundamental component. |
|  | current fundamental vector returns the fundamental vector of the current fundamental component. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Note

voltage waveforms

current waveforms

current waveforms

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Fundamental_Vector__N_Channels.gif']

|  | voltage waveforms specifies the voltage signal, in volts, for each channel. The number and sequence of voltage channels must correspond to the number and sequence of current channels. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | current waveforms specifies the current signal, in amperes, for each channel. The number and sequence of current channels must correspond to the number and sequence of voltage channels. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | frequency returns the frequency of the output fundamental components in hertz. |
|  | voltage fundamental vectors returns the fundamental vector of the voltage fundamental component for each channel. The phase of the first element of voltage fundamental vectors is always zero. The phases of other elements are relative to the phase of the first element. |
|  | current fundamental vectors returns the fundamental vector of the current fundamental component for each channel. If voltage waveforms is empty, the phase of the first element of current fundamental vectors is always zero. The phases of other elements are relative to the phase of the first element. If voltage waveforms is not empty, the phases of current fundamental vectors are relative to the phase of the first element of voltage fundamental vectors. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Fundamental Vector VI:

- Basic Power Measurement (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Measurement
- Advanced Power Measurement (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Measurement

Parent topic:

Power Measurement VIs

### Fundamental Vector VI

Calculates the fundamental vector for each cycle contained in the input waveforms.

This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Fundamental_Vector.gif']

Each array element represents one phase and contains samples representing 10-cycle time interval for a 50 Hz power system or 12-cycle time interval for a 60 Hz power system. Voltage waveforms are in volts and current waveforms are in amperes. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |

Each array element represents one measurement channel and contains one cycle fundamental vectors from 10-cycle time interval for a 50 Hz power system or 12-cycle time interval for a 60 Hz power system. Voltage vectors are in volts and current vectors are in amperes. timestamps returns timestamps of the end of each signal cycle. vectors returns values of fundamental vectors. channel returns the channel information of the fundamental vector. |

## TOPIC 00071: Get ABCN Waveforms by Index VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `get-abcn-waveforms-by-index-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/get-abcn-waveforms-by-index-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Indexes the input array of waveforms and builds the cluster typedefinition used by measurement-analysis VIs for the electric vehicle power test. Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI. Examples Get Current Waveforms by Index (3-Wire) Indexes the input array of waveforms and builds t

### Get ABCN Waveforms by Index VI

Indexes the input array of **waveforms** and builds the cluster typedefinition used by measurement-analysis VIs for the electric vehicle power test.

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Examples


Get Current Waveforms by Index (3-Wire)

Indexes the input array of **waveforms** and builds the cluster typedefinition used by measurement-analysis VIs for the electric vehicle power test.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Get_Current_Waveforms_by_Index_(3-Wire).gif']

|  | waveforms specifies the input array of waveforms where each channel index corresponds to a specific measurement. t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt specifies the sample interval. Y specifies the measured values. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt specifies the sample interval. |
|  | Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | line current indices specifies the channel indices for each current measurement in a 3-phase, 3-wire system. Ia is the current measured through line a. Ib is the current measured through line b. Ic is the current measured through line c. |
|  | Ia is the current measured through line a. |
|  | Ib is the current measured through line b. |
|  | Ic is the current measured through line c. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | waveforms (dup) returns the unchanged waveforms to be used for further parsing and processing. t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt returns the sample interval. Y returns the measured values. |
|  | t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt returns the sample interval. |
|  | Y returns the measured values. |
|  | current waveforms returns the current signals, in amperes, measured through lines a, b, and c in a 3-phase, 3-wire system. Ia returns the current signal measured through line a, in amperes. Ib returns the current signal, measured through line b, in amperes. Ic returns the current signal measured through line c, in amperes. |
|  | Ia returns the current signal measured through line a, in amperes. |
|  | Ib returns the current signal, measured through line b, in amperes. |
|  | Ic returns the current signal measured through line c, in amperes. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Get Current Waveforms by Index (4-Wire)

Indexes the input array of **waveforms** and builds the cluster typedefinition used by measurement-analysis VIs for the electric vehicle power test.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Get_Current_Waveforms_by_Index_(4-Wire).gif']

|  | waveforms specifies the input array of waveforms where each channel index corresponds to a specific measurement. t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt specifies the sample interval. Y specifies the measured values. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt specifies the sample interval. |
|  | Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | line current indices specifies the channel indices for each current measurement in a 3-phase, 4-wire system. Ia is the current measured through line a. Ib is the current measured through line b. Ic is the current measured through line c. In is the current measured through the neutral line. |
|  | Ia is the current measured through line a. |
|  | Ib is the current measured through line b. |
|  | Ic is the current measured through line c. |
|  | In is the current measured through the neutral line. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | waveforms (dup) returns the unchanged waveforms to be used for further parsing and processing. t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt returns the sample interval. Y returns the measured values. |
|  | t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt returns the sample interval. |
|  | Y returns the measured values. |
|  | current waveforms returns the current signals, in amperes, measured through lines a, b, c, and n in a 3-phase, 4-wire system. Ia returns the current signal measured through line a, in amperes. Ib returns the current signal, measured through line b, in amperes. Ic returns the current signal measured through line c, in amperes. In returns the current signal measured through the neutral line, in amperes. |
|  | Ia returns the current signal measured through line a, in amperes. |
|  | Ib returns the current signal, measured through line b, in amperes. |
|  | Ic returns the current signal measured through line c, in amperes. |
|  | In returns the current signal measured through the neutral line, in amperes. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Get Line Voltage Waveforms by Index

Indexes the input array of **waveforms** and builds the cluster typedefinition used by measurement-analysis VIs for the electric vehicle power test.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Get_Line_Voltage_Waveforms_by_Index.gif']

|  | waveforms specifies the input array of waveforms where each channel index corresponds to a specific measurement. t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt specifies the sample interval. Y specifies the measured values. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt specifies the sample interval. |
|  | Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | line voltage indices specifies the channel indices for each line to line voltage measurement in a 3-phase, 3-wire system. Vab is the channel index for Vab. Vbc is the channel index for Vbc. Vca is the channel index for Vca. |
|  | Vab is the channel index for Vab. |
|  | Vbc is the channel index for Vbc. |
|  | Vca is the channel index for Vca. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | waveforms (dup) returns the unchanged waveforms to be used for further parsing and processing. t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt returns the sample interval. Y returns the measured values. |
|  | t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt returns the sample interval. |
|  | Y returns the measured values. |
|  | line voltage waveforms returns the voltage signals, in volts, measured between two lines in a 3-phase, 3-wire system. Vab returns the voltage signal, in volts, between line a and line b. Vbc returns the voltage signal, in volts, between line b and line c. Vca returns the voltage signal, in volts, between line c and line a. |
|  | Vab returns the voltage signal, in volts, between line a and line b. |
|  | Vbc returns the voltage signal, in volts, between line b and line c. |
|  | Vca returns the voltage signal, in volts, between line c and line a. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Get Phase Voltage Waveforms by Index

Indexes the input array of **waveforms** and builds the cluster typedefinition used by measurement-analysis VIs for the electric vehicle power test.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Get_Phase_Voltage_Waveforms_by_Index.gif']

|  | waveforms specifies the input array of waveforms where each channel index corresponds to a specific measurement. t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt specifies the sample interval. Y specifies the measured values. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt specifies the sample interval. |
|  | Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | phase voltage indices specifies the channel indices for each phase voltage measurement in a 3-phase, 4-wire system. Van is the channel index for Van. Vbn is the channel index for Vbn. Vcn is the channel index for Vcn. |
|  | Van is the channel index for Van. |
|  | Vbn is the channel index for Vbn. |
|  | Vcn is the channel index for Vcn. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | waveforms (dup) returns the unchanged waveforms to be used for further parsing and processing. t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt returns the sample interval. Y returns the measured values. |

|  | dt returns the sample interval. |
|  | Y returns the measured values. |

Van returns the voltage signal, in volts, between phase a and neutral. Vbn returns the voltage signal, in volts, between phase b and neutral. Vcn returns the voltage signal, in volts, between phase c and neutral. |
