|  | digital channels specifies the configuration of the reporting digital channels. digital names specifies the names of the digital channels in the status words. digital names uses no more than 16 bytes in ASCII format for each digital channel as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. digital unit specifies the mask words for digital status words. digital unit corresponds to the DIGUNIT field of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. digital unit uses two 16-bit words for each digital word. The first 16-bit word indicates the normal status of the digital inputs by returning the logical exclusive or (XOR) result between the user-defined normal status and the status word. The second 16-bit word indicates the current valid digital inputs to the PMU, by setting the binary position of the digital input to 1 and setting the binary position of all the other bits to 0. You can customize the use of masks if you do not use digital status words as a Boolean. |  |
|  | digital names specifies the names of the digital channels in the status words. digital names uses no more than 16 bytes in ASCII format for each digital channel as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. |  |
|  | digital unit specifies the mask words for digital status words. digital unit corresponds to the DIGUNIT field of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. digital unit uses two 16-bit words for each digital word. The first 16-bit word indicates the normal status of the digital inputs by returning the logical exclusive or (XOR) result between the user-defined normal status and the status word. The second 16-bit word indicates the current valid digital inputs to the PMU, by setting the binary position of the digital input to 1 and setting the binary position of all the other bits to 0. You can customize the use of masks if you do not use digital status words as a Boolean. |  |
|  | CFG-3 specifies the configuration frame 3 that indicates the measurements that the data frame is transmitting or reporting as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The difference between CFG-3 and CFG-2 is that CFG-3 supports variable name length fields and adds more PMU and signal information. PMU ID specifies the ID of the PMU. PMU ID defines the two IDCODE fields of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. You can assign values within the range 1 to 65,534. Values 0 and 65,535 are reserved values. global PMU ID specifies the ID of the PMUs in a PMU system that has more than 65,535 PMUs. global PMU ID corresponds to the G_PMU_ID field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. You can set global PMU ID up to eight bytes. time base specifies the resolution of the FRACSEC field in all message frames. time base corresponds to the 4-byte TIME_BASE field of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. Bits 31–24 of TIME_BASE are reserved for flags. Bits 23–0 of TIME_BASE indicate the subdivision of the second that the FRACSEC field is based on. The FRACSEC field is the multiplication of the actual fractional second of the message frame by the TIME_BASE field. nominal frequency specifies the nominal line frequency of the input signal. nominal frequency corresponds to Bit 0 of the FNOM field of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. 050 HzCorresponds to the value 1 for Bit 0.160 Hz (default)Corresponds to the value 0 for Bit 0. reporting rate specifies the reporting frequency of the PMU in frames per second (fps). The default is 60. Valid reporting frequencies are 10 fps, 25 fps, and 50 fps for 50 Hz nominal line frequency and 10 fps, 12 fps, 15 fps, 20 fps, 30 fps, and 60 fps for 60 Hz nominal line frequency as defined in both IEEE Std C37.118.1-2011 and IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. station name specifies the station name by using no more than 255 bytes in ASCII format. station name corresponds to the STN field in the configuration frame as defined in IEEE C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. data format specifies the data format in the data frames. data format corresponds to the FORMAT field of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. Use the following bits to define the data format in the data frames. Bits 15-4Unused. Bit 3Specifies the format of the FREQ and DFREQ fields in the data frames. 0 indicates 16-bit integer and 1 indicates 32-bit floating-point. Bit 2Specifies the format of the ANALOG field in the data frames. 0 indicates 16-bit integer and 1 indicates 32-bit floating-point. Bit 1Specifies the format of the PHASORS field in the data frames. 0 indicates 16-bit integer and 1 indicates 32-bit floating-point. Bit 0Specifies the phasor format in the PHASORS field. 0 indicates the rectangular format and 1 indicates the polar format. phasor channels specifies the configuration of the reporting phasor channels. phasor name specifies the name of the phasor channel. phasor name uses no more than 255 bytes in ASCII format for each phasor as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. data modification flag specifies a 16-bit flag that indicates the type of data modification when a continuous process modifies the data. data modification flag corresponds to the most significant two bytes of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. Use data modification flag in conjunction with the data modification bit (Bit 9) of the STAT field in the data frame. The following list indicates the meaning of each bit when each bit is set. Bit 0Not used Bit 1Upsampled with interpolation Bit 2Upsampled with extrapolation Bit 3Downsampled by reselection (selecting every Nth sample) Bit 4Downsampled with FIR filter Bit 5Downsampled with non-FIR filter Bit 6Filtered without changing sampling Bit 7Phasor magnitude adjusted for calibration Bit 8Phasor phase adjusted for calibration Bit 9Phasor phase adjusted for rotation (±30°, ±120°, and so on) Bit 10Pseudo-phasor value (combined from other phasors) Bit 11–14Reserved for future assignment Bit 15Modification applied, type not here defined phasor unit specifies Bit 3 of the third byte of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. 0Voltage [V] (default)Corresponds to the value 0.1Current [A]Corresponds to the value 1. phasor phase specifies the measurement phase of the phasor channel. phasor phase corresponds to Bits 2-0 of the third byte of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. When phasor unit is Voltage [V], the value of phasor phase and the value channel phase of the corresponding physical channels determine the connection type of phasor channels. The following table lists the connection type based on different combinations of phasor phase and channel phase. Phasor Phase Channel Phase Connection Type Phase A (L1) Voltage Phase A (L1) Phase-to-Neutral (Phase AN) Phase A (L1) Voltage Phase AB (L1L2) Phase-to-Phase (Phase AB) Phase B (L2) Voltage Phase B (L2) Phase-to-Neutral (Phase BN) Phase B (L2) Voltage Phase BC (L2L3) Phase-to-Phase (Phase BC) Phase C (L3) Voltage Phase C (L3) Phase-to-Neutral (Phase CN) Phase C (L3) Voltage Phase CA (L3L1) Phase-to-Phase (Phase CA) The following table lists values of phasor phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)Corresponds to the value 100 for Bits 2-0.1Phase B (L2)Corresponds to the value 101 for Bits 2-0.2Phase C (L3)Corresponds to the value 110 for Bits 2-0.3Phase N (Neutral)Corresponds to the value 111 for Bits 2-0.4Positive sequenceCorresponds to the value 001 for Bits 2-0.5Negative sequenceCorresponds to the value 010 for Bits 2-0.6Zero sequenceCorresponds to the value 000 for Bits 2-0. 3-phase system ID specifies the ID of the three-phase system that the physical channel belongs to. user designation specifies for user designation. user designation corresponds to the fourth byte of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 0. scale factor specifies the conversion factor for phasor channels. scale factor corresponds to the second 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 1. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore scale factor by using the default value. angle offset specifies the phasor angle adjustment for phasor channels in radians. angle offset corresponds to the third 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 0. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore angle offset by using the default value. analog channels specifies the configuration of the reporting analog channels. analog name specifies the name of the analog channel. analog name uses no more than 255 bytes in ASCII format for each analog channel as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. scale factor specifies the magnitude scaling for analog channels. scale factor corresponds to the first 4-byte word of the ANSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 1. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore scale factor by using the default value. magnitude offset specifies the magnitude offset for analog channels. magnitude offset corresponds to the second 4-byte word of the ANSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 0. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore magnitude offset by using the default value. digital channels specifies the configuration of the reporting digital channels. digital names specifies the names of the digital channels in the status words. digital names uses no more than 255 bytes in ASCII format for each digital channel as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. digital unit specifies the mask words for digital status words. digital unit corresponds to the DIGUNIT field of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. digital unit uses two 16-bit words for each digital word. The first 16-bit word indicates the normal status of the digital inputs by returning the logical exclusive or (XOR) result between the user-defined normal status and the status word. The second 16-bit word indicates the current valid digital inputs to the PMU, by setting the binary position of the digital input to 1 and setting the binary position of all the other bits to 0. You can customize the use of masks if you do not use digital status words as a Boolean. |  |
|  | PMU ID specifies the ID of the PMU. PMU ID defines the two IDCODE fields of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. You can assign values within the range 1 to 65,534. Values 0 and 65,535 are reserved values. |  |
|  | global PMU ID specifies the ID of the PMUs in a PMU system that has more than 65,535 PMUs. global PMU ID corresponds to the G_PMU_ID field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. You can set global PMU ID up to eight bytes. |  |
|  | time base specifies the resolution of the FRACSEC field in all message frames. time base corresponds to the 4-byte TIME_BASE field of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. Bits 31–24 of TIME_BASE are reserved for flags. Bits 23–0 of TIME_BASE indicate the subdivision of the second that the FRACSEC field is based on. The FRACSEC field is the multiplication of the actual fractional second of the message frame by the TIME_BASE field. |  |
|  | nominal frequency specifies the nominal line frequency of the input signal. nominal frequency corresponds to Bit 0 of the FNOM field of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. 050 HzCorresponds to the value 1 for Bit 0.160 Hz (default)Corresponds to the value 0 for Bit 0. |  |
| 0 | 50 HzCorresponds to the value 1 for Bit 0. |  |
| 1 | 60 Hz (default)Corresponds to the value 0 for Bit 0. |  |
|  | reporting rate specifies the reporting frequency of the PMU in frames per second (fps). The default is 60. Valid reporting frequencies are 10 fps, 25 fps, and 50 fps for 50 Hz nominal line frequency and 10 fps, 12 fps, 15 fps, 20 fps, 30 fps, and 60 fps for 60 Hz nominal line frequency as defined in both IEEE Std C37.118.1-2011 and IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |  |
|  | station name specifies the station name by using no more than 255 bytes in ASCII format. station name corresponds to the STN field in the configuration frame as defined in IEEE C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. |  |
|  | data format specifies the data format in the data frames. data format corresponds to the FORMAT field of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. Use the following bits to define the data format in the data frames. Bits 15-4Unused. Bit 3Specifies the format of the FREQ and DFREQ fields in the data frames. 0 indicates 16-bit integer and 1 indicates 32-bit floating-point. Bit 2Specifies the format of the ANALOG field in the data frames. 0 indicates 16-bit integer and 1 indicates 32-bit floating-point. Bit 1Specifies the format of the PHASORS field in the data frames. 0 indicates 16-bit integer and 1 indicates 32-bit floating-point. Bit 0Specifies the phasor format in the PHASORS field. 0 indicates the rectangular format and 1 indicates the polar format. |  |
|  | phasor channels specifies the configuration of the reporting phasor channels. phasor name specifies the name of the phasor channel. phasor name uses no more than 255 bytes in ASCII format for each phasor as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. data modification flag specifies a 16-bit flag that indicates the type of data modification when a continuous process modifies the data. data modification flag corresponds to the most significant two bytes of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. Use data modification flag in conjunction with the data modification bit (Bit 9) of the STAT field in the data frame. The following list indicates the meaning of each bit when each bit is set. Bit 0Not used Bit 1Upsampled with interpolation Bit 2Upsampled with extrapolation Bit 3Downsampled by reselection (selecting every Nth sample) Bit 4Downsampled with FIR filter Bit 5Downsampled with non-FIR filter Bit 6Filtered without changing sampling Bit 7Phasor magnitude adjusted for calibration Bit 8Phasor phase adjusted for calibration Bit 9Phasor phase adjusted for rotation (±30°, ±120°, and so on) Bit 10Pseudo-phasor value (combined from other phasors) Bit 11–14Reserved for future assignment Bit 15Modification applied, type not here defined phasor unit specifies Bit 3 of the third byte of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. 0Voltage [V] (default)Corresponds to the value 0.1Current [A]Corresponds to the value 1. phasor phase specifies the measurement phase of the phasor channel. phasor phase corresponds to Bits 2-0 of the third byte of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. When phasor unit is Voltage [V], the value of phasor phase and the value channel phase of the corresponding physical channels determine the connection type of phasor channels. The following table lists the connection type based on different combinations of phasor phase and channel phase. Phasor Phase Channel Phase Connection Type Phase A (L1) Voltage Phase A (L1) Phase-to-Neutral (Phase AN) Phase A (L1) Voltage Phase AB (L1L2) Phase-to-Phase (Phase AB) Phase B (L2) Voltage Phase B (L2) Phase-to-Neutral (Phase BN) Phase B (L2) Voltage Phase BC (L2L3) Phase-to-Phase (Phase BC) Phase C (L3) Voltage Phase C (L3) Phase-to-Neutral (Phase CN) Phase C (L3) Voltage Phase CA (L3L1) Phase-to-Phase (Phase CA) The following table lists values of phasor phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)Corresponds to the value 100 for Bits 2-0.1Phase B (L2)Corresponds to the value 101 for Bits 2-0.2Phase C (L3)Corresponds to the value 110 for Bits 2-0.3Phase N (Neutral)Corresponds to the value 111 for Bits 2-0.4Positive sequenceCorresponds to the value 001 for Bits 2-0.5Negative sequenceCorresponds to the value 010 for Bits 2-0.6Zero sequenceCorresponds to the value 000 for Bits 2-0. 3-phase system ID specifies the ID of the three-phase system that the physical channel belongs to. user designation specifies for user designation. user designation corresponds to the fourth byte of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 0. scale factor specifies the conversion factor for phasor channels. scale factor corresponds to the second 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 1. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore scale factor by using the default value. angle offset specifies the phasor angle adjustment for phasor channels in radians. angle offset corresponds to the third 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 0. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore angle offset by using the default value. |  |
|  | phasor name specifies the name of the phasor channel. phasor name uses no more than 255 bytes in ASCII format for each phasor as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. |  |
|  | data modification flag specifies a 16-bit flag that indicates the type of data modification when a continuous process modifies the data. data modification flag corresponds to the most significant two bytes of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. Use data modification flag in conjunction with the data modification bit (Bit 9) of the STAT field in the data frame. The following list indicates the meaning of each bit when each bit is set. Bit 0Not used Bit 1Upsampled with interpolation Bit 2Upsampled with extrapolation Bit 3Downsampled by reselection (selecting every Nth sample) Bit 4Downsampled with FIR filter Bit 5Downsampled with non-FIR filter Bit 6Filtered without changing sampling Bit 7Phasor magnitude adjusted for calibration Bit 8Phasor phase adjusted for calibration Bit 9Phasor phase adjusted for rotation (±30°, ±120°, and so on) Bit 10Pseudo-phasor value (combined from other phasors) Bit 11–14Reserved for future assignment Bit 15Modification applied, type not here defined |  |
|  | phasor unit specifies Bit 3 of the third byte of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. 0Voltage [V] (default)Corresponds to the value 0.1Current [A]Corresponds to the value 1. |  |
| 0 | Voltage [V] (default)Corresponds to the value 0. |  |
| 1 | Current [A]Corresponds to the value 1. |  |
|  | phasor phase specifies the measurement phase of the phasor channel. phasor phase corresponds to Bits 2-0 of the third byte of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. When phasor unit is Voltage [V], the value of phasor phase and the value channel phase of the corresponding physical channels determine the connection type of phasor channels. The following table lists the connection type based on different combinations of phasor phase and channel phase. Phasor Phase Channel Phase Connection Type Phase A (L1) Voltage Phase A (L1) Phase-to-Neutral (Phase AN) Phase A (L1) Voltage Phase AB (L1L2) Phase-to-Phase (Phase AB) Phase B (L2) Voltage Phase B (L2) Phase-to-Neutral (Phase BN) Phase B (L2) Voltage Phase BC (L2L3) Phase-to-Phase (Phase BC) Phase C (L3) Voltage Phase C (L3) Phase-to-Neutral (Phase CN) Phase C (L3) Voltage Phase CA (L3L1) Phase-to-Phase (Phase CA) The following table lists values of phasor phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)Corresponds to the value 100 for Bits 2-0.1Phase B (L2)Corresponds to the value 101 for Bits 2-0.2Phase C (L3)Corresponds to the value 110 for Bits 2-0.3Phase N (Neutral)Corresponds to the value 111 for Bits 2-0.4Positive sequenceCorresponds to the value 001 for Bits 2-0.5Negative sequenceCorresponds to the value 010 for Bits 2-0.6Zero sequenceCorresponds to the value 000 for Bits 2-0. |  |
| Phasor Phase | Channel Phase | Connection Type |
| Phase A (L1) | Voltage Phase A (L1) | Phase-to-Neutral (Phase AN) |
| Phase A (L1) | Voltage Phase AB (L1L2) | Phase-to-Phase (Phase AB) |
| Phase B (L2) | Voltage Phase B (L2) | Phase-to-Neutral (Phase BN) |
| Phase B (L2) | Voltage Phase BC (L2L3) | Phase-to-Phase (Phase BC) |
| Phase C (L3) | Voltage Phase C (L3) | Phase-to-Neutral (Phase CN) |
| Phase C (L3) | Voltage Phase CA (L3L1) | Phase-to-Phase (Phase CA) |
| 0 | Phase A (L1) (default)Corresponds to the value 100 for Bits 2-0. |  |
| 1 | Phase B (L2)Corresponds to the value 101 for Bits 2-0. |  |
| 2 | Phase C (L3)Corresponds to the value 110 for Bits 2-0. |  |
| 3 | Phase N (Neutral)Corresponds to the value 111 for Bits 2-0. |  |
| 4 | Positive sequenceCorresponds to the value 001 for Bits 2-0. |  |
| 5 | Negative sequenceCorresponds to the value 010 for Bits 2-0. |  |
| 6 | Zero sequenceCorresponds to the value 000 for Bits 2-0. |  |
|  | 3-phase system ID specifies the ID of the three-phase system that the physical channel belongs to. |  |
|  | user designation specifies for user designation. user designation corresponds to the fourth byte of the first 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 0. |  |
|  | scale factor specifies the conversion factor for phasor channels. scale factor corresponds to the second 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 1. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore scale factor by using the default value. |  |
|  | angle offset specifies the phasor angle adjustment for phasor channels in radians. angle offset corresponds to the third 4-byte word of the PHSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 0. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore angle offset by using the default value. |  |
|  | analog channels specifies the configuration of the reporting analog channels. analog name specifies the name of the analog channel. analog name uses no more than 255 bytes in ASCII format for each analog channel as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. scale factor specifies the magnitude scaling for analog channels. scale factor corresponds to the first 4-byte word of the ANSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 1. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore scale factor by using the default value. magnitude offset specifies the magnitude offset for analog channels. magnitude offset corresponds to the second 4-byte word of the ANSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 0. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore magnitude offset by using the default value. |  |
|  | analog name specifies the name of the analog channel. analog name uses no more than 255 bytes in ASCII format for each analog channel as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. |  |
|  | scale factor specifies the magnitude scaling for analog channels. scale factor corresponds to the first 4-byte word of the ANSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 1. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore scale factor by using the default value. |  |
|  | magnitude offset specifies the magnitude offset for analog channels. magnitude offset corresponds to the second 4-byte word of the ANSCALE field of the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The default is 0. If the transmitted data is in floating-point format, you can ignore magnitude offset by using the default value. |  |
|  | digital channels specifies the configuration of the reporting digital channels. digital names specifies the names of the digital channels in the status words. digital names uses no more than 255 bytes in ASCII format for each digital channel as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. digital unit specifies the mask words for digital status words. digital unit corresponds to the DIGUNIT field of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. digital unit uses two 16-bit words for each digital word. The first 16-bit word indicates the normal status of the digital inputs by returning the logical exclusive or (XOR) result between the user-defined normal status and the status word. The second 16-bit word indicates the current valid digital inputs to the PMU, by setting the binary position of the digital input to 1 and setting the binary position of all the other bits to 0. You can customize the use of masks if you do not use digital status words as a Boolean. |  |
|  | digital names specifies the names of the digital channels in the status words. digital names uses no more than 255 bytes in ASCII format for each digital channel as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. The LabVIEW Electrical Power Toolkit does not support the UTF-8 format. |  |
|  | digital unit specifies the mask words for digital status words. digital unit corresponds to the DIGUNIT field of the configuration frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. digital unit uses two 16-bit words for each digital word. The first 16-bit word indicates the normal status of the digital inputs by returning the logical exclusive or (XOR) result between the user-defined normal status and the status word. The second 16-bit word indicates the current valid digital inputs to the PMU, by setting the binary position of the digital input to 1 and setting the binary position of all the other bits to 0. You can customize the use of masks if you do not use digital status words as a Boolean. |  |
|  | header specifies the information, such as PMUs, data sources, scaling, algorithms, and filtering, that you can read as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |  |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |  |
|  | configuration for data frame specifies whether the data frame uses CFG-2 or CFG-3 for transmitting or reporting the measurement data. If you select CFG-2, this VI uses the values in CFG-2 to initialize the system so that the data frames transmit or report the measurement data according to CFG-2. If you select CFG-3, this VI uses the values in CFG-3 to initialize the system so that the data frames transmit or report the measurement data according to CFG-3. 0CFG-2 (default)1CFG-3 |  |
| 0 | CFG-2 (default) |  |
| 1 | CFG-3 |  |
|  | initial samples to read returns the initial number of samples to read from DMA FIFOs. |  |
|  | M Class memory information returns the initial values and the memory sizes of two memories for the M class of performance. first memory initial values returns the initial values of the first memory as <±,18,-4> fixed-point numbers. second memory initial values returns the initial values of the second memory as <±,19,-3> fixed-point numbers. first memory size returns the size of the first memory. second memory size returns the size of the second memory. |  |
|  | first memory initial values returns the initial values of the first memory as <±,18,-4> fixed-point numbers. |  |
|  | second memory initial values returns the initial values of the second memory as <±,19,-3> fixed-point numbers. |  |
|  | first memory size returns the size of the first memory. |  |
|  | second memory size returns the size of the second memory. |  |
|  | algorithm factors returns the window length and group delay of the PMU in microseconds as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. window length returns the window length of the PMU in microseconds. window length corresponds to the WINDOW field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. window length includes all filters and estimation windows in effect. group delay returns the group delay of the PMU in microseconds. group delay corresponds to the GRP_DLY field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. group delay includes all filters and estimation windows in effect. |  |
|  | window length returns the window length of the PMU in microseconds. window length corresponds to the WINDOW field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. window length includes all filters and estimation windows in effect. |  |
|  | group delay returns the group delay of the PMU in microseconds. group delay corresponds to the GRP_DLY field in the CFG-3 frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. group delay includes all filters and estimation windows in effect. |  |
|  | system info out returns the basic configuration of the PMU system. |  |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |  |

Initialize System (Std 2011) Details

Example

Refer to the Synchrophasor Measurement.lvproj in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Synchrophasor directory for an example of using the Initialize System (Std 2011) VI.

## TOPIC 00081: Initialize System VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `initialize-system-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/initialize-system-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Power Quality VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Sets the basic configuration of the electrical power system based on different line and bus configurations. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. Details Examples Use the pull-down menu to select an ins

### Initialize System VI

**Owning Palette:** Power Quality VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Sets the basic configuration of the electrical power system based on different line and bus configurations. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use.

Details Examples

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Single Bus Single Line

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Initialize_System.gif']

|  | samples per cycle specifies the number of samples per cycle to resample using even numbers, whose range is from 128 to 512. More samples per cycle require more real-time computation resources of further processing on the resampled signal. Choosing low number of samples per cycle might make the signaling voltage measurement incompliant with IEC 61000-4-30:2008. The default is 192. For example, in 50 Hz systems, IEC 61000-4-30:2008 requires the frequency of signaling voltage must be at most 3,000 Hz. If number of samples per cycle is 128, which means the sample rate after resampling is 6,400 Hz, the measured signaling voltage is right at the edge of the passband of resampling filters thus the results might not be accurate enough. |
| --- | --- |
|  | wiring specifies the wiring method of the equipment terminals. Some wiring methods, such as the split single-phase wiring method and the one phase plus neutral wiring method, involve two voltage channels. If you use wiring methods with two voltage channels, you can select a four or three voltage channels method from the wiring methods that this VI supports and leave unused channels disconnected. 04xUph + 4xIFour phase voltage channels and four current channels.13xUph + 4xI (default)Three phase voltage channels and four current channels.23xUline + 4xIThree line voltage channels and four current channels.33xUph + 3xIThree phase voltage channels and three current channels.43xUline + 3xIThree line voltage channels and three current channels.53xUph + 2xIThree phase voltage channels and two current channels.63xUline + 2xIThree line voltage channels and two current channels71xUph + 1xIOne phase voltage channel and one current channel. |
| 0 | 4xUph + 4xIFour phase voltage channels and four current channels. |
| 1 | 3xUph + 4xI (default)Three phase voltage channels and four current channels. |
| 2 | 3xUline + 4xIThree line voltage channels and four current channels. |
| 3 | 3xUph + 3xIThree phase voltage channels and three current channels. |
| 4 | 3xUline + 3xIThree line voltage channels and three current channels. |
| 5 | 3xUph + 2xIThree phase voltage channels and two current channels. |
| 6 | 3xUline + 2xIThree line voltage channels and two current channels |
| 7 | 1xUph + 1xIOne phase voltage channel and one current channel. |
|  | nominal frequency specifies the nominal frequency of the sampled signal. 050 Hz (default)160 Hz |
| 0 | 50 Hz (default) |
| 1 | 60 Hz |
|  | nominal voltage specifies the nominal voltage reference level, in volts, for detecting voltage dips, swells, and interruptions, measuring deviations, and detecting rapid voltage change events. The default is 230 volts. |
|  | type of reference voltage specifies the type of voltage reference level for detection of voltage dip and swell events according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. 0Constant (default)Specifies to use constant reference voltage.1SlidingSpecifies to use sliding reference voltage. |
| 0 | Constant (default)Specifies to use constant reference voltage. |
| 1 | SlidingSpecifies to use sliding reference voltage. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | nominal sample rate returns the nominal sample rate in hertz. |
|  | system info out returns information about basic configuration of the electrical power system. You use the information of system info out to perform further power quality analysis. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Single Bus Multiple Lines

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Initialize_System_Multiple_Lines.gif']

|  | bus configuration specifies the bus configuration for the electrical power system. nominal voltage specifies the nominal voltage reference level, in volts, for detecting voltage dips, swells, and interruptions, measuring deviations, and detecting rapid voltage change events. The default is 230 volts. nominal frequency specifies the nominal frequency of the sampled signal. 050 Hz (default)160 Hz samples per cycle specifies the number of samples per cycle to resample using even numbers, whose range is from 128 to 512. More samples per cycle require more real-time computation resources of further processing on the resampled signal. Choosing low number of samples per cycle might make the signaling voltage measurement incompliant with IEC 61000-4-30:2008. The default is 192. For example, in 50 Hz systems, IEC 61000-4-30:2008 requires the frequency of signaling voltage must be at most 3,000 Hz. If number of samples per cycle is 128, which means the sample rate after resampling is 6,400 Hz, the measured signaling voltage is right at the edge of the passband of resampling filters thus the results might not be accurate enough. type of reference voltage specifies the type of voltage reference level for detection of voltage dip and swell events according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. 0Constant (default)Specifies to use constant reference voltage.1SlidingSpecifies to use sliding reference voltage. symmetrical components calculation specifies the calculation types for symmetrical components. The default is Phase to Neutral. The input type of this VI must match the voltage input type of the Symmetrical Components VI and the Symmetrical Components (1 Cycle) VI. If the types do not match, use the D to Y Conversion VI to convert line voltages to phase voltages or use the Y to D Conversion VI to convert phase voltages to line voltages before using the two VIs. 0Phase to PhaseSpecifies to calculate the line voltages.1Phase to Neutral (default)Specifies to calculate the phase voltages. voltage channels specifies the configuration of voltage channels. For line voltages, Phase A (L1), Phase B (L2), and Phase C (L3) equal Phase AB, Phase BC, and Phase CA respectively. The physical connection type of each voltage channel can be Phase to Phase or Phase to Neutral. 3-phase system ID specifies the ID of the three-phase system that the physical channel belongs to. physical channels specifies the physical channels of the 3-phase system. channel phase specifies the channel phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)1Phase B (L2)2Phase C (L3)3Phase N (Neutral) physical channel index specifies the physical channel index of the current bus. The bus can contain only voltage channels, only current channels, or a combination of voltage and current channels. For any type of channels, the index of the bus starts from 0 and matches the physical channel index. current channels specifies the configuration of current channels. 3-phase system ID specifies the ID of the three-phase system that the physical channel belongs to. physical channels specifies the physical channels of the 3-phase system. channel phase specifies the channel phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)1Phase B (L2)2Phase C (L3)3Phase N (Neutral) physical channel index specifies the physical channel index of the current bus. The bus can contain only voltage channels, only current channels, or a combination of voltage and current channels. For any type of channels, the index of the bus starts from 0 and matches the physical channel index. |
| --- | --- |
|  | nominal voltage specifies the nominal voltage reference level, in volts, for detecting voltage dips, swells, and interruptions, measuring deviations, and detecting rapid voltage change events. The default is 230 volts. |
|  | nominal frequency specifies the nominal frequency of the sampled signal. 050 Hz (default)160 Hz |
| 0 | 50 Hz (default) |
| 1 | 60 Hz |
|  | samples per cycle specifies the number of samples per cycle to resample using even numbers, whose range is from 128 to 512. More samples per cycle require more real-time computation resources of further processing on the resampled signal. Choosing low number of samples per cycle might make the signaling voltage measurement incompliant with IEC 61000-4-30:2008. The default is 192. For example, in 50 Hz systems, IEC 61000-4-30:2008 requires the frequency of signaling voltage must be at most 3,000 Hz. If number of samples per cycle is 128, which means the sample rate after resampling is 6,400 Hz, the measured signaling voltage is right at the edge of the passband of resampling filters thus the results might not be accurate enough. |
|  | type of reference voltage specifies the type of voltage reference level for detection of voltage dip and swell events according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. 0Constant (default)Specifies to use constant reference voltage.1SlidingSpecifies to use sliding reference voltage. |
| 0 | Constant (default)Specifies to use constant reference voltage. |
| 1 | SlidingSpecifies to use sliding reference voltage. |
|  | symmetrical components calculation specifies the calculation types for symmetrical components. The default is Phase to Neutral. The input type of this VI must match the voltage input type of the Symmetrical Components VI and the Symmetrical Components (1 Cycle) VI. If the types do not match, use the D to Y Conversion VI to convert line voltages to phase voltages or use the Y to D Conversion VI to convert phase voltages to line voltages before using the two VIs. 0Phase to PhaseSpecifies to calculate the line voltages.1Phase to Neutral (default)Specifies to calculate the phase voltages. |
| 0 | Phase to PhaseSpecifies to calculate the line voltages. |
| 1 | Phase to Neutral (default)Specifies to calculate the phase voltages. |
|  | voltage channels specifies the configuration of voltage channels. For line voltages, Phase A (L1), Phase B (L2), and Phase C (L3) equal Phase AB, Phase BC, and Phase CA respectively. The physical connection type of each voltage channel can be Phase to Phase or Phase to Neutral. 3-phase system ID specifies the ID of the three-phase system that the physical channel belongs to. physical channels specifies the physical channels of the 3-phase system. channel phase specifies the channel phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)1Phase B (L2)2Phase C (L3)3Phase N (Neutral) physical channel index specifies the physical channel index of the current bus. The bus can contain only voltage channels, only current channels, or a combination of voltage and current channels. For any type of channels, the index of the bus starts from 0 and matches the physical channel index. |
|  | 3-phase system ID specifies the ID of the three-phase system that the physical channel belongs to. |
|  | physical channels specifies the physical channels of the 3-phase system. channel phase specifies the channel phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)1Phase B (L2)2Phase C (L3)3Phase N (Neutral) physical channel index specifies the physical channel index of the current bus. The bus can contain only voltage channels, only current channels, or a combination of voltage and current channels. For any type of channels, the index of the bus starts from 0 and matches the physical channel index. |
|  | channel phase specifies the channel phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)1Phase B (L2)2Phase C (L3)3Phase N (Neutral) |
| 0 | Phase A (L1) (default) |
| 1 | Phase B (L2) |
| 2 | Phase C (L3) |
| 3 | Phase N (Neutral) |
|  | physical channel index specifies the physical channel index of the current bus. The bus can contain only voltage channels, only current channels, or a combination of voltage and current channels. For any type of channels, the index of the bus starts from 0 and matches the physical channel index. |
|  | current channels specifies the configuration of current channels. 3-phase system ID specifies the ID of the three-phase system that the physical channel belongs to. physical channels specifies the physical channels of the 3-phase system. channel phase specifies the channel phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)1Phase B (L2)2Phase C (L3)3Phase N (Neutral) physical channel index specifies the physical channel index of the current bus. The bus can contain only voltage channels, only current channels, or a combination of voltage and current channels. For any type of channels, the index of the bus starts from 0 and matches the physical channel index. |
|  | 3-phase system ID specifies the ID of the three-phase system that the physical channel belongs to. |

The bus can contain only voltage channels, only current channels, or a combination of voltage and current channels. For any type of channels, the index of the bus starts from 0 and matches the physical channel index. |
|  | channel phase specifies the channel phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)1Phase B (L2)2Phase C (L3)3Phase N (Neutral) |
| 0 | Phase A (L1) (default) |
| 1 | Phase B (L2) |
| 2 | Phase C (L3) |
| 3 | Phase N (Neutral) |
|  | physical channel index specifies the physical channel index of the current bus. The bus can contain only voltage channels, only current channels, or a combination of voltage and current channels. For any type of channels, the index of the bus starts from 0 and matches the physical channel index. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | nominal sample rate returns the nominal sample rate in hertz. |
|  | system info out returns information about basic configuration of the electrical power system. You use the information of system info out to perform further power quality analysis. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Multiple Buses

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Initialize_System_Multiple_Buses.gif']

|  | bus configuration specifies the configurations of multiple buses. nominal voltage specifies the nominal voltage reference level, in volts, for detecting voltage dips, swells, and interruptions, measuring deviations, and detecting rapid voltage change events. The default is 230 volts. nominal frequency specifies the nominal frequency of the sampled signal. 050 Hz (default)160 Hz samples per cycle specifies the number of samples per cycle to resample using even numbers, whose range is from 128 to 512. More samples per cycle require more real-time computation resources of further processing on the resampled signal. Choosing low number of samples per cycle might make the signaling voltage measurement incompliant with IEC 61000-4-30:2008. The default is 192. For example, in 50 Hz systems, IEC 61000-4-30:2008 requires the frequency of signaling voltage must be at most 3,000 Hz. If number of samples per cycle is 128, which means the sample rate after resampling is 6,400 Hz, the measured signaling voltage is right at the edge of the passband of resampling filters thus the results might not be accurate enough. type of reference voltage specifies the type of voltage reference level for detection of voltage dip and swell events according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. 0Constant (default)Specifies to use constant reference voltage.1SlidingSpecifies to use sliding reference voltage. symmetrical components calculation specifies the calculation types for symmetrical components. The default is Phase to Neutral. The input type of this VI must match the voltage input type of the Symmetrical Components VI and the Symmetrical Components (1 Cycle) VI. If the types do not match, use the D to Y Conversion VI to convert line voltages to phase voltages or use the Y to D Conversion VI to convert phase voltages to line voltages before using the two VIs. 0Phase to PhaseSpecifies to calculate the line voltages.1Phase to Neutral (default)Specifies to calculate the phase voltages. voltage channels specifies the configuration of voltage channels. For line voltages, Phase A (L1), Phase B (L2), and Phase C (L3) equal Phase AB, Phase BC, and Phase CA respectively. The physical connection type of each voltage channel can be Phase to Phase or Phase to Neutral. 3-phase system ID specifies the ID of the three-phase system that the physical channel belongs to. physical channels specifies the physical channels of the 3-phase systems. channel phase specifies the channel phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)1Phase B (L2)2Phase C (L3)3Phase N (Neutral) physical channel index specifies the physical channel index of the current bus. Each bus can contain only voltage channels, only current channels, or a combination of voltage and current channels. For any type of channels, the index of each bus starts from 0 and matches the physical channel index. current channels specifies the configuration of current channels. 3-phase system ID specifies the ID of the three-phase system that the physical channel belongs to. physical channels specifies the physical channels of the 3-phase systems. channel phase specifies the channel phase. 0Phase A (L1) (default)1Phase B (L2)2Phase C (L3)3Phase N (Neutral) physical channel index specifies the physical channel index of the current bus. Each bus can contain only voltage channels, only current channels, or a combination of voltage and current channels. For any type of channels, the index of each bus starts from 0 and matches the physical channel index. |
| --- | --- |
|  | nominal voltage specifies the nominal voltage reference level, in volts, for detecting voltage dips, swells, and interruptions, measuring deviations, and detecting rapid voltage change events. The default is 230 volts. |
|  | nominal frequency specifies the nominal frequency of the sampled signal. 050 Hz (default)160 Hz |
| 0 | 50 Hz (default) |
| 1 | 60 Hz |
|  | samples per cycle specifies the number of samples per cycle to resample using even numbers, whose range is from 128 to 512. More samples per cycle require more real-time computation resources of further processing on the resampled signal. Choosing low number of samples per cycle might make the signaling voltage measurement incompliant with IEC 61000-4-30:2008. The default is 192. For example, in 50 Hz systems, IEC 61000-4-30:2008 requires the frequency of signaling voltage must be at most 3,000 Hz. If number of samples per cycle is 128, which means the sample rate after resampling is 6,400 Hz, the measured signaling voltage is right at the edge of the passband of resampling filters thus the results might not be accurate enough. |
|  | type of reference voltage specifies the type of voltage reference level for detection of voltage dip and swell events according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. 0Constant (default)Specifies to use constant reference voltage.1SlidingSpecifies to use sliding reference voltage. |

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=instantaneous-flicker-sensation-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00082: Instantaneous Flicker Sensation VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `instantaneous-flicker-sensation-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/instantaneous-flicker-sensation-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Flicker VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the instantaneous flicker values of a voltage waveform, according to IEC 61000-4-15:2010. This VI supports measuring single-bus single-line or multiple-bus single-line systems. Details Examples reset? specifies whether to init

### Instantaneous Flicker Sensation VI

**Owning Palette:** Flicker VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the instantaneous flicker values of a voltage waveform, according to IEC 61000-4-15:2010.

This VI supports measuring single-bus single-line or multiple-bus single-line systems.

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/flicker_sensation.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to initialize the flicker algorithm. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the VI initializes automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | voltage waveforms specifies the input voltage waveforms. Each array element represents one phase and contains samples representing 10-cycle time interval for a 50 Hz power system or 12-cycle time interval for a 60 Hz power system. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | voltage system specifies the voltage system from which you acquire the input voltage waveforms. 0120 V (default)1230 V |
| 0 | 120 V (default) |
| 1 | 230 V |
|  | half-cycle voltage RMS specifies RMS values measured over half cycle, which starts at fundamental zero crossing and refreshes each half cycle. Each element of half-cycle voltage RMS corresponds to one channel or phase. timestamps specifies the end time of the half cycle. RMS values specifies the RMS value over half cycle. |
|  | timestamps specifies the end time of the half cycle. |
|  | RMS values specifies the RMS value over half cycle. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the electrical power system. Use the Initialize System VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | Pinst returns instantaneous flicker values according to IEC 61000-4-15:2010. t0 returns the start time of the waveform. dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y returns the instantaneous flicker values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 returns the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y returns the instantaneous flicker values of the waveform. |
|  | system info out returns information about the basic configuration of the electrical power system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Instantaneous Flicker Sensation Details

**Related Information**
Line and Bus Configurations


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Instantaneous Flicker Sensation VI:

- Flicker Measurement (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (DAQmx)
- Flicker Measurement VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)
- Flicker Measurement VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (SAR)
- Multiple Bus Power Quality Applications VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Multiple Line Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)

Parent topic:

Flicker VIs

## TOPIC 00083: Integrate Ampere to Coulomb VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `integrate-ampere-to-coulomb-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/integrate-ampere-to-coulomb-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Battery VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Measures the electrical charge of a battery pack in a specific amount of time. If you run this VI multiple times, this VI accumulates the charge value measured in previous executions. Details Example Use the pull-down menu to select an i

### Integrate Ampere to Coulomb VI

**Owning Palette:** Battery VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Measures the electrical charge of a battery pack in a specific amount of time.

If you run this VI multiple times, this VI accumulates the charge value measured in previous executions.

Details Example

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Integrate Ampere to Coulomb (1 Channel)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Integrate_Ampere_to_Coulomb__1_Channel.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether this VI calculates cumulative charge and total integration duration starting from 0 and sets measurement timestamp to the start time, t0, of current waveform. The default is False, which means this VI accumulates the values for cumulative charge and total integration duration and does not set measurement timestamp to the start time, t0, of current waveform. If you call this VI for the first time, this VI automatically resets. |
| --- | --- |
|  | current waveform specifies the current signal in amperes. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | integration method specifies the method to calculate the charge of the battery pack. 0Trapezoidal Rule1Simpson's Rule (Default)2Simpson's 3/8 Rule3Bode Rule |
| 0 | Trapezoidal Rule |
| 1 | Simpson's Rule (Default) |
| 2 | Simpson's 3/8 Rule |
| 3 | Bode Rule |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | cumulative charge returns the electrical charge, in coulombs, transferred since the last reset. |
|  | total integration duration returns the total amount of measurement time, in seconds, since the last reset. |
|  | measurement timestamp returns the time of the charge measurement at the last reset. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Integrate Ampere to Coulomb (N Channels)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Integrate_Ampere_to_Coulomb__N_Channels.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether this VI calculates cumulative charges and total integration duration starting from 0 and sets measurement timestamp to the start time, t0, of current waveform. The default is False, which means this VI accumulates the values for cumulative charges and total integration duration and does not set measurement timestamp to the start time, t0, of current waveforms. If you call this VI for the first time, this VI automatically resets. |
| --- | --- |
|  | current waveforms specifies the waveforms of the current signals, in amperes, for multiple channels. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | integration method specifies the method to calculate the charge of the battery pack. 0Trapezoidal Rule1Simpson's Rule (Default)2Simpson's 3/8 Rule3Bode Rule |
| 0 | Trapezoidal Rule |
| 1 | Simpson's Rule (Default) |
| 2 | Simpson's 3/8 Rule |
| 3 | Bode Rule |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | cumulative charges returns the electrical charge, in coulombs, transferred since the last reset for each channel. |
|  | total integration duration returns the total amount of measurement time, in seconds, since the last reset. |
|  | measurement timestamp returns the time of the charge measurement at the last reset. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Integrate Ampere to Coulomb Details

This VI uses the following equation to calculate the electrical charge:

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/eq_ampere_to_coulomb.gif']

where

I represents the current in amperes

t represents the time in seconds


Example

Refer to the Process Pack Voltage and Current from BMS VI in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Battery directory for an example of using the Integrate Ampere to Coulomb VI.

Parent topic:

Battery VIs

## TOPIC 00084: Integrate Watt to Joule VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `integrate-watt-to-joule-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/integrate-watt-to-joule-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Battery VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Measures the electrical energy of a battery pack in a specific amount of time. If you run this VI multiple times, this VI accumulates the energy values measured in previous executions. Details Example Use the pull-down menu to select an

### Integrate Watt to Joule VI

**Owning Palette:** Battery VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Measures the electrical energy of a battery pack in a specific amount of time.

If you run this VI multiple times, this VI accumulates the energy values measured in previous executions.

Details Example

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Integrate Watt to Joule (1 Channel)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Integrate_Watt_to_Joule__1_Channel.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether this VI calculates cumulative energy and total integration duration starting from 0 and sets measurement timestamp to start timestamp. The default is False, which means this VI accumulates the values for cumulative energy and total integration duration and does not set measurement timestamp to start timestamp. If you call this VI for the first time, this VI automatically resets. |
| --- | --- |
|  | power specifies the delivered power in watts. |
|  | interval duration specifies the duration, in seconds, of the interval of the power measurement. |
|  | start timestamp specifies the time when the power measurement starts. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | cumulative energy returns the electrical energy, in joules, accumulated since the last reset. |
|  | total integration duration returns the time duration, in seconds, accumulated since the last reset. |
|  | measurement timestamp returns the time of the energy measurement at the last reset. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Integrate Watt to Joule (N Channels)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Integrate_Watt_to_Joule__N_Channels.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether this VI calculates cumulative energies and total integration duration starting from 0 and sets measurement timestamp to start timestamp. The default is False, which means this VI accumulates the values for cumulative energies and total integration duration and does not set measurement timestamp to start timestamp. If you call this VI for the first time, this VI automatically resets. |
| --- | --- |
|  | powers specifies the delivered power, in watts, for each channel. |
|  | interval duration specifies the duration, in seconds, of the interval of the power measurement. |
|  | start timestamp specifies the time when the power measurement starts. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | cumulative energies returns the electrical energies, in joules, accumulated since the last reset for each channel. |
|  | total integration duration returns the time duration, in seconds, accumulated since the last reset. |
|  | measurement timestamp returns the time of the energy measurement at the last reset. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Integrate Watt to Joule Details

This VI uses the following equation to calculate the electrical energy:

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/eq_watt_to_joule.gif']

where

P represents the power in watts

t represents the time in seconds


Example

Refer to the Process Pack Voltage and Current from BMS VI in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Battery directory for an example of using the Integrate Watt to Joule VI.

Parent topic:

Battery VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=inverse-time-curves-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00085: Inverse Time Curves VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `inverse-time-curves-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/inverse-time-curves-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Protection VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Generates memory initial values and memory size, which contain the information of the inverse time curves, for the FPGA Inverse Time Delay VI to use. You can compensate for the processing delay of the input energizing quantity under a

### Inverse Time Curves VI

**Owning Palette:** Protection VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Generates memory initial values and memory size, which contain the information of the inverse time curves, for the FPGA Inverse Time Delay VI to use. You can compensate for the processing delay of the input energizing quantity under a specific G/Gs to achieve higher operate time accuracy.

Example

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Inverse Time Curves (IEC, Dependent Reset)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Inverse_Time_Curves__IEC_Dependent_Reset.gif']

|  | GD specifies the value of the input energizing quantity when the operate time turns from dependent time operation to independent time operation. The default is 25. GD must be between 2 and 64. |
| --- | --- |
|  | curve type specifies the curve types that IEC 60255-151:2009 defines. 0IEC inverse (default)1IEC very inverse2IEC extremely inverse |
| 0 | IEC inverse (default) |
| 1 | IEC very inverse |
| 2 | IEC extremely inverse |
|  | time multiplier setting specifies the time multiplier that applies to the theoretical curve of time versus energizing quantity. The default is 1. time multiplier setting must be between 0.025 and 20. |
|  | reset time parameters specifies the dependent reset time parameters. Refer to IEC 60255-151:2009 for detailed information about the dependent reset time parameters. tr specifies the dependent reset time setting in seconds. The default is 1 second. alpha specifies a constant for characterizing the curve type that you select. The default is 1. |
|  | tr specifies the dependent reset time setting in seconds. The default is 1 second. |
|  | alpha specifies a constant for characterizing the curve type that you select. The default is 1. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | operate time compensation specifies the compensation for the operate time. You can configure the value of G/Gs and its corresponding operate time compensation. G/Gs specifies the ratio between G and Gs. G is the input energizing quantity and Gs is the pick-up setting value. compensation specifies the time, in seconds, to compensate for the processing delay of the input energizing quantity under a specific G/Gs to achieve higher operate time accuracy. The processing delay of the input energizing quantity may include the internal measurement delay, the delay of the digital I/O (DIO) modules, and so on. |
|  | G/Gs specifies the ratio between G and Gs. G is the input energizing quantity and Gs is the pick-up setting value. |
|  | compensation specifies the time, in seconds, to compensate for the processing delay of the input energizing quantity under a specific G/Gs to achieve higher operate time accuracy. The processing delay of the input energizing quantity may include the internal measurement delay, the delay of the digital I/O (DIO) modules, and so on. |
|  | memory initial values returns the memory initial values as <+,51,51> fixed-point numbers for representing the curve. The lower 30 bits represent the reciprocal of the operate or reset time. The higher 21 bits represent the difference between the current value and the next value. If the value of operate or reset time is less than 0.002048, this VI coerces the value to 0.002048. If the difference between the reciprocals of two values of operate or reset time is greater than 3.81469, this VI coerces the difference to 3.81469. The coercion may cause a slight operate timing inaccuracy or reset timing inaccuracy. |
|  | memory size returns the value of the memory size, which is equal to 8 * GD. Wire memory size to the FPGA Inverse Time Delay VI for implementing protection devices. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |
|  | curve data returns raw curve data that have eight samples in each G/Gs step. You can view the curve data in XY graphs. G/Gs returns the ratios between G and Gs. G is the input energizing quantity and Gs is the pick-up setting value. When you use an XY graph to view the curve, G/Gs is the x value. times returns the operate times, in seconds, when G/Gs is greater than or equal to 1 and the reset time when G/Gs is smaller than 1. When you use an XY graph to view the curve, times is the y value. |
|  | G/Gs returns the ratios between G and Gs. G is the input energizing quantity and Gs is the pick-up setting value. When you use an XY graph to view the curve, G/Gs is the x value. |
|  | times returns the operate times, in seconds, when G/Gs is greater than or equal to 1 and the reset time when G/Gs is smaller than 1. When you use an XY graph to view the curve, times is the y value. |

Inverse Time Curves (IEC, Definite Reset)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Inverse_Time_Curves__IEC_Definite_Reset.gif']

|  | GD specifies the value of the input energizing quantity when the operate time turns from dependent time operation to independent time operation. The default is 25. GD must be between 2 and 64. |
| --- | --- |
|  | curve type specifies the curve types that IEC 60255-151:2009 defines. 0IEC inverse (default)1IEC very inverse2IEC extremely inverse |
| 0 | IEC inverse (default) |
| 1 | IEC very inverse |
| 2 | IEC extremely inverse |
|  | time multiplier setting specifies the time multiplier that applies to the theoretical curve of time versus energizing quantity. The default is 1. time multiplier setting must be between 0.025 and 20. |
|  | reset delay specifies the definite reset delay in seconds. The default is 0 second. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | operate time compensation specifies the compensation for the operate time. You can configure the value of G/Gs and its corresponding operate time compensation. Inverse Time Curves (Formula, Dependent Reset)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Inverse_Time_Curves__Formula_Dependent_Reset.gif']

|  | GD specifies the value of the input energizing quantity when the operate time turns from dependent time operation to independent time operation. The default is 25. GD must be between 2 and 64. |
| --- | --- |
|  | operate time parameters specifies the operate time parameters according to a generalized formula. where G is the input energizing quantity and Gs is the pick-up setting value. A specifies a constant, in seconds, for characterizing the curve that you customize. alpha specifies a constant for characterizing the curve that you customize. K2 specifies a constant, in seconds, for characterizing the curve that you customize. B specifies a constant, in seconds, for characterizing the curve that you customize. K1 specifies a constant, in seconds, for characterizing the curve that you customize. K3 specifies a constant, in seconds, for characterizing the curve that you customize. |
|  | A specifies a constant, in seconds, for characterizing the curve that you customize. |
|  | alpha specifies a constant for characterizing the curve that you customize. |
|  | K2 specifies a constant, in seconds, for characterizing the curve that you customize. |
|  | B specifies a constant, in seconds, for characterizing the curve that you customize. |
|  | K1 specifies a constant, in seconds, for characterizing the curve that you customize. |
|  | K3 specifies a constant, in seconds, for characterizing the curve that you customize. |
|  | time multiplier setting specifies the time multiplier that applies to the theoretical curve of time versus energizing quantity. The default is 1. time multiplier setting must be between 0.025 and 20. |
|  | reset time parameters specifies the dependent reset time parameters. Refer to IEC 60255-151:2009 for detailed information about the dependent reset time parameters. tr specifies the dependent reset time setting in seconds. The default is 1 second. alpha specifies a constant for characterizing the curve type that you select. The default is 1. |
|  | tr specifies the dependent reset time setting in seconds. The default is 1 second. |
|  | alpha specifies a constant for characterizing the curve type that you select. The default is 1. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | operate time compensation specifies the compensation for the operate time. You can configure the value of G/Gs and its corresponding operate time compensation.
