Inverse Time Curves (Curve Data)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Inverse_Time_Curves__Curve_Data.gif']

|  | curve data specifies curve data that you can customize by configuring the value of G/Gs and its corresponding operate time or reset time. When G/Gs is greater than or equal to 1, the operate time of the curve data must be inversely proportional to G/Gs. When G/Gs is less than 1, the reset time of the curve data must be proportional to G/Gs. G/Gs specifies the ratio between G and Gs. G is the input energizing quantity and Gs is the pick-up setting value. operate or reset time specifies the operate time, in seconds, when G/Gs is greater than or equal to 1 and the reset time when G/Gs is smaller than 1. If some values of operate or reset time are less than what the internal fixed-point data type can represent, this VI coerces those values to 0.002048. |
| --- | --- |
|  | G/Gs specifies the ratio between G and Gs. G is the input energizing quantity and Gs is the pick-up setting value. |
|  | operate or reset time specifies the operate time, in seconds, when G/Gs is greater than or equal to 1 and the reset time when G/Gs is smaller than 1. If some values of operate or reset time are less than what the internal fixed-point data type can represent, this VI coerces those values to 0.002048. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | operate time compensation specifies the compensation for the operate time. You can configure the value of G/Gs and its corresponding operate time compensation. G/Gs specifies the ratio between G and Gs. G is the input energizing quantity and Gs is the pick-up setting value. compensation specifies the time, in seconds, to compensate for the processing delay of the input energizing quantity under a specific G/Gs to achieve higher operate time accuracy. The processing delay of the input energizing quantity may include the internal measurement delay, the delay of the digital I/O (DIO) modules, and so on. |
|  | G/Gs specifies the ratio between G and Gs. G is the input energizing quantity and Gs is the pick-up setting value. |
|  | compensation specifies the time, in seconds, to compensate for the processing delay of the input energizing quantity under a specific G/Gs to achieve higher operate time accuracy. The processing delay of the input energizing quantity may include the internal measurement delay, the delay of the digital I/O (DIO) modules, and so on. |
|  | memory initial values returns the memory initial values as <+,51,51> fixed-point numbers for representing the curve. The lower 30 bits represent the reciprocal of the operate or reset time. The higher 21 bits represent the difference between the current value and the next value. If the value of operate or reset time is less than 0.002048, this VI coerces the value to 0.002048. If the difference between the reciprocals of two values of operate or reset time is greater than 3.81469, this VI coerces the difference to 3.81469. The coercion may cause a slight operate timing inaccuracy or reset timing inaccuracy. |
|  | memory size returns the value of the memory size, which is equal to 8 * GD. GD is the value of the input energizing quantity when the operate time turns from dependent time operation to independent time operation. Wire memory size to the FPGA Inverse Time Delay VI for implementing protection devices. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |
|  | actual curve data returns raw curve data that have eight samples in each G/Gs step. You can view the curve data in XY graphs. G/Gs returns the ratios between G and Gs. G is the input energizing quantity and Gs is the pick-up setting value. When you use an XY graph to view the curve, G/Gs is the x value. times returns the operate times, in seconds, when G/Gs is greater than or equal to 1 and the reset time when G/Gs is smaller than 1. When you use an XY graph to view the curve, times is the y value. |
|  | G/Gs returns the ratios between G and Gs. G is the input energizing quantity and Gs is the pick-up setting value. When you use an XY graph to view the curve, G/Gs is the x value. |
|  | times returns the operate times, in seconds, when G/Gs is greater than or equal to 1 and the reset time when G/Gs is smaller than 1. When you use an XY graph to view the curve, times is the y value. |

Example

Refer to the Recloser Controller.lvproj in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Protection\Recloser Controller directory for an example of using the Inverse Time Curves VI.

Parent topic:

Protection VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=inverter-efficiency-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00086: Inverter Efficiency VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `inverter-efficiency-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/inverter-efficiency-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Efficiency VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the power efficiency of an inverter. Details Examples DC power specifies the power of the DC signal, in watts, from a battery pack. power value specifies the active power, effective apparent power, non-active power, and pow

### Inverter Efficiency VI

**Owning Palette:** Efficiency VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the power efficiency of an inverter.

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Inverter_Efficiency.gif']

|  | DC power specifies the power of the DC signal, in watts, from a battery pack. |
| --- | --- |
|  | power value specifies the active power, effective apparent power, non-active power, and power factor. This VI uses only the active power to calculate the power efficiency of the inverter. Refer to the Power VI for more information about the power value. active power specifies the active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. effective apparent power specifies the effective apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. non-active power specifies the non-active power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. power factor specifies the power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. |
|  | active power specifies the active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. |
|  | effective apparent power specifies the effective apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. |
|  | non-active power specifies the non-active power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. |
|  | power factor specifies the power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | inverter efficiency returns the power efficiency of the inverter. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Inverter Efficiency Details

This VI uses the following equation to calculate the inverter efficiency:

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/eq_inverter_efficiency.gif']


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Inverter Efficiency VI:

- Efficiency VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Efficiency
- Traction Inverter and Electric Motor Efficiency Measurement (TDMS) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Traction Inverter

Parent topic:

Efficiency VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=long-term-flicker-severity-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00087: Long-Term Flicker Severity VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `long-term-flicker-severity-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/long-term-flicker-severity-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Flicker VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Aggregates short-term flicker values and returns the long-term flicker of a voltage waveform, according to IEC 61000-4-15:2010. Long-term flicker is the cubic average of short-term flicker values recorded over a two-hour period. This VI

### Long-Term Flicker Severity VI

**Owning Palette:** Flicker VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Aggregates short-term flicker values and returns the long-term flicker of a voltage waveform, according to IEC 61000-4-15:2010. Long-term flicker is the cubic average of short-term flicker values recorded over a two-hour period.

This VI supports measuring single-bus single-line or multiple-bus single-line systems.

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/long_term_flicker_severity.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to initialize the flicker algorithm. The default is FALSE. When you call this VI for the first time, the VI initializes automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | Pst specifies the short-term flicker values. |
|  | Pst timestamp specifies the end time of the 10-minute period for the short-term flicker. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the electrical power system. Use the Initialize System VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | Plt returns long-term flicker derived from the short-term flicker values over a two-hour period. |
|  | Plt timestamp returns the end time of the two-hour period for the long-term flicker. |
|  | system info out returns information about the basic configuration of the electrical power system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |
|  | output valid? returns TRUE if the output data is valid. |

Long-Term Flicker Severity Details

**Related Information**
Line and Bus Configurations


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Long-Term Flicker Severity VI:

- Flicker Measurement (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (DAQmx)
- Flicker Measurement VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)
- Flicker Measurement VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (SAR)
- Multiple Bus Power Quality Applications VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Multiple Line Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)

Parent topic:

Flicker VIs

## TOPIC 00088: MCSA Spectra VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `mcsa-spectra-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/mcsa-spectra-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Motor VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the zoom fast Fourier Transform (FFT) spectra of the motor. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. Compared with the Spectrum VI and the Harmonic Spectrum VI that use FFT to calculate spectra, this VI

### MCSA Spectra VI

**Owning Palette:** Motor VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Note

Details Example

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

N Motors

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/MCSA_Spectra_N_Motors.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | waveforms specifies the motor waveforms that the VI uses to calculate the spectra. If you measure one phase of a motor with this VI, specify the one-phase data in waveforms and specify 1-phase signal for algorithm. If you measure three-phase data of a motor, specify the three-phase data in waveforms and specify 3-phase signals for algorithm. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | spectrum settings specifies the settings to calculate the spectrum. If you change spectrum settings while the VI runs, the new settings take effect only when reset? is TRUE. number of lines specifies the number of spectral lines to calculate between 0 hertz and maximum frequency. The default is 15,000. % overlap specifies the percentage of data reused by consecutive FFT computations. The value range is from 0 to 100. The default is 0. You can use % overlap to make the computations more responsive when the values of maximum frequency and number of lines require a longer time to produce a new valid output. The VI typically does not return a spectrum each time LabVIEW calls this VI. You can use % overlap to increase the rate at which a new spectrum is computed by reusing acquired data. For example, setting % overlap to 50 causes this VI to compute a new spectrum twice as often as a setting of 0, because each computation reuses half the data. maximum frequency specifies the maximum frequency, in hertz, of the band of waveforms to analyze. The default is 250 hertz. The value range is from 0 to the Nyquist frequency. maximum frequency must be a constant value and not a variable value. Refer to the MCSA Spectra VI Details for more information about calculating the maximum frequency. |
|  | number of lines specifies the number of spectral lines to calculate between 0 hertz and maximum frequency. The default is 15,000. |
|  | % overlap specifies the percentage of data reused by consecutive FFT computations. The value range is from 0 to 100. The default is 0. You can use % overlap to make the computations more responsive when the values of maximum frequency and number of lines require a longer time to produce a new valid output. The VI typically does not return a spectrum each time LabVIEW calls this VI. You can use % overlap to increase the rate at which a new spectrum is computed by reusing acquired data. For example, setting % overlap to 50 causes this VI to compute a new spectrum twice as often as a setting of 0, because each computation reuses half the data. |
|  | maximum frequency specifies the maximum frequency, in hertz, of the band of waveforms to analyze. The default is 250 hertz. The value range is from 0 to the Nyquist frequency. maximum frequency must be a constant value and not a variable value. Refer to the MCSA Spectra VI Details for more information about calculating the maximum frequency. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | demodulation settings specifies the settings to calculate the spectra. If you change demodulation settings while the VI runs, the new settings take effect only when reset? is TRUE. enable? specifies whether spectra of waveforms is demodulated. The default is FALSE. algorithm specifies the input signal type. This VI uses different demodulation algorithms for different signal types. The default is 3-phase signals. 01-phase signalSpecifies to use a one-phase signal from a motor for demodulation.13-phase signals (default)Specifies to use three-phase signals from a motor for demodulation. The array size of waveforms must be a multiple of three. |
|  | enable? specifies whether spectra of waveforms is demodulated. The default is FALSE. |
|  | algorithm specifies the input signal type. This VI uses different demodulation algorithms for different signal types. The default is 3-phase signals. 01-phase signalSpecifies to use a one-phase signal from a motor for demodulation.13-phase signals (default)Specifies to use three-phase signals from a motor for demodulation. The array size of waveforms must be a multiple of three. |
| 0 | 1-phase signalSpecifies to use a one-phase signal from a motor for demodulation. |
| 1 | 3-phase signals (default)Specifies to use three-phase signals from a motor for demodulation. The array size of waveforms must be a multiple of three. |
|  | new spectra available? returns TRUE when this VI finishes updating spectra. |
|  | spectrum information returns the spectrum of waveforms. spectra returns the spectra for multiple motors. One array element represents one spectrum for one motor. f0 returns the start frequency, in hertz, of the spectrum. df returns the frequency resolution, in hertz, of the spectrum. magnitude returns the magnitude, in decibels, of the spectrum. The decibel reference is 1 Ampere or 1 Volt if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is demodulated. The decibel reference is the fundamental component magnitude value if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is not demodulated. demodulated? returns whether spectra are demodulated. The output returns TRUE if you specify enable? to be TRUE. |
|  | spectra returns the spectra for multiple motors. One array element represents one spectrum for one motor. f0 returns the start frequency, in hertz, of the spectrum. df returns the frequency resolution, in hertz, of the spectrum. magnitude returns the magnitude, in decibels, of the spectrum. The decibel reference is 1 Ampere or 1 Volt if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is demodulated. The decibel reference is the fundamental component magnitude value if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is not demodulated. |
|  | f0 returns the start frequency, in hertz, of the spectrum. |
|  | df returns the frequency resolution, in hertz, of the spectrum. |
|  | magnitude returns the magnitude, in decibels, of the spectrum. The decibel reference is 1 Ampere or 1 Volt if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is demodulated. The decibel reference is the fundamental component magnitude value if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is not demodulated. |
|  | demodulated? returns whether spectra are demodulated. The output returns TRUE if you specify enable? to be TRUE. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

You can use % overlap to make the computations more responsive when the values of maximum frequency and number of lines require a longer time to produce a new valid output. The VI typically does not return a spectrum each time LabVIEW calls this VI. You can use % overlap to increase the rate at which a new spectrum is computed by reusing acquired data. For example, setting % overlap to 50 causes this VI to compute a new spectrum twice as often as a setting of 0, because each computation reuses half the data. |

|  | initial conditions specifies initial internal VI conditions to guarantee consecutive calculation correctness. If you use this VI for the first time or reset? is TRUE, this VI automatically creates the initial internal conditions. Otherwise, wire the most recent final conditions to the subsequent initial conditions to inherit the conditions. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | demodulation settings specifies the settings to calculate the spectrum. If you change demodulation settings when the VI runs, the new settings take effect only when reset? is TRUE. enable? specifies whether spectrum of waveforms is demodulated. The default is FALSE. algorithm specifies the signal source type to use different demodulation algorithms. 01-phase signalSpecifies that the signal type is a one-phase signal from a motor for demodulation. The array size of waveforms must be one.13-phase signals(default) Specifies that the signal type is three-phase signals from a motor for demodulation. The array size of waveforms must be three. |
|  | enable? specifies whether spectrum of waveforms is demodulated. The default is FALSE. |
|  | algorithm specifies the signal source type to use different demodulation algorithms. 01-phase signalSpecifies that the signal type is a one-phase signal from a motor for demodulation. The array size of waveforms must be one.13-phase signals(default) Specifies that the signal type is three-phase signals from a motor for demodulation. The array size of waveforms must be three. |
| 0 | 1-phase signalSpecifies that the signal type is a one-phase signal from a motor for demodulation. The array size of waveforms must be one. |
| 1 | 3-phase signals(default) Specifies that the signal type is three-phase signals from a motor for demodulation. The array size of waveforms must be three. |
|  | new spectrum available? returns TRUE when this VI finishes updating the spectrum. |
|  | spectrum information returns the spectrum of waveforms after calculation. spectrum returns the spectrum of the motor. f0 returns the start frequency, in hertz, of the spectrum. df returns the frequency resolution, in hertz, of the spectrum. magnitude returns the magnitude, in decibels, of the spectrum. The decibel reference is 1 Ampere or 1 Volt if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is demodulated. The decibel reference is the fundamental component magnitude value if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is not demodulated. demodulated? returns whether spectrum is demodulated. The output returns TRUE if you specify enable? to be TRUE. |
|  | spectrum returns the spectrum of the motor. f0 returns the start frequency, in hertz, of the spectrum. df returns the frequency resolution, in hertz, of the spectrum. magnitude returns the magnitude, in decibels, of the spectrum. The decibel reference is 1 Ampere or 1 Volt if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is demodulated. The decibel reference is the fundamental component magnitude value if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is not demodulated. |
|  | f0 returns the start frequency, in hertz, of the spectrum. |
|  | df returns the frequency resolution, in hertz, of the spectrum. |
|  | magnitude returns the magnitude, in decibels, of the spectrum. The decibel reference is 1 Ampere or 1 Volt if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is demodulated. The decibel reference is the fundamental component magnitude value if the spectrum of sideband magnitude is not demodulated. |
|  | demodulated? returns whether spectrum is demodulated. The output returns TRUE if you specify enable? to be TRUE. |
|  | final conditions returns the final internal VI conditions after the internal calculation of this VI. Use final conditions as initial conditions of the subsequent iteration except that you want to customize the conditions according to your needs. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

MCSA Spectra Details

Because the band of **waveforms** that the VI analyzes is between 0 hertz and **maximum frequency**, set the maximum frequency value large enough to cover all frequencies for analysis according to your needs. For example, when you detect broken rotor bars, ensure that you specify the **maximum frequency** input of this VI using the following formula for the spectrum that is not demodulated:

Maximum Frequency > Fundamental Frequency + 2 · Harmonic Order · Slip · Fundamental Frequency

| where Maximum Frequency is maximum frequency to specify in this VI, |
| --- |
| Fundamental Frequency is the value of the frequency output or the frequencies output of the Frequency VI, |
| Slip is the value of the slip output of the Speed Estimation VI, |
| Harmonic Order is the highest order of the rotor bar sideband. |

Ensure that you specify the **maximum frequency** input of this VI using the following formula for the demodulated spectrum:

Maximum Frequency > 2 · Harmonic Order · Slip · Fundamental Frequency

| where Maximum Frequency is maximum frequency to specify in this VI, |
| --- |
| Fundamental Frequency is the value of the frequency output or the frequencies output of the Frequency VI, |
| Slip data is the value of the slip output of the Speed Estimation VI, |
| Harmonic Order is the highest order of the rotor bar sideband. |

**Related Information**
Delta Connections
Power Transmission


Example

Refer to the Motor Monitoring.lvproj in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Motor Monitoring directory for an example of using the MCSA Spectra VI.

Parent topic:

Motor VIs

## TOPIC 00089: Mechanical Power VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`

Details Examples motor speed specifies the angular speed of the motor in radians per second. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, b

### Mechanical Power VI

**Owning Palette:** Efficiency VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the output mechanical power of a motor.

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Mechanical_Power.gif']

|  | motor speed specifies the angular speed of the motor in radians per second. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | motor torque specifies the torque of the motor in Newton meters. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | mechanical power returns the mechanical power of the motor in watts. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Mechanical Power Details

This VI uses the following equation to calculate the output mechanical power of a motor:

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/eq_mechanical_power.gif']

where

τ represents the torque of the motor in Newton meters

ω represents the angular speed of the motor in radians per second


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Mechanical Power VI:

- Efficiency VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Efficiency
- Traction Inverter and Electric Motor Efficiency Measurement (TDMS) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Traction Inverter

Use the Mechanical Power VI to calculate the mechanical power of the motor. Details Examples mechanical power specifies the mechanical power of the motor in watts. power value specifies the

### Motor Efficiency VI

**Owning Palette:** Efficiency VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the power efficiency of a motor.

Use the Mechanical Power VI to calculate the mechanical power of the motor.

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Motor_Efficiency.gif']

|  | mechanical power specifies the mechanical power of the motor in watts. |
| --- | --- |
|  | power value specifies the active power, effective apparent power, non-active power, and power factor. This VI uses only the active power to calculate the power efficiency of the motor. Refer to the Power VI for more information about the power value. active power specifies the active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. effective apparent power specifies the effective apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. non-active power specifies the non-active power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. power factor specifies the power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. |
|  | active power specifies the active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. |
|  | effective apparent power specifies the effective apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. |
|  | non-active power specifies the non-active power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. |
|  | power factor specifies the power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | motor efficiency returns the power efficiency of the motor. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Motor Efficiency Details

This VI uses the following equation to calculate the motor efficiency:

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/eq_motor_efficiency.gif']


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Motor Efficiency VI:

- Efficiency VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Efficiency
- Traction Inverter and Electric Motor Efficiency Measurement (TDMS) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Traction Inverter

## TOPIC 00091: Motor VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `motor-vis.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/motor-vis.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Electrical Power VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use the Motor VIs to conduct condition monitoring on AC induction motors, including performing

### Motor VIs

**Owning Palette:** Electrical Power VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the Motor VIs to conduct condition monitoring on AC induction motors, including performing the motor current signature analysis (MCSA).

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Broken Rotor Bar Harmonics | Calculates the sideband harmonics to detect broken rotor bars. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. |
| MCSA Spectra | Calculates the zoom fast Fourier Transform (FFT) spectra of the motor. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. |
| NEMA Unbalance | Calculates the unbalance in compliance with NEMA MG 1-2014. |
| Speed Estimation | Calculates the motor speed. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. |
| Torque, Load, and Efficiency | Calculates the torque, load, and efficiency values based on the motor nameplate information. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. |

Parent topic:

Electrical Power VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=nema-unbalance-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00092: NEMA Unbalance VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `nema-unbalance-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/nema-unbalance-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Motor VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the unbalance in compliance with NEMA MG 1-2014. Details Example voltage RMS values specifies the three-phase voltage RMS. The array size must be a multiple of three. If you have multiple motors, ensure that you specify the thre

### NEMA Unbalance VI

**Owning Palette:** Motor VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the unbalance in compliance with NEMA MG 1-2014.

Details Example

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/NEMA_Unbalance.gif']

|  | voltage RMS values specifies the three-phase voltage RMS. The array size must be a multiple of three. If you have multiple motors, ensure that you specify the three-phase voltage RMS motor by motor. National Instruments recommends using the line-to-line voltage RMS in delta connections to calculate unbalance. |
| --- | --- |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | unbalance returns the unbalance, as a percentage, of voltage RMS values. Each element corresponds to the results of the three phases for each motor. |
|  | unbalance derating factors returns the value to derate the motor load for unbalance calculation. Each array element corresponds to the results of one three-phase motor. NEMA MG 1-2014 does not define how to derate the motor load when the unbalance is greater than 5%. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

NEMA Unbalance Details

**Related Information**Delta Connections
Measuring UnbalanceWiring


Example

Refer to the Motor Monitoring.lvproj in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Motor Monitoring directory for an example of using the NEMA Unbalance VI.

Parent topic:

Motor VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=ni-ep-frequency-domain-power-analysis-lvlib-h.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00093: NI_EP_Frequency Domain Power Analysis.lvlib:Harmonic Analysis VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `ni-ep-frequency-domain-power-analysis-lvlib-h.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/ni-ep-frequency-domain-power-analysis-lvlib-h.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Measures the DC, fundamental, and harmonic components of an input waveform that contains an integer number of cycles of the fundamental frequency component. The fundamental frequency is used to calculate harmonic component levels and phases. Harmonic components may indicate design and production cha

### NI_EP_Frequency Domain Power Analysis.lvlib:Harmonic Analysis VI

Measures the DC, fundamental, and harmonic components of an input waveform that contains an integer number of cycles of the fundamental frequency component. The fundamental frequency is used to calculate harmonic component levels and phases. Harmonic components may indicate design and production characteristics that lead to decreased power conversion efficiency.

Wire data to the **waveform** and **electrical frequency** or **electrical angle** inputs to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Harmonic Analysis (1 Ch)

Measures the DC, fundamental, and harmonic components of an input waveform that contains an integer number of cycles of the fundamental frequency component. The fundamental frequency is used to calculate harmonic component levels and phases. Harmonic components may indicate design and production characteristics that lead to decreased power conversion efficiency.

Wire data to the **waveform** and **electrical frequency** inputs to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Harmonic_Analysis_(1_Ch).gif']

|  | waveform specifies the measured electrical or vibration signal. This signal should contain one or more integer cycles of the fundamental component. t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt specifies the sample interval. Y specifies the measured values. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt specifies the sample interval. |
|  | Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | maximum harmonic specifies the maximum harmonic, or highest multiple of the fundamental to be measured. Specify a value of -1 to measure all harmonics up to the Nyquist frequency. The default maximum harmonic is 20. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | electrical frequency specifies the expected electrical speed of the system in Hz. |
|  | harmonic levels returns all the harmonics between DC and input maximum harmonic. harmonics returns the multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. rms levels returns the measured level of the corresponding harmonic component. The base unit of level is the same as that of the input waveform. |
|  | harmonics returns the multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. |
|  | rms levels returns the measured level of the corresponding harmonic component. The base unit of level is the same as that of the input waveform. |
|  | harmonic phase lags returns the measured phase lags for all harmonics between DC and input maximum harmonic. harmonics returns a multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. phase lags returns the measured phase lag, expressed in radians, of the corresponding harmonic component relative to the timestamp of the input waveform. |
|  | harmonics returns a multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. |
|  | phase lags returns the measured phase lag, expressed in radians, of the corresponding harmonic component relative to the timestamp of the input waveform. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Harmonic Analysis (N Ch)

Measures the DC, fundamental, and harmonic components of an input waveform that contains an integer number of cycles of the fundamental frequency component. The fundamental frequency is used to calculate harmonic component levels and phases. Harmonic components may indicate design and production characteristics that lead to decreased power conversion efficiency.

Wire data to the **waveforms** and **electrical frequency** inputs to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Harmonic_Analysis_(N_Ch).gif']

|  | waveforms specifies the measured electrical or vibration signal. This signal should contain one or more integer cycles of the fundamental component. t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt specifies the sample interval. Y specifies the measured values. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt specifies the sample interval. |
|  | Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | maximum harmonic specifies the maximum harmonic, or highest multiple of the fundamental to be measured. Specify a value of -1 to measure all harmonics up to the Nyquist frequency. The default maximum harmonic is 20. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | electrical frequency specifies the expected electrical speed of the system in Hz. |
|  | harmonic levels returns all the harmonics between DC and input maximum harmonic. harmonics returns the multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. rms levels returns the measured level of the corresponding harmonic component. The base unit of level is the same as that of the input waveform. |
|  | harmonics returns the multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. |
|  | rms levels returns the measured level of the corresponding harmonic component. The base unit of level is the same as that of the input waveform. |
|  | harmonic phase lags returns the measured phase lags for all harmonics between DC and input maximum harmonic. harmonics returns a multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. phase lags returns the measured phase lag, expressed in radians, of the corresponding harmonic component relative to the timestamp of the input waveform. |
|  | harmonics returns a multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. |
|  | phase lags returns the measured phase lag, expressed in radians, of the corresponding harmonic component relative to the timestamp of the input waveform. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Harmonic Analysis with Phase Reference (1 Ch)

Measures the DC, fundamental, and harmonic components of an input waveform that contains an integer number of cycles of the fundamental frequency component. The fundamental frequency is used to calculate harmonic component levels and phases. Harmonic components may indicate design and production characteristics that lead to decreased power conversion efficiency.

Wire data to the **waveform** and **electrical angle** inputs to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Harmonic_Analysis_with_Phase_Reference_(1_Ch).gif']

|  | waveform specifies the measured electrical or vibration signal. This signal should contain one or more integer cycles of the fundamental component. t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt specifies the sample interval. Y specifies the measured values. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt specifies the sample interval. |
|  | Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | electrical angle specifies the phase, in radians, of the electrical system as measured by a quadrature encoder or resolver, or as calculated from the mechanical angle. This phase reference is synchronous to the fundamental component of the electrical signals and is used to calculate harmonic levels and harmonic phase lags. t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt specifies the sample interval. Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt specifies the sample interval. |
|  | Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | maximum harmonic specifies the maximum harmonic, or highest multiple of the fundamental to be measured. Specify a value of -1 to measure all harmonics up to the Nyquist frequency. The default maximum harmonic is 20. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | harmonic levels returns all the harmonics between DC and input maximum harmonic. harmonics returns the multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. rms levels returns the measured level of the corresponding harmonic component. The base unit of level is the same as that of the input waveform. |
|  | harmonics returns the multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. |
|  | rms levels returns the measured level of the corresponding harmonic component. The base unit of level is the same as that of the input waveform. |
|  | harmonic phase lags returns the measured phase lags for all harmonics between DC and input maximum harmonic. harmonics returns a multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. phase lags returns the measured phase lag, expressed in radians, of the corresponding harmonic component relative to the timestamp of the input waveform. |
|  | harmonics returns a multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. |
|  | phase lags returns the measured phase lag, expressed in radians, of the corresponding harmonic component relative to the timestamp of the input waveform. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Harmonic Analysis with Phase Reference (N Ch)

Measures the DC, fundamental, and harmonic components of an input waveform that contains an integer number of cycles of the fundamental frequency component. The fundamental frequency is used to calculate harmonic component levels and phases. Harmonic components may indicate design and production characteristics that lead to decreased power conversion efficiency.

Wire data to the **waveforms** and **electrical angle** inputs to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Harmonic_Analysis_with_Phase_Reference_(N_Ch).gif']

|  | waveforms specifies the measured electrical or vibration signal. This signal should contain one or more integer cycles of the fundamental component. t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt specifies the sample interval. Y specifies the measured values. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt specifies the sample interval. |
|  | Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | electrical angle specifies the phase, in radians, of the electrical system as measured by a quadrature encoder or resolver, or as calculated from the mechanical angle. This phase reference is synchronous to the fundamental component of the electrical signals and is used to calculate harmonic levels and harmonic phase lags. t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt specifies the sample interval. Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt specifies the sample interval. |
|  | Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | maximum harmonic specifies the maximum harmonic, or highest multiple of the fundamental to be measured. Specify a value of -1 to measure all harmonics up to the Nyquist frequency. The default maximum harmonic is 20. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | harmonic levels returns all the harmonics between DC and input maximum harmonic. harmonics returns the multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. rms levels returns the measured level of the corresponding harmonic component. The base unit of level is the same as that of the input waveform. |

|  | rms levels returns the measured level of the corresponding harmonic component. The base unit of level is the same as that of the input waveform. |
|  | harmonic phase lags returns the measured phase lags for all harmonics between DC and input maximum harmonic. harmonics returns a multiple of the fundamental frequency. 0.0 corresponds to DC, 1.0 corresponds to the fundamental frequency (f), 2.0 corresponds to 2*f, and so forth. phase lags returns the measured phase lag, expressed in radians, of the corresponding harmonic component relative to the timestamp of the input waveform. |

## TOPIC 00094: Obtain (SGL) VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `obtain-sgl-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/obtain-sgl-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Obtains a buffer that can be used to connect the data source with multiple data processing elements. Examples number of channels specifies the number of channels that are preallocated in a circular buffer. max samples per channel specifies the number of samples per channel that are preallocated in a

### Obtain (SGL) VI

Obtains a buffer that can be used to connect the data source with multiple data processing elements.

Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Obtain_(SGL).gif']

|  | number of channels specifies the number of channels that are preallocated in a circular buffer. |
| --- | --- |
|  | max samples per channel specifies the number of samples per channel that are preallocated in a circular buffer. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | circular buffer returns the new, created data value reference to the circular buffer. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Obtain (SGL) VI.

- Traction Inverter Acquisition and Waveform Logging (DAQmx) VI: LabVIEW\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Traction Inverter
- Traction Inverter Power and Efficiency Measurement and Logging (DAQmx) VI: LabVIEW\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Traction Inverter

Parent topic:

Utility VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=parse-command-frame-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00095: Parse Command Frame VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `parse-command-frame-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/parse-command-frame-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: C37.118 Frame VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Parses command frames from a phasor data concentrator (PDC) as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. Examples command frame specifies the command frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. error in describes error conditions that o

### Parse Command Frame VI

**Owning Palette:** C37.118 Frame VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Parses command frames from a phasor data concentrator (PDC) as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011.

Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Parse_Command_Frame.gif']

|  | command frame specifies the command frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |
| --- | --- |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | ID code returns the ID code of the command sender. |
|  | command returns the command that this VI parses from the command frame. Phasor measurement units (PMUs) read and execute the command. |
|  | extended frame data returns the extended frame data from the PDC. |
|  | frame valid? returns TRUE if the received command is valid. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Parse Command Frame VI:

- Synchrophasor Measurement and Communication (TCP) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Synchrophasor
- Synchrophasor Measurement and Communication (UDP) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Synchrophasor

Parent topic:

C37.118 Frame VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=phasor-correction-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00096: Phasor Correction VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `phasor-correction-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/phasor-correction-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Synchrophasor VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Scales the magnitudes of phasors based on the sensitivity of the voltage/current transformer or transducer and compensates the timestamps based on the delays that the data acquisition module, the voltage/current transformer, or the

### Phasor Correction VI

**Owning Palette:** Synchrophasor VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Scales the magnitudes of phasors based on the sensitivity of the voltage/current transformer or transducer and compensates the timestamps based on the delays that the data acquisition module, the voltage/current transformer, or the voltage/current transducer introduces.

Example

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Scaling_PMU.gif']

|  | magnitudes in specifies the fundamental magnitudes, in RMS value, of each sample in the current data block of all channels. |
| --- | --- |
|  | timestamps in specifies the timestamp, in seconds, of each sample in the current data block of all channels. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the phasor measurement unit (PMU) system. Use the Initialize System (Std 2011) VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | magnitudes out returns the scaled fundamental magnitudes, in RMS value, of each sample in the current data block of all channels. |
|  | timestamps out returns the compensated timestamp, in seconds, of each sample in the current data block of all channels. |
|  | system info out returns the basic configuration of the PMU system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Example

Refer to the Synchrophasor Measurement.lvproj in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Synchrophasor directory for an example of using the Phasor Correction VI.

Parent topic:

Synchrophasor VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=phasor-measurement-std-2011-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00097: Phasor Measurement (Std 2011) VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `phasor-measurement-std-2011-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/phasor-measurement-std-2011-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Synchrophasor VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the phasors, the frequency, and the rate of change of frequency at the reporting timestamp as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.1-2011. This VI also returns the reported timestamp and other timing information for the fields in

### Phasor Measurement (Std 2011) VI

**Owning Palette:** Synchrophasor VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the phasors, the frequency, and the rate of change of frequency at the reporting timestamp as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.1-2011.

This VI also returns the reported timestamp and other timing information for the fields in data frame, configuration frame, header frame, and command frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011.

Example

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Phasor_Measurement__Std_2011.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | frequency configuration specifies the reference channel and correction parameters for frequency calculation. channel index specifies the index of the reference channel for frequency calculation. nominal Fs/actual Fs specifies the ratio of the nominal sample rate to the actual sample rate. This VI uses nominal Fs/actual Fs as the correction parameters for the measured frequency values. |
|  | channel index specifies the index of the reference channel for frequency calculation. |
|  | nominal Fs/actual Fs specifies the ratio of the nominal sample rate to the actual sample rate. This VI uses nominal Fs/actual Fs as the correction parameters for the measured frequency values. |
|  | data in specifies the data block for calculating the phasors, frequency, and rate of change of frequency. The input rate for data in must be 24 samples per cycle for phasor measurement of M class of performance. The input rate for data in must be 25 samples per cycle for phasor measurement of P class of performance. magnitudes specifies the fundamental magnitudes in the current data block of all channels. phases specifies the fundamental phases in the current data block of all channels. timestamps specifies the timestamps of each sample in the current data block of all channels. The timestamp is the number of seconds since the International Atomic Time (TAI) epoch of 00:00:00, January 1, 1970, UTC time. The timestamp is not adjusted for leap seconds. frequencies specifies the frequencies in the current data block of all channels. |
|  | magnitudes specifies the fundamental magnitudes in the current data block of all channels. |
|  | phases specifies the fundamental phases in the current data block of all channels. |
|  | timestamps specifies the timestamps of each sample in the current data block of all channels. The timestamp is the number of seconds since the International Atomic Time (TAI) epoch of 00:00:00, January 1, 1970, UTC time. The timestamp is not adjusted for leap seconds. |
|  | frequencies specifies the frequencies in the current data block of all channels. |
|  | synchronization error specifies the real-time synchronization timing error, in seconds, that the phasor measurement unit (PMU) timing source determines. The default is 0.0000001 second. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the PMU system. Use the Initialize System (Std 2011) VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | GPS information specifies the GPS timing information of each sample in the current data block. unlocked seconds specifies the time since the GPS signal was unlocked. unlocked seconds corresponds to Bits 5-4 of the STAT field in the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. You can use unlocked seconds to set Bits 5-4 of the STAT field as follows. 00Locked or unlocked less than 10 s 01Unlocked 10 s or longer but less than 100 s 10Unlocked 100 s or longer but less than 1000 s 11Unlocked 1000 s or more leap seconds specifies the leap seconds between the UTC time scale and the time scale of the timestamps in data in. locked? specifies whether the GPS signal is locked. The default is FALSE. leap second pending? specifies whether a leap second is pending. The default is FALSE. leap second pending? must remain TRUE for greater than or equal to 1 second and less than or equal to 60 seconds before a leap second occurs and must turn to FALSE in the first second after the leap second occurs as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. leap second occurred? specifies whether a leap second occurs. The default is FALSE. leap second occurred? must turn to TRUE in the first second after a leap second occurs and the TRUE status must remain for 24 hours as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. leap second direction? specifies whether to add or delete the leap second. The default is FALSE. leap second direction? turns to FALSE to add the leap second or turns to TRUE to delete the leap second as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |
|  | unlocked seconds specifies the time since the GPS signal was unlocked. unlocked seconds corresponds to Bits 5-4 of the STAT field in the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. You can use unlocked seconds to set Bits 5-4 of the STAT field as follows. 00Locked or unlocked less than 10 s 01Unlocked 10 s or longer but less than 100 s 10Unlocked 100 s or longer but less than 1000 s 11Unlocked 1000 s or more |
|  | leap seconds specifies the leap seconds between the UTC time scale and the time scale of the timestamps in data in. |
|  | locked? specifies whether the GPS signal is locked. The default is FALSE. |
|  | leap second pending? specifies whether a leap second is pending. The default is FALSE. leap second pending? must remain TRUE for greater than or equal to 1 second and less than or equal to 60 seconds before a leap second occurs and must turn to FALSE in the first second after the leap second occurs as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |
|  | leap second occurred? specifies whether a leap second occurs. The default is FALSE. leap second occurred? must turn to TRUE in the first second after a leap second occurs and the TRUE status must remain for 24 hours as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |
|  | leap second direction? specifies whether to add or delete the leap second. The default is FALSE. leap second direction? turns to FALSE to add the leap second or turns to TRUE to delete the leap second as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |
|  | samples to read returns the number of samples to read per channel from the DMA FIFOs. |
|  | reporting data returns the phasor-related values from the phasor channels at the reporting timestamp. phasors returns the phasors of the phasor channels at the reporting timestamp as complex numbers. frequency returns the frequency at the reporting timestamp. df/dt returns the rate of change of frequency at the reporting timestamp. |
|  | phasors returns the phasors of the phasor channels at the reporting timestamp as complex numbers. |
|  | frequency returns the frequency at the reporting timestamp. |
|  | df/dt returns the rate of change of frequency at the reporting timestamp. |
|  | reporting timing information returns the timing information at the reporting timestamp for the field in data frame, configuration frame, header frame, and command frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. timestamp returns the reporting timestamp in UTC time scale that is adjusted for leap seconds. timestamp returns the current time if the GPS has not been locked. time quality flag returns the quality of the time being reported and the leap second status. time quality flag corresponds to Bits 31-24 of the FRACSEC field in the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. PMU time quality returns the maximum time uncertainty that the timing source and the PMU measuring processing introduce. PMU time quality corresponds to Bits 8-6 of the STAT field in the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. unlocked seconds returns the time since the GPS signal was unlocked. unlocked seconds corresponds to Bits 5-4 of the STAT field in the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |
|  | timestamp returns the reporting timestamp in UTC time scale that is adjusted for leap seconds. timestamp returns the current time if the GPS has not been locked. |
|  | time quality flag returns the quality of the time being reported and the leap second status. time quality flag corresponds to Bits 31-24 of the FRACSEC field in the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |
|  | PMU time quality returns the maximum time uncertainty that the timing source and the PMU measuring processing introduce. PMU time quality corresponds to Bits 8-6 of the STAT field in the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |
|  | unlocked seconds returns the time since the GPS signal was unlocked. unlocked seconds corresponds to Bits 5-4 of the STAT field in the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |
|  | system info out returns the basic configuration of the PMU system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |
|  | physical phasors returns the phasors of the physical channels at the reporting timestamp as complex numbers. |

Example

Refer to the Synchrophasor Measurement.lvproj in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Synchrophasor directory for an example of using the Phasor Measurement (Std 2011) VI.

Parent topic:

Synchrophasor VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=power-components-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00098: Power Components VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `power-components-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/power-components-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Three Phase Power Measurement VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the power components for nonsinusoidal signals as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. Wire data to the voltage waveform input or the current waveform inputs to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manu

### Power Components VI

**Owning Palette:** Three Phase Power Measurement VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the power components for nonsinusoidal signals as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. Wire data to the **voltage waveform** input or the **current waveform** inputs to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

Example

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Note

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Power_Components__1-Phase_1_Ch.gif']

|  | voltage waveform specifies the voltage signal in volts. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | current waveform specifies the current signal in amperes. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | electrical frequency specifies the expected electrical speed of the system in hertz. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the electrical frequency: |
|  | combined powers returns the combined powers for nonsinusoidal, single-phase signals as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. active power returns the active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the active power: where T represents the cycle time in seconds k represents a positive integer number τ represents the moment when the measurement starts Pa represents the instantaneous active power in watts apparent power returns the apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the apparent power: where V represents the RMS value of the voltage in volts I represents the RMS value of the current in amperes non-active power returns the non-active power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the non-active power: where S represents the apparent power in volt-amperes P represents the active power in watts power factor returns the power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the power factor: where P represents the active power in watts S represents the apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | active power returns the active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the active power: where T represents the cycle time in seconds k represents a positive integer number τ represents the moment when the measurement starts Pa represents the instantaneous active power in watts |

|  | line voltage waveforms specifies the voltage signals, in volts, measured between two lines. Vab specifies the voltage signal, in volts, between line a and line b. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. Vbc specifies the voltage signal, in volts, between line b and line c. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. Vca specifies the voltage signal, in volts, between line c and line a. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |

|  | phase voltage waveforms specifies the voltage signals, in volts, measured between any phase and neutral. Van specifies the voltage signal, in volts, between phase a and neutral. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. Vbn specifies the voltage signal, in volts, between phase b and neutral. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. Vcn specifies the voltage signal, in volts, between phase c and neutral. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
