|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | In specifies the current signal through the neutral line n in amperes. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | electrical frequency specifies the expected electrical speed of the system in hertz. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the electrical frequency: |
|  | combined powers returns the combined powers for nonsinusoidal, three-phase, four-wire signals as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. active power returns the active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the active power: where T represents the cycle time in seconds k represents a positive integer number τ represents the moment when the measurement starts Pa represents the instantaneous active power in watts effective apparent power returns the effective apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate effective the apparent power: where where Van, Vbn, and Vcn represent the RMS values of the phase voltage in volts Vab, Vbc, and Vca represent the RMS values of the line voltage in volts where Ia, Ib, Ic, and In represent the RMS values of the line current in amperes non-active power returns the non-active power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the non-active power: where Se represents the effective apparent power in volt-amperes P represents the active power in watts power factor returns the power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the power factor: where P represents the active power in watts Se represents the effective apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | active power returns the active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the active power: where T represents the cycle time in seconds k represents a positive integer number τ represents the moment when the measurement starts Pa represents the instantaneous active power in watts |
|  | effective apparent power returns the effective apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate effective the apparent power: where where Van, Vbn, and Vcn represent the RMS values of the phase voltage in volts Vab, Vbc, and Vca represent the RMS values of the line voltage in volts where Ia, Ib, Ic, and In represent the RMS values of the line current in amperes |
|  | non-active power returns the non-active power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the non-active power: where Se represents the effective apparent power in volt-amperes P represents the active power in watts |
|  | power factor returns the power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the power factor: where P represents the active power in watts Se represents the effective apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | fundamental powers returns the values of fundamental powers for nonsinusoidal, three-phase, four-wire signals as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. active power P1 returns the fundamental active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental active power: where T represents the cycle time in seconds k represents a positive integer number τ represents the moment when the measurement starts Pa represents the instantaneous active power in watts reactive power Q1 returns the fundamental reactive power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental reactive power: where Ve1 represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the voltage signal in volts Ie1 represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the current signal in amperes θ1 represents the phase angle between the fundamental component of the current and the fundamental component of the voltage in radians apparent power Se1 returns the nonfundamental apparent power. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental apparent power: where Ve1 represents the fundamental component of the effective apparent voltage in volts. Ie1 represents the fundamental component of the effective apparent current in amperes. power factor PF1 = P1/S1 returns the reactive power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the power factor: where P1 represents the fundamental active power in watts Se1 represents the fundamental effective apparent power in volt-amperes active power P1+ returns the fundamental positive-sequence active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental positive-sequence active power: where V1+ represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the positive-sequence voltage signal in volts I1+ represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the positive-sequence current signal in amperes θ1+ represents the phase angle between the fundamental component of the positive-sequence current and the fundamental component of the positive-sequence voltage in radians reactive power Q1+ returns the fundamental positive-sequence reactive power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental positive-sequence reactive power: where V1+ represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the positive-sequence voltage signal in volts I1+ represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the positive-sequence current signal in amperes θ1+ represents the phase angle between the fundamental component of the positive-sequence current and the fundamental component of the positive-sequence voltage in radians apparent power S1+ returns the fundamental positive-sequence apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental positive-sequence apparent power: where P1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence active power in watts Q1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence effective power in volt-amperes power factor PF1+ = P1/S1+ returns the fundamental positive-sequence reactive power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the power factor: where P1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence active power in watts S1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence apparent power in volt-amperes unbalanced S1U returns the fundamental unbalanced power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental unbalanced power: where Se1 represents the fundamental effective apparent power in volt-amperes S1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence apparent power in volt-amperes load unbalance S1U/S1+ returns the fundamental power load unbalance. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the load unbalance: where S1U represents the fundamental unbalanced power in volt-amperes S1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | active power P1 returns the fundamental active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental active power: where T represents the cycle time in seconds k represents a positive integer number τ represents the moment when the measurement starts Pa represents the instantaneous active power in watts |
|  | reactive power Q1 returns the fundamental reactive power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental reactive power: where Ve1 represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the voltage signal in volts Ie1 represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the current signal in amperes θ1 represents the phase angle between the fundamental component of the current and the fundamental component of the voltage in radians |
|  | apparent power Se1 returns the nonfundamental apparent power. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental apparent power: where Ve1 represents the fundamental component of the effective apparent voltage in volts. Ie1 represents the fundamental component of the effective apparent current in amperes. |
|  | power factor PF1 = P1/S1 returns the reactive power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the power factor: where P1 represents the fundamental active power in watts Se1 represents the fundamental effective apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | active power P1+ returns the fundamental positive-sequence active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental positive-sequence active power: where V1+ represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the positive-sequence voltage signal in volts I1+ represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the positive-sequence current signal in amperes θ1+ represents the phase angle between the fundamental component of the positive-sequence current and the fundamental component of the positive-sequence voltage in radians |
|  | reactive power Q1+ returns the fundamental positive-sequence reactive power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental positive-sequence reactive power: where V1+ represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the positive-sequence voltage signal in volts I1+ represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the positive-sequence current signal in amperes θ1+ represents the phase angle between the fundamental component of the positive-sequence current and the fundamental component of the positive-sequence voltage in radians |
|  | apparent power S1+ returns the fundamental positive-sequence apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental positive-sequence apparent power: where P1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence active power in watts Q1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence effective power in volt-amperes |
|  | power factor PF1+ = P1/S1+ returns the fundamental positive-sequence reactive power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the power factor: where P1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence active power in watts S1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | unbalanced S1U returns the fundamental unbalanced power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the fundamental unbalanced power: where Se1 represents the fundamental effective apparent power in volt-amperes S1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | load unbalance S1U/S1+ returns the fundamental power load unbalance. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the load unbalance: where S1U represents the fundamental unbalanced power in volt-amperes S1+ represents the fundamental positive-sequence apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | nonfundamental powers returns the values of nonfundamental powers for nonsinusoidal, three-phase, four-wire signals as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. active harmonic power P_H returns the nonfundamental active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental active power: where P represents the active power in watts P1 represents the fundamental active power in watts apparent harmonic power Se_H returns the nonfundamental apparent harmonic power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental apparent harmonic power: where VeH represents the RMS value of the nonfundamental component of the voltage signal in volts IeH represents the RMS value of the nonfundamental component of the current signal in amperes apparent power Se_N returns the nonfundamental effective apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental effective apparent power: where DeI represents the current distortion power in volt-amperes reactive DeV represents the voltage distortion power in volt-amperes reactive SeH represents the harmonic apparent power in volt-amperes reactive current distortion power De_I returns the nonfundamental current distortion power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental current distortion power: where Ve1 represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the voltage signal in volts IeH represents the RMS value of the nonfundamental component of the current signal in amperes voltage distortion power De_V returns the nonfundamental voltage distortion power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental voltage distortion power: where VeH represents the RMS value of the nonfundamental component of the voltage signal in volts Ie1 represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the current signal in amperes harmonic distortion power De_H returns the nonfundamental harmonic distortion power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental harmonic distortion power: where SeH represents the nonfundamental effective apparent power in volt-amperes PeH represents the nonfundamental effective active power in watts harmonic pollution Se_N/Se1 returns the nonfundamental harmonic pollution as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental harmonic pollution: where SeN represents the nonfundamental apparent power in volt-amperes Se1 represents the fundamental effective apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | active harmonic power P_H returns the nonfundamental active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental active power: where P represents the active power in watts P1 represents the fundamental active power in watts |
|  | apparent harmonic power Se_H returns the nonfundamental apparent harmonic power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental apparent harmonic power: where VeH represents the RMS value of the nonfundamental component of the voltage signal in volts IeH represents the RMS value of the nonfundamental component of the current signal in amperes |
|  | apparent power Se_N returns the nonfundamental effective apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental effective apparent power: where DeI represents the current distortion power in volt-amperes reactive DeV represents the voltage distortion power in volt-amperes reactive SeH represents the harmonic apparent power in volt-amperes reactive |
|  | current distortion power De_I returns the nonfundamental current distortion power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental current distortion power: where Ve1 represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the voltage signal in volts IeH represents the RMS value of the nonfundamental component of the current signal in amperes |
|  | voltage distortion power De_V returns the nonfundamental voltage distortion power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental voltage distortion power: where VeH represents the RMS value of the nonfundamental component of the voltage signal in volts Ie1 represents the RMS value of the fundamental component of the current signal in amperes |
|  | harmonic distortion power De_H returns the nonfundamental harmonic distortion power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental harmonic distortion power: where SeH represents the nonfundamental effective apparent power in volt-amperes PeH represents the nonfundamental effective active power in watts |
|  | harmonic pollution Se_N/Se1 returns the nonfundamental harmonic pollution as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the nonfundamental harmonic pollution: where SeN represents the nonfundamental apparent power in volt-amperes Se1 represents the fundamental effective apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |
|  | detected frequency returns the frequency of the detected single sine tone in hertz. |

Example

Refer to the Frequency Domain Power Value of 3-Phase System VI in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Frequency Analysis directory for an example of using the Power Components VI.

Three Phase Power Measurement VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=power-energy-vis.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00099: Power & Energy VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `power-energy-vis.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/power-energy-vis.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Power Quality VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use the Power & Energy VIs to calculate power and energy values. Palette ObjectDescriptionEnergy

### Power & Energy VIs

**Owning Palette:** Power Quality VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the Power & Energy VIs to calculate power and energy values.

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Energy Values | Calculates energy values for a specified interval length. This VI returns apparent, active, and reactive energy values. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |
| Fundamental Power Values (1 Cycle) | Calculates the fundamental power values from the voltage and current spectra for a single-cycle block. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |
| Power Values | Calculates the power values from the voltage and current spectra of different line configurations. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. |

Parent topic:

Power Quality VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=power-measurement-vis.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00100: Power Measurement VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `power-measurement-vis.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/power-measurement-vis.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Electrical Power VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use the Power Measurement VIs to measure common electrical power parameters and to perform har

### Power Measurement VIs

**Owning Palette:** Electrical Power VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the Power Measurement VIs to measure common electrical power parameters and to perform harmonic analysis.

To measure common electrical power parameters using integral-cycle voltage and current signals and to perform harmonic analysis using resampled integral-cycle voltage and current signals, first use the Advanced VIs to preprocess the voltage and current signals.

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Energy | Calculates the energy value from the power value for a specified interval. This VI returns the value of active energy, apparent energy, and reactive energy. Wire data to the power value input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. |
| Frequency | Calculates the fundamental frequency of a voltage or current signal. Wire data to the waveform input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. |
| Fundamental Vector | Calculates the fundamental vector of a voltage or current signal. Wire data to the voltage waveform input or the current waveform input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. |
| Power | Calculates the power values from a voltage and current signal. Wire data to the voltage waveform input or the current waveform input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. |
| RMS | Calculates the RMS level of a voltage or current signal. Wire data to the waveform input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. |
| Symmetrical Components | Calculates the zero, positive, and negative sequence components of a three-phase electrical power system. |

| Subpalette | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Advanced VIs | Use the Advanced VIs to perform common electrical power measurement using integral-cycle voltage and current signals and to perform harmonic analysis using resampled integral-cycle voltage and current signals. |

Parent topic:

Electrical Power VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=power-quality-vis.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00101: Power Quality VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `power-quality-vis.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/power-quality-vis.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Electrical Power VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use the Power Quality VIs to perform power quality measurement in compliance with IEC 61000-4-

### Power Quality VIs

**Owning Palette:** Electrical Power VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the Power Quality VIs to perform power quality measurement in compliance with IEC 61000-4-7:2009, IEC 61000-4-15:2010, and IEC 61000-4-30:2008.

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Convert Data to Waveforms | Converts resampled voltage and current data to voltage and current waveforms of different line configurations. This VI also arranges the order of the elements in the waveforms of each three-phase electrical power system for further measurement. For example, the orders are A-B-C-N for the four-channel method and A-B-C for the three-channel method. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. |
| Initialize System | Sets the basic configuration of the electrical power system based on different line and bus configurations. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. |

| Subpalette | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Aggregation VIs | Use the Aggregation VIs to aggregate data at a specific interval according to Class S of IEC 61000-4-30:2008. |
| Events VIs | Use the Events VIs to detect voltage dips, swells, interruptions, and rapid change events as defined in IEC 61000-4-30:2008. |
| Flicker VIs | Use the Flicker VIs to measure instantaneous flicker sensation, short-term flicker severity, and long-term flicker severity as defined in IEC 61000-4-15:2010. |
| Harmonics VIs | Use the Harmonics VIs to perform harmonic analysis according to IEC 61000-4-7:2009 and IEC 61000-4-30:2008. |
| Power & Energy VIs | Use the Power & Energy VIs to calculate power and energy values. |
| Preprocessing VIs | Use the Preprocessing VIs to preprocess signals for further power quality analysis. |
| Scaling & Calibration VIs | Use the Scaling & Calibration VIs to scale a signal to the appropriate engineering units if the signal is from a transducer or to scale a secondary-side measured signal to primary-side signal if the signal is from a transformer. You also use these VIs to apply system calibration correction coefficients to the input signal before performing any power quality measurement. |
| Signaling VIs | Use the Signaling VIs to measure mains signaling voltage on the supply voltage as defined in IEC 61000-4-30:2008. |
| U, I & Freq VIs | Use the U, I & Freq VIs to calculate voltage, current, and frequency values for different electrical power data. |
| Unbalance VIs | Use the Unbalance VIs to calculate symmetrical components and analyze voltage and current unbalance as defined in IEC 61000-4-30:2008. |
| Utility VIs | Use the Utility VIs to perform connection conversion between Delta and Wye, perform AC/DC coupling, and set triggers on input data. |

Parent topic:

Electrical Power VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=power-values-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00102: Power Values VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `power-values-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/power-values-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Power & Energy VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the power values from the voltage and current spectra of different line configurations. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. You must use the D to Y Conversion VI to convert line voltages t

### Power Values VI

**Owning Palette:** Power & Energy VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Note

Details Examples

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Single Line

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Power_Values.gif']

|  | phase voltage spectra specifies 10/12-cycle complex voltage spectra, in volts, up to a harmonic order that you specify in maximum harmonic order in the Spectrum VI. The real part of the first vector represents the DC component of the input waveforms. Each element of this array corresponds to one channel. Each array element of phase voltage spectra and the corresponding array element of current spectra must be in the same phase of the electrical power system. f0 specifies the start frequency of the spectrum. df specifies the frequency resolution of the spectrum. magnitudes specifies the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. phases specifies the phase of the spectrum. channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. For line voltages, Voltage Phase A (L1), Voltage Phase B (L2), and Voltage Phase C (L3) equal Phase AB, Phase BC, and Phase CA respectively. |
| --- | --- |
|  | f0 specifies the start frequency of the spectrum. |
|  | df specifies the frequency resolution of the spectrum. |
|  | magnitudes specifies the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. |
|  | phases specifies the phase of the spectrum. |
|  | channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. For line voltages, Voltage Phase A (L1), Voltage Phase B (L2), and Voltage Phase C (L3) equal Phase AB, Phase BC, and Phase CA respectively. |
|  | current spectra specifies 10/12-cycle complex current spectra, in amperes, up to a harmonic order that you specify in maximum harmonic order in the Spectrum VI. The real part of the first vector represents the DC component of the input waveforms. Each element of this array corresponds to one channel. Each array element of current spectra and the corresponding array element of phase voltage spectra must be in the same phase of the electrical power system. f0 specifies the start frequency of the spectrum. df specifies the frequency resolution of the spectrum. magnitudes specifies the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. phases specifies the phase of the spectrum. channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. |
|  | f0 specifies the start frequency of the spectrum. |
|  | df specifies the frequency resolution of the spectrum. |
|  | magnitudes specifies the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. |
|  | phases specifies the phase of the spectrum. |
|  | channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the electrical power system. Use the Initialize System VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | power values returns power values that this VI calculates through a basic measurement time interval, which is typically 10 or 12 cycles. active power returns the active power value in watts. Each element of active power corresponds to one channel. reactive power returns the reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of reactive power corresponds to one channel. apparent power returns the apparent power value in volt-amperes. Each element of apparent power corresponds to one channel. fundamental active power returns the fundamental active power value in watts. Each element of fundamental active power corresponds to one channel. fundamental reactive power returns the fundamental reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of fundamental reactive power corresponds to one channel. cosine returns the fundamental power factor. Each element of cosine corresponds to one channel. power factor returns the power factor value. Each element of power factor corresponds to one channel. |
|  | active power returns the active power value in watts. Each element of active power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | reactive power returns the reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of reactive power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | apparent power returns the apparent power value in volt-amperes. Each element of apparent power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | fundamental active power returns the fundamental active power value in watts. Each element of fundamental active power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | fundamental reactive power returns the fundamental reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of fundamental reactive power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | cosine returns the fundamental power factor. Each element of cosine corresponds to one channel. |
|  | power factor returns the power factor value. Each element of power factor corresponds to one channel. |
|  | total power values returns total power values. active power returns the total active power value in watts. reactive power returns the total reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. apparent power returns the total apparent power value in volt-amperes. fundamental active power returns the total fundamental active power value in watts. fundamental reactive power returns the total fundamental reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. power factor returns the total power factor. |
|  | active power returns the total active power value in watts. |
|  | reactive power returns the total reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. |
|  | apparent power returns the total apparent power value in volt-amperes. |
|  | fundamental active power returns the total fundamental active power value in watts. |
|  | fundamental reactive power returns the total fundamental reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. |
|  | power factor returns the total power factor. |
|  | system info out returns information about the basic configuration of the electrical power system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Multiple Lines

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Power_Values_Multiple_Lines.gif']

|  | phase voltage spectra specifies 10/12-cycle complex voltage spectra, in volts, one three-phase system after another. The spectra are up to a harmonic order that you specify in maximum harmonic order in the Spectrum VI. The real part of the first vector represents the DC component of the input waveforms. Each element of this array corresponds to one channel. Each array element of phase voltage spectra and the corresponding array element of current spectra must be in the same phase of the electrical power system. For 3-phase power values calculation, ensure that you specify Phase A (L1), Phase B (L2), and Phase C (L3). f0 specifies the start frequency of the spectrum. df specifies the frequency resolution of the spectrum. magnitudes specifies the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. phases specifies the phase of the spectrum. channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. For line voltages, Voltage Phase A (L1), Voltage Phase B (L2), and Voltage Phase C (L3) equal Phase AB, Phase BC, and Phase CA respectively. |
| --- | --- |
|  | f0 specifies the start frequency of the spectrum. |
|  | df specifies the frequency resolution of the spectrum. |
|  | magnitudes specifies the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. |
|  | phases specifies the phase of the spectrum. |
|  | channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. For line voltages, Voltage Phase A (L1), Voltage Phase B (L2), and Voltage Phase C (L3) equal Phase AB, Phase BC, and Phase CA respectively. |
|  | current spectra specifies 10/12-cycle complex current spectra, in amperes, one three-phase system after another. The spectra are up to a harmonic order that you specify in maximum harmonic order in the Spectrum VI. The real part of the first vector represents the DC component of the input waveforms. Each element of this array corresponds to one channel. Each array element of current spectra and the corresponding array element of phase voltage spectra must be in the same phase of the electrical power system. For 3-phase power values calculation, ensure that you specify Phase A (L1), Phase B (L2), and Phase C (L3). f0 specifies the start frequency of the spectrum. df specifies the frequency resolution of the spectrum. magnitudes specifies the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. phases specifies the phase of the spectrum. channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. |
|  | f0 specifies the start frequency of the spectrum. |
|  | df specifies the frequency resolution of the spectrum. |
|  | magnitudes specifies the magnitude of the spectrum in RMS level. |
|  | phases specifies the phase of the spectrum. |
|  | channel specifies the channel of the voltage or current spectra. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the electrical power system. Use the Initialize System VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | power values returns power values that this VI calculates through a basic measurement time interval, which is typically 10 or 12 cycles. active power returns the active power value in watts. Each element of active power corresponds to one channel. reactive power returns the reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of reactive power corresponds to one channel. apparent power returns the apparent power value in volt-amperes. Each element of apparent power corresponds to one channel. fundamental active power returns the fundamental active power value in watts. Each element of fundamental active power corresponds to one channel. fundamental reactive power returns the fundamental reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of fundamental reactive power corresponds to one channel. cosine returns the fundamental power factor. Each element of cosine corresponds to one channel. power factor returns the power factor value. Each element of power factor corresponds to one channel. |
|  | active power returns the active power value in watts. Each element of active power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | reactive power returns the reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of reactive power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | apparent power returns the apparent power value in volt-amperes. Each element of apparent power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | fundamental active power returns the fundamental active power value in watts. Each element of fundamental active power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | fundamental reactive power returns the fundamental reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of fundamental reactive power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | cosine returns the fundamental power factor. Each element of cosine corresponds to one channel. |
|  | power factor returns the power factor value. Each element of power factor corresponds to one channel. |
|  | 3-phase power values returns the total power values one three-phase system after another. active power returns the active power value in watts. Each element of active power corresponds to one channel. reactive power returns the reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of reactive power corresponds to one channel. apparent power returns the apparent power value in volt-amperes. Each element of apparent power corresponds to one channel. fundamental active power returns the fundamental active power value in watts. Each element of fundamental active power corresponds to one channel. fundamental reactive power returns the fundamental reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of fundamental reactive power corresponds to one channel. cosine returns the fundamental power factor. Each element of cosine corresponds to one channel. power factor returns the power factor value. Each element of power factor corresponds to one channel. |
|  | active power returns the active power value in watts. Each element of active power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | reactive power returns the reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of reactive power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | apparent power returns the apparent power value in volt-amperes. Each element of apparent power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | fundamental active power returns the fundamental active power value in watts. Each element of fundamental active power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | fundamental reactive power returns the fundamental reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of fundamental reactive power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | cosine returns the fundamental power factor. Each element of cosine corresponds to one channel. |
|  | power factor returns the power factor value. Each element of power factor corresponds to one channel. |
|  | system info out returns information about the basic configuration of the electrical power system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Power Values Details

Line and Bus Configurations


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Power Values VI:

## TOPIC 00103: Power VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `power-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/power-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Three Phase Power Measurement VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the power values from a nonsinusoidal voltage and current signal as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. Wire data to the voltage waveform input or the current waveform input to determine the polymorphic instan

### Power VI

**Owning Palette:** Three Phase Power Measurement VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the power values from a nonsinusoidal voltage and current signal as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. Wire data to the **voltage waveform** input or the **current waveform** input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

Examples

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Note

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Power__1-Phase_1_Ch.gif']

|  | voltage waveform specifies the voltage signal in volts. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | current waveform specifies the current signal in amperes. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | power value returns the power value for nonsinusoidal, single-phase signals as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. active power returns the active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the active power: where T represents the cycle time in seconds k represents a positive integer number τ represents the moment when the measurement starts Pa represents the instantaneous active power in watts apparent power returns the apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the apparent power: where V represents the RMS value of the voltage in volts I represents the RMS value of the current in amperes non-active power returns the non-active power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the non-active power: where S represents the apparent power in volt-amperes P represents the active power in watts power factor returns the power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the power factor: where P represents the active power in watts S represents the apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | active power returns the active power in watts as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the active power: where T represents the cycle time in seconds k represents a positive integer number τ represents the moment when the measurement starts Pa represents the instantaneous active power in watts |
|  | apparent power returns the apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the apparent power: where V represents the RMS value of the voltage in volts I represents the RMS value of the current in amperes |
|  | non-active power returns the non-active power in volt-amperes reactive as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the non-active power: where S represents the apparent power in volt-amperes P represents the active power in watts |
|  | power factor returns the power factor as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the power factor: where P represents the active power in watts S represents the apparent power in volt-amperes |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Y specifies the data values of the waveform. This VI uses the following equation to calculate the active power: where T represents the cycle time in seconds k represents a positive integer number τ represents the moment when the measurement starts Pa represents the instantaneous active power in watts effective apparent power returns the effective apparent power in volt-amperes as defined in IEEE Std 1459-2010. ## TOPIC 00104: Power VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `power-vi2.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/power-vi2.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Power Measurement VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the power values from a voltage and current signal. Wire data to the voltage waveform input or the current waveform input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. If you do not pr

### Power VI

**Owning Palette:** Power Measurement VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the power values from a voltage and current signal. Wire data to the **voltage waveform** input or the **current waveform** input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

If you do not preprocess the voltage and current signals, you must have at least ten cycles of the signals to ensure measurement accuracy.

Examples

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Power (1 Channel)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Power__1_Channel.gif']

|  | voltage waveform specifies the voltage signal in volts. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | current waveform specifies the current signal in amperes. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | power value returns power values from the voltage and current signals. active power returns the value of active power in watts. apparent power returns the value of apparent power in volt-amperes. reactive power returns the value of reactive power in volt-ampere reactives. The reactive power that this VI calculates is the positive value of the reactive power, regardless of the phase difference between voltage and current signals. power factor returns the value of power factor. |
|  | active power returns the value of active power in watts. |
|  | apparent power returns the value of apparent power in volt-amperes. |
|  | reactive power returns the value of reactive power in volt-ampere reactives. The reactive power that this VI calculates is the positive value of the reactive power, regardless of the phase difference between voltage and current signals. |
|  | power factor returns the value of power factor. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Power (N Channels)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Power__N_Channels.gif']

|  | voltage waveforms specifies the voltage signal, in volts, for each channel. The number and sequence of voltage channels must correspond to the number and sequence of current channels. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
| --- | --- |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | current waveforms specifies the current signal, in amperes, for each channel. The number and sequence of current channels must correspond to the number and sequence of voltage channels. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | power values returns power values from the voltage and current signals for each channel. active power returns the value of active power in watts. apparent power returns the value of apparent power in volt-amperes. reactive power returns the value of reactive power in volt-ampere reactives. The reactive power that this VI calculates is the positive value of the reactive power, regardless of the phase difference between voltage and current signals. power factor returns the value of power factor. |
|  | active power returns the value of active power in watts. |
|  | apparent power returns the value of apparent power in volt-amperes. |
|  | reactive power returns the value of reactive power in volt-ampere reactives. The reactive power that this VI calculates is the positive value of the reactive power, regardless of the phase difference between voltage and current signals. |
|  | power factor returns the value of power factor. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Power VI:

- Basic Power Measurement (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Measurement
- Advanced Power Measurement (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Measurement

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=preprocessing-vis.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00105: Preprocessing VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `preprocessing-vis.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/preprocessing-vis.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Power Quality VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use the Preprocessing VIs to preprocess signals for further power quality analysis. Palette Objec

### Preprocessing VIs

**Owning Palette:** Power Quality VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the Preprocessing VIs to preprocess signals for further power quality analysis.

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Build Data Blocks | Builds even-angle-spaced signals into data blocks. You must use the Resampling for Power Quality VI to resample even-time-spaced voltage and current signals into even-angle-spaced signals. By default, this VI builds data blocks according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. You can use a customized setting when your application does not follow IEC 61000-4-30:2008. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |
| Resampling for Power Quality | Resamples even-time-spaced voltage and current signals into even-angle-spaced signals. The output signals are synchronous with the line frequency with a constant number of samples per cycle. Use the FPGA Resampling VI if you want to perform resampling on FPGA targets. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |

Parent topic:

Power Quality VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=protection-vis.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00106: Protection VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `protection-vis.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/protection-vis.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: FPGA VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use the Protection VIs to implement protection devices on FPGA targets. Protection devices measure input e

### Protection VIs

**Owning Palette:** FPGA VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the Protection VIs to implement protection devices on FPGA targets. Protection devices measure input energizing quantities, set thresholds of pick-up signals, and delay for certain time intervals before trip signals assert. Protection devices also control the automatic reclosing and lockout of circuit breakers.

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| FPGA Definite Time Delay | Delays for the time interval that you specify in time delay settings, which includes the operate time and the reset time. Use this VI to build a protection relay with definite time delay characteristics, such as a 27 undervoltage relay, 50 instantaneous overcurrent relay, 59 overvoltage relay, and 81 frequency relay. You can compensate for the processing delay of the input energizing quantity to achieve higher operate time accuracy. |
| FPGA Inverse Time Delay | Delays for the time interval that you specify in the Inverse Time Curves VI before the trip signal asserts. The Inverse Time Curves VI generates inverse time curves for this VI to use. You can compensate for the processing delay of the input energizing quantity under a specific G/Gs in the Inverse Time Curves VI to achieve higher operate time accuracy. |
| FPGA Measurement for Protection | Computes the instantaneous magnitude, phase, and frequency values of the fundamental frequency component for protection applications. The output rate is 24 samples per cycle. You must use the FPGA Resampling VI to resample even-time-spaced voltage and current signals into even-angle-spaced signals before running this VI. |
| FPGA Reclosing Logic | Controls the automatic reclosing and lockout of a circuit breaker based on the reclosing settings that you specify. This VI also displays the reclosing status. |
| FPGA Threshold | Detects the input energizing quantity G for deciding whether G turns to a pick-up signal according to pick-up settings. |

Parent topic:

FPGA VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=protection-vis2.html language=enus -->

Use the Protection VIs to generate data for the Protection VIs on FPGA targets to use. Palette

### Protection VIs

**Owning Palette:** Electrical Power VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the Protection VIs to generate data for the Protection VIs on FPGA targets to use.

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Inverse Time Curves | Generates memory initial values and memory size, which contain the information of the inverse time curves, for the FPGA Inverse Time Delay VI to use. You can compensate for the processing delay of the input energizing quantity under a specific G/Gs to achieve higher operate time accuracy. |

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=rapid-voltage-changes-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00108: Rapid Voltage Changes VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `rapid-voltage-changes-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/rapid-voltage-changes-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Events VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Detects rapid RMS voltage changes between two steady state conditions according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. The variation in voltage must not exceed the voltage dip threshold, the voltage swell threshold, or the thresholds that contain both v

### Rapid Voltage Changes VI

**Owning Palette:** Events VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Detects rapid RMS voltage changes between two steady state conditions according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. The variation in voltage must not exceed the voltage dip threshold, the voltage swell threshold, or the thresholds that contain both voltage dip and voltage swell. EN 50160:2007 defines default threshold values. You also can specify custom threshold values.

This VI supports measuring single-bus single-line or multiple-bus single-line systems.

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Rapid_Voltage_Changes.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | half-cycle voltage RMS specifies RMS values measured over half cycle, which starts at fundamental zero crossing and refreshes each half cycle. Each element of half-cycle voltage RMS corresponds to one channel or phase. The value order must be in the sequence of Phase A (L1), Phase B (L2), and Phase C (L3). This VI does not calculate Phase N. timestamps specifies the end time of the half cycle. RMS values specifies the RMS value over half cycle. |
|  | timestamps specifies the end time of the half cycle. |
|  | RMS values specifies the RMS value over half cycle. |
|  | RVC settings specifies threshold values. Refer to IEC 61000-4-30:2008 for detailed information about threshold values. min rate of change specifies the minimum rate of change in percentage of nominal voltage per second. The rate of change is the difference between two successive steady state values divided by transition time. If the actual rate of change is less than min rate of change, rapid voltage change is not detected. min rate of change must be less than 10 and greater than zero. The default is 0.5. steadiness of steady state specifies the steadiness of the steady-state conditions in percent of nominal voltage. steadiness of steady state specifies the tolerance band for the detection of the begin and the end of steady state condition. steadiness of steady state must be less than 10 but greater than 0.01. The default is 0.2. min steady state duration specifies the minimum duration of the steady state conditions in seconds. min steady state duration must be less than 10 and greater than or equal to 0.2. The default is 1. min steady state difference specifies the minimum difference between the two steady state conditions in percent of nominal voltage. If the steady state voltage change is not greater than or equal to min steady state difference, rapid voltage change is not detected. min steady state difference must be greater than or equal to 0. The default is 0. max voltage change specifies the voltage threshold for calculating t(dt)>max voltage change during the transition period in percent of nominal voltage. The default is 3.3. |
|  | min rate of change specifies the minimum rate of change in percentage of nominal voltage per second. The rate of change is the difference between two successive steady state values divided by transition time. If the actual rate of change is less than min rate of change, rapid voltage change is not detected. min rate of change must be less than 10 and greater than zero. The default is 0.5. |
|  | steadiness of steady state specifies the steadiness of the steady-state conditions in percent of nominal voltage. steadiness of steady state specifies the tolerance band for the detection of the begin and the end of steady state condition. steadiness of steady state must be less than 10 but greater than 0.01. The default is 0.2. |
|  | min steady state duration specifies the minimum duration of the steady state conditions in seconds. min steady state duration must be less than 10 and greater than or equal to 0.2. The default is 1. |
|  | min steady state difference specifies the minimum difference between the two steady state conditions in percent of nominal voltage. If the steady state voltage change is not greater than or equal to min steady state difference, rapid voltage change is not detected. min steady state difference must be greater than or equal to 0. The default is 0. |
|  | max voltage change specifies the voltage threshold for calculating t(dt)>max voltage change during the transition period in percent of nominal voltage. The default is 3.3. |
|  | voltage flags? specifies voltage flags of a basic measurement time interval, which is 10-cycle or 12-cycle. The interval is flagged during a dip, swell, or interruption, if any half-cycle voltage RMS is out of dip or swell thresholds for each cycle. Each element of voltage flags? corresponds to one channel or phase. The default is FALSE. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the electrical power system. Use the Initialize System VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | rapid voltage changes events returns information of rapid voltage changes. RVC timestamp returns the start time of the rapid voltage change event. phase returns the measurement phase of the voltage change event. dc returns the steady state voltage change, which is the difference between two successive steady state values. dc is expressed as percentage of the nominal voltage. dc is positive if voltage decreases during a change. dc value is negative if voltage increases during a change. dmax returns the maximum voltage change during a voltage change, which is the value of the maximum difference between the last steady state condition and half-cycle voltage RMS values, observed during a voltage change. dmax is expressed as a percent of the nominal voltage. dmax is positive when maximum voltage deviation is observed during a voltage decrease. dmax is negative when maximum voltage deviation is observed during a voltage increase. t(dt)>max voltage change returns the accumulated time during a transition time, when the voltage deviation exceeds max voltage change. transition time returns the time difference between the end of a steady state condition and begin of a new steady state condition. |
|  | RVC timestamp returns the start time of the rapid voltage change event. |
|  | phase returns the measurement phase of the voltage change event. |
|  | dc returns the steady state voltage change, which is the difference between two successive steady state values. dc is expressed as percentage of the nominal voltage. dc is positive if voltage decreases during a change. dc value is negative if voltage increases during a change. |
|  | dmax returns the maximum voltage change during a voltage change, which is the value of the maximum difference between the last steady state condition and half-cycle voltage RMS values, observed during a voltage change. dmax is expressed as a percent of the nominal voltage. dmax is positive when maximum voltage deviation is observed during a voltage decrease. dmax is negative when maximum voltage deviation is observed during a voltage increase. |
|  | t(dt)>max voltage change returns the accumulated time during a transition time, when the voltage deviation exceeds max voltage change. |
|  | transition time returns the time difference between the end of a steady state condition and begin of a new steady state condition. |
|  | system info out returns information about the basic configuration of the electrical power system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |
|  | half-cycle voltage RMS specifies RMS values measured over half cycle, which starts at fundamental zero crossing and refreshes each half cycle. Each element of half-cycle voltage RMS corresponds to one channel or phase. The value order must be in the sequence of Phase A (L1), Phase B (L2), and Phase C (L3). This VI does not calculate Phase N. timestamps specifies the end time of the half cycle. RMS values specifies the RMS value over half cycle. |
|  | timestamps specifies the end time of the half cycle. |
|  | RMS values specifies the RMS value over half cycle. |

Rapid Voltage Changes Details

**Related Information**
Line and Bus Configurations


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Rapid Voltage Changes VI:

- Events Detection (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (DAQmx)
- Events Detection VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)
- Events Detection VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (SAR)
- Multiple Bus Power Quality Applications VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Multiple Line Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)

## TOPIC 00109: Read Waveforms (SGL) VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `read-waveforms-sgl-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/read-waveforms-sgl-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Reads the selected data from a circular buffer. Select the instance of the VI that corresponds to your processing requirements. Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI. Examples Read Most Recent (SGL) Reads the most recent data from the buffer. This read does not guarantee a continuo

### Read Waveforms (SGL) VI

Reads the selected data from a circular buffer. Select the instance of the VI that corresponds to your processing requirements.

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Examples


Read Most Recent (SGL)

Reads the most recent data from the buffer. This read does not guarantee a continuous waveform data. Use this instance when the application only needs to process the most recent data to update current status or a data dashboard.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Read_Most_Recent_(SGL).gif']

|  | circular buffer in specifies the data value reference to the circular buffer to operate on. |
| --- | --- |
|  | length specifies the number of samples per channel to read from the circular buffer. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | timeout specifies the timeout, in ms, for the operation. The default for this instance of read is 250 ms. |
|  | circular buffer out returns an identical data value reference to the circular buffer. |
|  | waveforms returns the requested data for all the channels in the circular buffer. t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt returns the sample interval. Y returns the measured values. |
|  | t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt returns the sample interval. |
|  | Y returns the measured values. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Read Continuous (SGL)

Reads continuous data from the buffer. Continuous data is defined as continuing with no time gap with respect to the last data read by this VI instance.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Read_Continuous_(SGL).gif']

|  | reset specifies whether to reset the current read sample index to the index of the first available sample index. The default is FALSE. This VI will automatically get the first available data on First Call. |
| --- | --- |
|  | circular buffer in specifies the data value reference to the circular buffer to operate on. |
|  | length specifies the number of samples per channel to read from the circular buffer. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | timeout specifies the timeout, in ms, for the operation. The default for this instance of read is 250 ms. |
|  | circular buffer out returns an identical data value reference to the circular buffer. |
|  | waveforms returns the requested data for all the channels in the circular buffer. t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt returns the sample interval. Y returns the measured values. |
|  | t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt returns the sample interval. |
|  | Y returns the measured values. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Read Continuous (1CH) (SGL)

Reads continuous data from the buffer. Continuous data is defined as continuing with no time gap with respect to the last data read by this VI instance.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Read_Continuous_(1CH)_(SGL).gif']

|  | reset specifies whether to reset the current read sample index to the index of the first available sample index. The default is FALSE. This VI will automatically get the first available data on First Call. |
| --- | --- |
|  | circular buffer in specifies the data value reference to the circular buffer to operate on. |
|  | channel index specifies the channel element index within the array of waveforms in the circular buffer. This index value should be constant between runs and should index a reference channel. |
|  | length specifies the number of samples per channel to read from the circular buffer. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | timeout specifies the timeout, in ms, for the operation. The default for this instance of read is 250 ms. |
|  | circular buffer out returns an identical data value reference to the circular buffer. |
|  | waveform returns a waveform with the requested number of samples specified by length for the channel specified by channel index. t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt returns the sample interval. Y returns the measured values. |
|  | t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt returns the sample interval. |
|  | Y returns the measured values. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Read Subset at Timestamp (SGL)

Reads the specified time segment of data from the buffer. The time segment is defined by a start time of **timestamp** and number of samples equal to **length**.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Read_Subset_at_Timestamp_(SGL).gif']

|  | circular buffer in specifies the data value reference to the circular buffer to operate on. |
| --- | --- |
|  | timestamp specifies the timestamp of the first sample to read from the circular buffer. |
|  | length specifies the number of samples per channel to read from the circular buffer. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | timeout specifies the timeout, in ms, for the operation. The default for this instance of read is 250 ms. |
|  | circular buffer out returns an identical data value reference to the circular buffer. |
|  | waveforms returns the requested data for all the channels in the circular buffer. t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt returns the sample interval. Y returns the measured values. |
|  | t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt returns the sample interval. |
|  | Y returns the measured values. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Read Between Timestamps (SGL)

Reads the specified time segment of data from the buffer. The time segment is defined by a start time equal to **start timestamp** and an end time equal to **end timestamp**.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Read_Between_Timestamps_(SGL).gif']

|  | circular buffer in specifies the data value reference to the circular buffer to operate on. |
| --- | --- |
|  | start timestamp specifies the timestamp of the first sample to read from the circular buffer. If left unwired, the default start timestamp of 0 will be set to the oldest timestamp in the circular buffer. |
|  | end timestamp specifies the timestamp of the last sample to read from the circular buffer. If left unwired, the default start timestamp of 0 will be set to the newest timestamp in the circular buffer. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | timeout specifies the timeout, in ms, for the operation. The default for this instance of read is 0. |
|  | circular buffer out returns an identical data value reference to the circular buffer. |
|  | waveforms returns the requested data for all the channels in the circular buffer. t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt returns the sample interval. Y returns the measured values. |
|  | t0 returns the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt returns the sample interval. |
|  | Y returns the measured values. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Read Waveforms (SGL) VI.

- Traction Inverter Acquisition and Waveform Logging (DAQmx) VI: LabVIEW\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Traction Inverter
- Traction Inverter Power and Efficiency Measurement and Logging (DAQmx) VI: LabVIEW\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Traction Inverter
