## TOPIC 00137: Torque, Load, and Efficiency VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `torque-load-and-efficiency-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/torque-load-and-efficiency-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Motor VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the torque, load, and efficiency values based on the motor nameplate information. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. Details Example Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI. Torque

### Torque, Load, and Efficiency VI

**Owning Palette:** Motor VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the torque, load, and efficiency values based on the motor nameplate information. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use.

Details Example

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

Note

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Torque_Load_and_Efficiency_AG.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | fundamental frequency specifies the instantaneous fundamental frequency, in hertz, of the motor signal. Use the corresponding upstream Electrical Power VIs, such as the Frequency VI, to measure the fundamental frequency. The value must be greater than zero. |
|  | phase voltage waveforms specifies motor voltage waveforms. Each array element represents a waveform of one three-phase motor. The array size must be a multiple of three. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | current waveforms specifies motor current waveforms. Each array element represents one phase. The array size must be a multiple of three. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | speed specifies the motor speed in revolutions per minute (RPM). Each array element corresponds to one motor. Use the Speed Estimation VI to calculate the motor speed. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | nameplate information specifies the nameplate information to calculate the torque, load, and efficiency. If you change nameplate information while the VI runs, the information changes take effect only when reset? is TRUE. nominal frequency specifies the nominal frequency of phase voltage waveforms. 050 Hz (default)160 Hz motor information specifies the motor information on the nameplate. Each array element corresponds to one motor. synchronous speed specifies the speed in RPM under no load. full load specifies the motor full load, in kilowatts, according to the nameplate. full load efficiency specifies the motor full load efficiency, as a percentage, according to the nameplate. |
|  | nominal frequency specifies the nominal frequency of phase voltage waveforms. 050 Hz (default)160 Hz |
| 0 | 50 Hz (default) |
| 1 | 60 Hz |
|  | motor information specifies the motor information on the nameplate. Each array element corresponds to one motor. synchronous speed specifies the speed in RPM under no load. full load specifies the motor full load, in kilowatts, according to the nameplate. full load efficiency specifies the motor full load efficiency, as a percentage, according to the nameplate. |
|  | synchronous speed specifies the speed in RPM under no load. |
|  | full load specifies the motor full load, in kilowatts, according to the nameplate. |
|  | full load efficiency specifies the motor full load efficiency, as a percentage, according to the nameplate. |
|  | total input active power specifies the total input active power, in watts, for each motor. Each array element corresponds to the total input active power of one motor. |
|  | stator resistance specifies the stator resistance, in ohms, for each motor. The value is half of the line-to-line resistance. Each array element represents the resistance of a motor. If you change stator resistance while the VI runs, the change takes effect only when reset? is TRUE. |
|  | torque waveforms returns the instantaneous torque waveforms in Newton meters. Each array element corresponds to one motor. t0 returns the start time of the waveform. dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 returns the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | torque returns the torque value in Newton meters. Each array element corresponds to one motor. |
|  | load returns the motor load in kilowatts. Each array element corresponds to one motor. Use the load value as the output mechanical power. |
|  | efficiency returns the motor efficiency as a percentage. Each array element corresponds to one motor. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Note

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Torque_Load_and_Efficiency_AG1.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | fundamental frequency specifies the instantaneous fundamental frequency, in hertz, of the motor signal. Use the corresponding upstream Electrical Power VIs, such as the Frequency VI, to measure the fundamental frequency. The value must be greater than zero. |
|  | phase voltage waveforms specifies motor voltage waveforms. Each array element represents a waveform of one three-phase motor. The array size must be three. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | current waveforms specifies motor current waveforms. Each array element represents one phase. The array size must be three. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | speed specifies the motor speed in revolutions per minute (RPM). Use the Speed Estimation VI to calculate the motor speed. |
|  | initial conditions specifies initial internal VI conditions to guarantee consecutive calculation correctness. If you use this VI for the first time or reset? is TRUE, this VI automatically creates the initial internal conditions. Otherwise, wire the most recent final conditions to the subsequent initial conditions to inherit the conditions. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | nameplate information specifies the nameplate information to calculate the torque, load, and efficiency. If you change nameplate information while the VI runs, the information changes take effect only when reset? is TRUE. nominal frequency specifies the nominal frequency of phase voltage waveforms. 050 Hz (default)160 Hz motor information specifies the motor information on the nameplate. synchronous speed specifies the speed in RPM under no load. full load specifies the motor full load, in kilowatts, according to the nameplate. full load efficiency specifies the motor full load efficiency, as a percentage, according to the nameplate. |
|  | nominal frequency specifies the nominal frequency of phase voltage waveforms. 050 Hz (default)160 Hz |
| 0 | 50 Hz (default) |
| 1 | 60 Hz |
|  | motor information specifies the motor information on the nameplate. synchronous speed specifies the speed in RPM under no load. full load specifies the motor full load, in kilowatts, according to the nameplate. full load efficiency specifies the motor full load efficiency, as a percentage, according to the nameplate. |
|  | synchronous speed specifies the speed in RPM under no load. |
|  | full load specifies the motor full load, in kilowatts, according to the nameplate. |
|  | full load efficiency specifies the motor full load efficiency, as a percentage, according to the nameplate. |
|  | total input active power specifies the total input active power in watts. |
|  | stator resistance specifies the stator resistance in ohms. The value is half of the line-to-line resistance. If you change stator resistance while the VI runs, the change takes effect only when reset? is TRUE. |
|  | torque waveform returns the instantaneous torque waveforms in Newton meters. t0 returns the start time of the waveform. dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 returns the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt returns the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y returns the data values of the waveform. |
|  | torque returns the torque value in Newton meters. |
|  | load returns the motor load in kilowatts. Use the load value as the output mechanical power. |
|  | efficiency returns the motor efficiency as a percentage. |
|  | final conditions returns the final internal VI conditions after the internal calculation of this VI. Use final conditions as initial conditions of the subsequent iteration except that you want to customize the conditions according to your needs. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Note

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Torque_Load_and_Efficiency_IP.gif']

|  | additional efficiency information specifies motor efficiencies under different loads. Each array element corresponds to one motor. If you do not wire this parameter, the Input Power instance uses full load efficiency of nameplate information as the motor efficiency at the output terminal to calculate the torque, load, and efficiency. To return more accurate torque, load, and efficiency values, National Instruments recommends that you refer to the motor vendor's documentation, websites, or databases to specify the motor efficiencies under different loads. If you change additional efficiency information while the VI runs, the information changes take effect only when reset? is TRUE. 3/4 load efficiency specifies the motor efficiency as a percentage when the load is three quarters of the full load. 1/2 load efficiency specifies the motor efficiency as a percentage when the load is half of the full load. 1/4 load efficiency specifies the motor efficiency as a percentage when the load is a quarter of the full load. |
| --- | --- |
|  | 3/4 load efficiency specifies the motor efficiency as a percentage when the load is three quarters of the full load. |
|  | 1/2 load efficiency specifies the motor efficiency as a percentage when the load is half of the full load. |
|  | 1/4 load efficiency specifies the motor efficiency as a percentage when the load is a quarter of the full load. |
|  | speed specifies the motor speed in revolutions per minute (RPM). Each array element corresponds to one motor. Use the Speed Estimation VI to calculate the motor speed. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | nameplate information specifies the nameplate information that the VI uses to calculate the torque, load, and efficiency. If you change nameplate information while the VI runs, the information changes take effect only when reset? is TRUE. full load specifies the motor full load, in kilowatts, according to the nameplate. full load efficiency specifies the motor full load efficiency, as a percentage, according to the nameplate. |
|  | full load specifies the motor full load, in kilowatts, according to the nameplate. |
|  | full load efficiency specifies the motor full load efficiency, as a percentage, according to the nameplate. |
|  | total input active power specifies the total input active power, in watts, for each motor. Each array element corresponds to the total input active power of one motor. |
|  | torque returns the torque value in Newton meters. Each array element corresponds to one motor. |
|  | load returns the motor load in kilowatts. Each array element corresponds to one motor. Use the load value as the output mechanical power. |
|  | efficiency returns the motor efficiency as a percentage. Each array element corresponds to one motor. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Torque, Load, and Efficiency Details

**Related Information**
Delta Connections


Example

Refer to the Motor Monitoring.lvproj in the labview\examples\Electrical Power\Motor Monitoring directory for an example of using the Torque, Load, and Efficiency VI.

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=transform-vis.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00138: Transform VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `transform-vis.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/transform-vis.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Three Phase Power Measurement VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use the Transform VIs to convert nonsinusoidal three-phase signals from one refer

### Transform VIs

**Owning Palette:** Three Phase Power Measurement VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the Transform VIs to convert nonsinusoidal three-phase signals from one reference frame to another reference frame and to convert between line voltages and phase voltages and between line currents and phase-to-phase currents.

The following vector diagram shows transforms from one reference frame to another reference frame.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/ep_ref_frame_vector_diagram.gif']

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| ABC to Alpha-Beta-Gamma Transform | Transforms signals from the ABC reference frame to the alpha-beta-gamma reference frame. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. |
| ABC to DQZ Transform | Transforms signals from the ABC reference frame to the DQZ reference frame. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. |
| Alpha-Beta-Gamma to ABC Transform | Transforms signals from the alpha-beta-gamma reference frame to the ABC reference frame. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. |
| Alpha-Beta-Gamma to DQZ Transform | Transforms signals from the alpha-beta-gamma reference frame to the DQZ reference frame. |
| Convert Line Current to Phase Current (Delta Configuration) | Converts line current to phase current in Delta configuration. This VI supports only nonsinusoidal, three-phase, three-wire, balanced signals. |
| Convert Line Voltage to Phase Voltage (Y Configuration) | Converts line voltage to phase voltage in Y configuration. This VI supports only nonsinusoidal, three-phase, three-wire, balanced signals. |
| Convert Phase Voltage to Line Voltage (Y Configuration) | Converts phase voltage to line voltage in Y configuration. |
| DQZ to ABC Transform | Transforms signals from the DQZ reference frame to the ABC reference frame. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. |
| DQZ to Alpha-Beta-Gamma Transform | Transforms signals from the DQZ reference frame to the alpha-beta-gamma reference frame. |

Parent topic:

Three Phase Power Measurement VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=trigger-detector-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00139: Trigger Detector VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `trigger-detector-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/trigger-detector-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Utility VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Detects crossing of limits for the measurement that you specify and returns triggers if occur. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems except the DBL instance. Use the DBL instance to measure one channel on one l

### Trigger Detector VI

**Owning Palette:** Utility VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Detects crossing of limits for the measurement that you specify and returns triggers if occur.

This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems except the DBL instance. Use the DBL instance to measure one channel on one line at one time.

Details

Use the pull-down menu to select an instance of this VI.

| Add to the block diagram | Find on the palette |
| --- | --- |

Trigger Detector (DBL)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Trigger_Detector__DBL.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | data in specifies the input data for trigger detection. You pass the input data to this VI point-by-point. |
|  | trigger definition specifies trigger configuration. trigger ID specifies a unique identifier for the trigger configuration. This VI uses trigger ID for each event detected so that you can find the origin of the event in further analysis. low limit specifies the value of the low limit for trigger detection. high limit specifies the value of the high limit for trigger detection. direction specifies the direction of limit cross that causes a trigger. 0Out of limits (default)This VI generates a trigger only when the value leaves the limits.1Back to limitsThis VI generates a trigger only when the value goes back into the limits.2BothThis VI generates a trigger each time the value cross the limits. hysteresis type specifies the type the hysteresis and how this VI uses the hysteresis value that you specify. 0Level (default)Applies hysteresis on the data value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit plus hysteresis value or crosses a limit minus hysteresis value.1TimeApply hysteresis on the time value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit and stays above/below the limit for the time equal or longer than the hysteresis value. hysteresis specifies the hysteresis value. This VI uses hysteresis for trigger hysteresis evaluation according the hysteresis type. If hysteresis type is Level, hysteresis value has the same unit as the input data. If hysteresis type is Time, hysteresis value is in seconds. |
|  | trigger ID specifies a unique identifier for the trigger configuration. This VI uses trigger ID for each event detected so that you can find the origin of the event in further analysis. |
|  | low limit specifies the value of the low limit for trigger detection. |
|  | high limit specifies the value of the high limit for trigger detection. |
|  | direction specifies the direction of limit cross that causes a trigger. 0Out of limits (default)This VI generates a trigger only when the value leaves the limits.1Back to limitsThis VI generates a trigger only when the value goes back into the limits.2BothThis VI generates a trigger each time the value cross the limits. |
| 0 | Out of limits (default)This VI generates a trigger only when the value leaves the limits. |
| 1 | Back to limitsThis VI generates a trigger only when the value goes back into the limits. |
| 2 | BothThis VI generates a trigger each time the value cross the limits. |
|  | hysteresis type specifies the type the hysteresis and how this VI uses the hysteresis value that you specify. 0Level (default)Applies hysteresis on the data value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit plus hysteresis value or crosses a limit minus hysteresis value.1TimeApply hysteresis on the time value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit and stays above/below the limit for the time equal or longer than the hysteresis value. |
| 0 | Level (default)Applies hysteresis on the data value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit plus hysteresis value or crosses a limit minus hysteresis value. |
| 1 | TimeApply hysteresis on the time value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit and stays above/below the limit for the time equal or longer than the hysteresis value. |
|  | hysteresis specifies the hysteresis value. This VI uses hysteresis for trigger hysteresis evaluation according the hysteresis type. If hysteresis type is Level, hysteresis value has the same unit as the input data. If hysteresis type is Time, hysteresis value is in seconds. |
|  | data timestamp specifies the timestamp of the input data. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | triggers returns information about the trigger that this VI detects. trigger timestamp returns the timestamp of the trigger that this VI detects. trigger ID returns the identifier of the detected trigger. limit returns the limit value that is crossed during the trigger detection. cross direction returns the direction of the limit cross that generates the trigger. 0Over limit1Below limit |
|  | trigger timestamp returns the timestamp of the trigger that this VI detects. |
|  | trigger ID returns the identifier of the detected trigger. |
|  | limit returns the limit value that is crossed during the trigger detection. |
|  | cross direction returns the direction of the limit cross that generates the trigger. 0Over limit1Below limit |
| 0 | Over limit |
| 1 | Below limit |
|  | actual trigger state returns the state of the detected trigger. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | data in specifies the input data for trigger detection. Each element of data in corresponds to one measurement or one channel. You pass the input data to this VI point-by-point. |
|  | triggers definition specifies trigger configurations. trigger ID specifies a unique identifier for the trigger configuration. This VI uses trigger ID for each event detected so that you can find the origin of the event in further analysis. data index specifies the index of the data for which this VI detects triggers. low limit specifies the value of the low limit for trigger detection. high limit specifies the value of the high limit for trigger detection. direction specifies the direction of limit cross that causes a trigger. 0Out of limits (default)This VI generates a trigger only when the value leaves the limits.1Back to limitsThis VI generates a trigger only when the value goes back into the limits.2BothThis VI generates a trigger each time the value cross the limits. hysteresis type specifies the type the hysteresis and how this VI uses the hysteresis value that you specify. 0Level (default)Applies hysteresis on the data value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit plus hysteresis value or crosses a limit minus hysteresis value.1TimeApply hysteresis on the time value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit and stays above/below the limit for the time equal or longer than the hysteresis value. hysteresis specifies the hysteresis value. This VI uses hysteresis for trigger hysteresis evaluation according the hysteresis type. If hysteresis type is Level, hysteresis value has the same unit as the input data. If hysteresis type is Time, hysteresis value is in seconds. |

|  | high limit specifies the value of the high limit for trigger detection. |

| 1 | TimeApply hysteresis on the time value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit and stays above/below the limit for the time equal or longer than the hysteresis value. |
|  | hysteresis specifies the hysteresis value. This VI uses hysteresis for trigger hysteresis evaluation according the hysteresis type. If hysteresis type is Level, hysteresis value has the same unit as the input data. If hysteresis type is Time, hysteresis value is in seconds. |

Trigger Detector (Waveform)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Trigger_Detector__Waveform.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | waveforms specifies the input waveforms for trigger detection. t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | t0 specifies the start time of the waveform. |
|  | dt specifies the time interval, in seconds, between data points in the waveform. |
|  | Y specifies the data values of the waveform. |
|  | triggers definition specifies trigger configurations. trigger ID specifies a unique identifier for the trigger configuration. This VI uses trigger ID for each event detected so that you can find the origin of the event in further analysis. channel index specifies the channel index of the waveform for which this VI detects triggers. waveform parameter specifies the waveform parameter for which you want to perform trigger detection. 0Peak (default)Detects trigger for the waveform peak value.1Rate of rise (d/dt)Detects trigger for the waveform derivation. low limit specifies the value of the low limit for trigger detection. high limit specifies the value of the high limit for trigger detection. direction specifies the direction of limit cross that causes a trigger. 0Out of limits (default)This VI generates a trigger only when the value leaves the limits.1Back to limitsThis VI generates a trigger only when the value goes back into the limits.2BothThis VI generates a trigger each time the value cross the limits. hysteresis type specifies the type the hysteresis and how this VI uses the hysteresis value that you specify. 0Level (default)Applies hysteresis on the data value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit plus hysteresis value or crosses a limit minus hysteresis value.1TimeApply hysteresis on the time value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit and stays above/below the limit for the time equal or longer than the hysteresis value. hysteresis specifies the hysteresis value. This VI uses hysteresis for trigger hysteresis evaluation according the hysteresis type. If hysteresis type is Level, hysteresis value has the same unit as the input data. If hysteresis type is Time, hysteresis value is in seconds. |
|  | trigger ID specifies a unique identifier for the trigger configuration. This VI uses trigger ID for each event detected so that you can find the origin of the event in further analysis. |
|  | channel index specifies the channel index of the waveform for which this VI detects triggers. |
|  | waveform parameter specifies the waveform parameter for which you want to perform trigger detection. 0Peak (default)Detects trigger for the waveform peak value.1Rate of rise (d/dt)Detects trigger for the waveform derivation. |
| 0 | Peak (default)Detects trigger for the waveform peak value. |
| 1 | Rate of rise (d/dt)Detects trigger for the waveform derivation. |

Trigger Detector (Power Values)

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Trigger_Detector__Power_Values.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | power values specifies power values for trigger detection. active power specifies the active power value in watts. Each element of active power corresponds to one channel. reactive power specifies the reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of reactive power corresponds to one channel. apparent power specifies the apparent power value in volt-amperes. Each element of apparent power corresponds to one channel. fundamental active power specifies the fundamental active power value in watts. Each element of fundamental active power corresponds to one channel. fundamental reactive power specifies the fundamental reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of fundamental reactive power corresponds to one channel. cosine specifies the fundamental power factor. Each element of cosine corresponds to one channel. power factor specifies the power factor value. Each element of power factor corresponds to one channel. |
|  | active power specifies the active power value in watts. Each element of active power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | reactive power specifies the reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of reactive power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | apparent power specifies the apparent power value in volt-amperes. Each element of apparent power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | fundamental active power specifies the fundamental active power value in watts. Each element of fundamental active power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | fundamental reactive power specifies the fundamental reactive power value in volt-ampere reactives. Each element of fundamental reactive power corresponds to one channel. |
|  | cosine specifies the fundamental power factor. Each element of cosine corresponds to one channel. |
|  | power factor specifies the power factor value. Each element of power factor corresponds to one channel. |
|  | triggers definition specifies trigger configurations. trigger ID specifies a unique identifier for the trigger configuration. This VI uses trigger ID for each event detected so that you can find the origin of the event in further analysis. power value selection specifies the power value for which this VI detects triggers. 0Active power (default)1Reactive power2Apparent power3Fundamental active power4Fundamental reactive power5Cosine6Power factor power value index specifies the index of the selected power value for which this VI detects triggers. low limit specifies the value of the low limit for trigger detection. high limit specifies the value of the high limit for trigger detection. direction specifies the direction of limit cross that causes a trigger. 0Out of limits (default)This VI generates a trigger only when the value leaves the limits.1Back to limitsThis VI generates a trigger only when the value goes back into the limits.2BothThis VI generates a trigger each time the value cross the limits. hysteresis type specifies the type the hysteresis and how this VI uses the hysteresis value that you specify. 0Level (default)Applies hysteresis on the data value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit plus hysteresis value or crosses a limit minus hysteresis value.1TimeApply hysteresis on the time value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit and stays above/below the limit for the time equal or longer than the hysteresis value. hysteresis specifies the hysteresis value. This VI uses hysteresis for trigger hysteresis evaluation according the hysteresis type. If hysteresis type is Level, hysteresis value has the same unit as the input data. If hysteresis type is Time, hysteresis value is in seconds. |

Trigger Detector (Symmetrical Components)

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | symmetrical components specifies the symmetrical components data for trigger detection. All the symmetrical components have the following array order. 0Zero sequence symmetrical component1Positive sequence symmetrical component2Negative sequence symmetrical component voltage specifies voltage symmetrical components. fundamental voltage specifies fundamental voltage symmetrical components. current specifies current symmetrical components. fundamental current specifies fundamental current symmetrical components. |
| 0 | Zero sequence symmetrical component |
| 1 | Positive sequence symmetrical component |
| 2 | Negative sequence symmetrical component |
|  | voltage specifies voltage symmetrical components. |
|  | fundamental voltage specifies fundamental voltage symmetrical components. |
|  | current specifies current symmetrical components. |
|  | fundamental current specifies fundamental current symmetrical components. |
|  | triggers definition specifies trigger configurations. trigger ID specifies a unique identifier for the trigger configuration. This VI uses trigger ID for each event detected so that you can find the origin of the event in further analysis. component selection specifies the symmetrical component for which this VI detects triggers. 0Voltage (default)1Fundamental voltage2Current3Fundamental current component index specifies the index of the selected symmetrical component for which this VI detects triggers. All the symmetrical components have the following array order. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit and stays above/below the limit for the time equal or longer than the hysteresis value. |
| 0 | Level (default)Applies hysteresis on the data value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit plus hysteresis value or crosses a limit minus hysteresis value. |
| 1 | TimeApply hysteresis on the time value. This VI generates a trigger when the value crosses a limit and stays above/below the limit for the time equal or longer than the hysteresis value. |
|  | hysteresis specifies the hysteresis value. This VI uses hysteresis for trigger hysteresis evaluation according the hysteresis type. If hysteresis type is Level, hysteresis value has the same unit as the input data. If hysteresis type is Time, hysteresis value is in seconds. |
|  | data timestamp specifies the timestamp of the input data. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | triggers returns information about triggers that this VI detects. trigger timestamp returns the timestamp of the trigger that this VI detects. trigger ID returns the identifier of the detected trigger. limit returns the limit value that is crossed during the trigger detection. cross direction returns the direction of the limit cross that generates the trigger. 0Over limit1Below limit |
|  | trigger timestamp returns the timestamp of the trigger that this VI detects. |
|  | trigger ID returns the identifier of the detected trigger. |
|  | limit returns the limit value that is crossed during the trigger detection. |
|  | cross direction returns the direction of the limit cross that generates the trigger. 0Over limit1Below limit |
| 0 | Over limit |
| 1 | Below limit |
|  | actual trigger state returns the state of the detected triggers. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

|  | unbalance specifies the unbalance data for trigger detection. The first element of each array is the negative sequence unbalance, which is represented as sequence symmetrical component/positive sequence symmetrical component. The second element of each array is zero sequence unbalance, which is represented as zero sequence symmetrical component/positive sequence symmetrical component. voltage specifies negative sequence and zero sequence voltage unbalance in percentages. fundamental voltage specifies negative sequence and zero sequence fundamental voltage unbalance in percentages. current specifies negative sequence and zero sequence current unbalance in percentages. fundamental current specifies negative sequence and zero sequence fundamental current unbalance in percentages. |

|  | triggers definition specifies trigger configurations. trigger ID specifies a unique identifier for the trigger configuration. The following table lists the index and the corresponding unbalance data. <!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=trigger-detector-vi2.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00140: Trigger Detector VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `trigger-detector-vi2.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/trigger-detector-vi2.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Synchrophasor VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Detects triggers and returns the bitmapped flags of trigger reason in the STAT field of the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. Examples reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE.

### Trigger Detector VI

**Owning Palette:** Synchrophasor VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Detects triggers and returns the bitmapped flags of trigger reason in the STAT field of the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011.

Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Trigger_Detector_PMU.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | triggers definition specifies the configuration of the triggers. magnitudes and phases specifies the trigger configuration for magnitude and phase values. magnitude low limit specifies the value of the low limit for magnitude trigger detection. The default is 200. magnitude high limit specifies the value of the high limit for magnitude trigger detection. The default is 250. phase-angle diff limit specifies the absolute value of the phase-angle difference limit for phase-angle trigger detection. The default is 0.5. frequency high limit specifies the value of the high limit for frequency trigger detection. The default is 70. frequency low limit specifies the value of the low limit for frequency trigger detection. The default is 40. df/dt limit specifies the value of the high limit for rate of change of frequency trigger detection. The default is 323.67. |
|  | magnitudes and phases specifies the trigger configuration for magnitude and phase values. magnitude low limit specifies the value of the low limit for magnitude trigger detection. The default is 200. magnitude high limit specifies the value of the high limit for magnitude trigger detection. The default is 250. phase-angle diff limit specifies the absolute value of the phase-angle difference limit for phase-angle trigger detection. The default is 0.5. |
|  | magnitude low limit specifies the value of the low limit for magnitude trigger detection. The default is 200. |
|  | magnitude high limit specifies the value of the high limit for magnitude trigger detection. The default is 250. |
|  | phase-angle diff limit specifies the absolute value of the phase-angle difference limit for phase-angle trigger detection. The default is 0.5. |
|  | frequency high limit specifies the value of the high limit for frequency trigger detection. The default is 70. |
|  | frequency low limit specifies the value of the low limit for frequency trigger detection. The default is 40. |
|  | df/dt limit specifies the value of the high limit for rate of change of frequency trigger detection. The default is 323.67. |
|  | phasors specifies the phasor data for trigger detection. |
|  | frequency specifies the frequency data for trigger detection. |
|  | df/dt specifies the rate of change of frequency for trigger detection. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | triggers returns the information about the triggers that this VI detects. You can add logical operations on the status to generate Bit 11 of the STAT field in the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. magnitude low? returns TRUE when the magnitude value is less than magnitude low limit. magnitude high? returns TRUE when the magnitude value is greater than magnitude high limit. phase-angle diff? returns TRUE when the absolute value of phase-angle difference is greater than phase-angle diff limit. frequency high/low? returns TRUE when the frequency is greater than frequency high limit or less than frequency low limit. df/dt high? returns TRUE when the rate of change of frequency is greater than df/dt limit. |
|  | magnitude low? returns TRUE when the magnitude value is less than magnitude low limit. |
|  | magnitude high? returns TRUE when the magnitude value is greater than magnitude high limit. |
|  | phase-angle diff? returns TRUE when the absolute value of phase-angle difference is greater than phase-angle diff limit. |
|  | frequency high/low? returns TRUE when the frequency is greater than frequency high limit or less than frequency low limit. |
|  | df/dt high? returns TRUE when the rate of change of frequency is greater than df/dt limit. |
|  | trigger reason returns the bitmapped flags of trigger reason according to Bits 3-0 of the STAT field in the data frame as defined in IEEE Std C37.118.2-2011. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Trigger Detector VI:

- Synchrophasor Measurement and Communication (TCP) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Synchrophasor
- Synchrophasor Measurement and Communication (UDP) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Synchrophasor

Parent topic:

Synchrophasor VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=u-i-freq-vis.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00141: U, I & Freq VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `u-i-freq-vis.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/u-i-freq-vis.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Power Quality VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use the U, I & Freq VIs to calculate voltage, current, and frequency values for different electri

### U, I & Freq VIs

**Owning Palette:** Power Quality VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the U, I & Freq VIs to calculate voltage, current, and frequency values for different electrical power data.

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Deviation | Calculates underdeviation and overdeviation values from 10/12-cycle voltage RMS values. You can use the RMS (10 or 12 Cycles) VI to calculate voltage RMS values. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |
| Frequency (Half Cycle) | Calculates the half, single, and 10/12-cycle frequency of a voltage or current waveform, by using the zero crossings. You can use the Zero Crossing VI to calculate zero crossing and samples since the last cross. This VI refreshes the half and single-cycle frequency every half-cycle. If the value is flagged, this VI returns the last non-flagged frequency. You can use half-cycle frequency with the 10 Seconds instance of the EN50160 Aggregation VI to calculate frequencies according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. This VI supports measuring one channel on one line at one time. |
| RMS (10 or 12 Cycles) | Calculates the RMS voltage value or RMS current value of a complex spectrum for a 10/12-cycle block, according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |
| RMS (Half Cycle) | Calculates the RMS voltage or RMS current for a half/single-cycle block, by using the zero crossings. You can use the Zero Crossing VI to calculate zero crossing. This VI refreshes the RMS values every half-cycle. You use the half/single-cycle RMS values only for detection of voltage dips, swells, and interruption events, detection of rapid voltage changes, and measurement of flickers. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |

Parent topic:

Power Quality VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=unbalance-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00142: Unbalance VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `unbalance-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/unbalance-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Unbalance VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Calculates the voltage and current unbalance for a three-phase electrical power system, according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. Details Examples symmetrical components specifi

### Unbalance VI

**Owning Palette:** Unbalance VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Calculates the voltage and current unbalance for a three-phase electrical power system, according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008.

This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems.

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Unbalance.gif']

|  | symmetrical components specifies the voltage and current symmetrical components, returned from the Symmetrical Components VI, to calculate unbalance. The array elements of each symmetrical component list the sequences in the following order. 0Zero sequence symmetrical component1Positive sequence symmetrical component2Negative sequence symmetrical component In the single-bus single-line measurement, each array has three elements that represent the three sequences in the order. In the single-bus multiple-line or multiple-bus measurement, each array has a multiple of the three elements that represent the three sequences in the order one three-phase system after another. voltage specifies the voltage symmetrical components. fundamental voltage specifies the fundamental voltage symmetrical components. current specifies the current symmetrical components. fundamental current specifies the fundamental current symmetrical components. |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | Zero sequence symmetrical component |
| 1 | Positive sequence symmetrical component |
| 2 | Negative sequence symmetrical component |
|  | voltage specifies the voltage symmetrical components. |
|  | fundamental voltage specifies the fundamental voltage symmetrical components. |
|  | current specifies the current symmetrical components. |
|  | fundamental current specifies the fundamental current symmetrical components. |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the electrical power system. Use the Initialize System VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | unbalance returns the voltage and current unbalance. Each array has a multiple of two elements that returns the unbalance of three-phase systems one after another. The first array element of each array returns the negative sequence unbalance, which equals the ratio of the negative sequence symmetrical component to the positive sequence symmetrical component. The second element of each array returns the zero sequence unbalance, which equals the ratio of the zero sequence symmetrical component to the positive sequence symmetrical component. voltage returns negative sequence and zero sequence voltage unbalance expressed as a percentage. fundamental voltage returns negative sequence and zero sequence fundamental voltage unbalance expressed as a percentage. current returns negative sequence and zero sequence current unbalance expressed as a percentage. fundamental current returns negative sequence and zero sequence fundamental current unbalance expressed as a percentage. |
|  | voltage returns negative sequence and zero sequence voltage unbalance expressed as a percentage. |
|  | fundamental voltage returns negative sequence and zero sequence fundamental voltage unbalance expressed as a percentage. |
|  | current returns negative sequence and zero sequence current unbalance expressed as a percentage. |
|  | fundamental current returns negative sequence and zero sequence fundamental current unbalance expressed as a percentage. |
|  | system info out returns information about the basic configuration of the electrical power system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Unbalance Details

**Related Information**
Line and Bus Configurations
Power Transmission


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Unbalance VI:

- Unbalance Measurement (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (DAQmx)
- Unbalance Measurement VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)
- Unbalance Measurement VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (SAR)
- Single Bus Multiple Line Power Quality Applications VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Multiple Line Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)

Parent topic:

Unbalance VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=unbalance-vis.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00143: Unbalance VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `unbalance-vis.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/unbalance-vis.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Power Quality VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use the Unbalance VIs to calculate symmetrical components and analyze voltage and current unbalan

### Unbalance VIs

**Owning Palette:** Power Quality VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the Unbalance VIs to calculate symmetrical components and analyze voltage and current unbalance as defined in IEC 61000-4-30:2008.

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Symmetrical Components | Calculates voltage, current, and power symmetrical components for a 10-cycle or 12-cycle block. Use the results of this VI to analyze the voltage or current unbalance of a three-phase electrical power system. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |
| Symmetrical Components (1 Cycle) | Calculates the fundamental voltage, current, and power symmetrical components for a single-cycle block. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |
| Unbalance | Calculates the voltage and current unbalance for a three-phase electrical power system, according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |

Parent topic:

Power Quality VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=utility-vis.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00144: Utility VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `utility-vis.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/utility-vis.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Power Quality VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use the Utility VIs to perform connection conversion between Delta and Wye, perform AC/DC couplin

### Utility VIs

**Owning Palette:** Power Quality VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use the Utility VIs to perform connection conversion between Delta and Wye, perform AC/DC coupling, and set triggers on input data.

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| AC DC Coupling | Eliminates the DC component of voltage waveforms, current waveforms, or waveforms that contain both voltage and current. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |
| D to Y Conversion | Converts line voltages to phase voltages. This VI calculates an approximate value for the phase voltage in an unbalanced system because the neutral point is placed at the center of the gravity of the line voltages triangle. When calculating phase voltage values, this VI places the neutral point at the center of gravity of the line voltage triangle, which are the characteristics of a balanced three-phase electrical power system. Thus the phase voltage values this VI returns are approximate values for unbalanced system. You must use this VI before performing power measurement, one-cycle power measurement, and signaling measurement, if the input wiring is line voltage. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |
| Trigger Detector | Detects crossing of limits for the measurement that you specify and returns triggers if occur. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems except the DBL instance. Use the DBL instance to measure one channel on one line at one time. |
| Y to D Conversion | Converts phase voltages to line voltages. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |
| Zero Crossing | Calculates the zero crossing indexes for voltage waveforms, current waveforms, or waveforms that contain both voltage and current. Zero crossing is the instantaneous point at which the voltage or current turns from positive to negative or from negative to positive. This VI supports measuring single-line and multiple-line systems. |

Parent topic:

Power Quality VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=utility-vis2.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00145: Utility VIs

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `utility-vis2.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/utility-vis2.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Three Phase Power Measurement VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target. Use these Utility VIs to share waveform data between sections of your application

### Utility VIs

**Owning Palette:** Three Phase Power Measurement VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit. This topic might not match its corresponding palette in LabVIEW depending on your operating system, licensed product(s), and target.

Use these Utility VIs to share waveform data between sections of your application, to parse signals into cycle-based segments, and to build data clusters for three-phase power measurements.

| Palette Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Get ABCN Waveforms by Index | Indexes the input array of waveforms and builds the cluster typedefinition used by measurement-analysis VIs for the electric vehicle power test. |
| Get Segments (Level Trigger) | Inspects the signal for level triggers that mark the beginning of each electrical cycle and returns arrays of cycle-defined timestamps. |
| Get Segments (Phase Trigger) | Inspects the signal for phase triggers that mark the beginning of each electrical cycle and returns arrays of cycle-defined timestamps. |
| Release (SGL) | Releases the circular buffer by deleting all data value references and requesting memory deallocation. |
| Read Waveforms (SGL) | Reads the selected data from a circular buffer. Select the instance of the VI that corresponds to your processing requirements. |
| Obtain (SGL) | Obtains a buffer that can be used to connect the data source with multiple data processing elements. |
| Write Waveforms (SGL) | Writes data (an array of single-precision waveforms) to the buffer. |

Parent topic:

Three Phase Power Measurement VIs

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=vector-diagram.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00146: Vector Diagram

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `vector-diagram.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/vector-diagram.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Electrical Power Controls Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Displays vectors using an arrow style, especially for vectors of voltage and current. A vector diagram uses arrays of double complex data type and the vector elements appear in sequence in clockwise. The Vector Diagram disp

### Vector Diagram

**Owning Palette:**Electrical Power Controls
**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Displays vectors using an arrow style, especially for vectors of voltage and current. A vector diagram uses arrays of double complex data type and the vector elements appear in sequence in clockwise.

The Vector Diagram displays only the relative magnitude relationship between all voltage channels or between all current channels. The Vector Diagram does not display the relative magnitude relationship between voltage channels and current channels. All voltage channels are nominalized by the voltage channel that has the greatest magnitude and all current channels are nominalized by the current channel that has the greatest magnitude. Thus the displayed vector length of the voltage channel with the greatest magnitude is the same as the vector length of the current channel with the greatest magnitude.

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/vector_diagram.gif']
Properties

You can use the following Vector Diagram properties with the Property Node.

| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Active Vector | Use this property to get and set the active cursor and set properties and methods on that cursor. |
| Diagram:Colors:BG Color | Sets the background color of the vector diagram. |
| Diagram:Colors:FG Color | Sets the foreground color of the vector diagram. |
| Diagram:Grid:Grid Color | Sets the color of the grid. |
| Diagram:Grid:Number of Circle Grids | Sets the number of circle grids. |
| Diagram:Grid:Number of Polar Grids | Sets the number of polar grids. |
| Diagram:Size:Height | Sets the height, in pixels, of the vector diagram. |
| Diagram:Size:Width | Sets the width, in pixels, of the vector diagram. |
| Diagram:Vector:Line Width | Sets the line width, in pixels, of the active vector. |
| Diagram:Vector:Vector Color | Sets the line color of the active vector. |
| Diagram:Vector:Vector Type | Sets the vector type of the active vector. Diagram:Vector:Vector Type contains two instances with enumerated data type: voltage and current. |

Parent topic:

Electrical Power Controls

<!--NI_TOPIC bundle=labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref path=voltage-dips-swells-and-interruptions-vi.html language=enus -->
## TOPIC 00147: Voltage Dips, Swells, and Interruptions VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `voltage-dips-swells-and-interruptions-vi.html`
- source_url: https://docs-be.ni.com/bundle/labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref/raw/resource/enus/voltage-dips-swells-and-interruptions-vi.html
- document_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Owning Palette: Events VIs Requires: Electrical Power Toolkit Detects RMS voltage dips, swells, and interruptions, according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. EN 50160:2007 defines the default threshold values. You also can specify custom threshold values. You can use EN 50160:2007 to evaluate voltage events

### Voltage Dips, Swells, and Interruptions VI

**Owning Palette:** Events VIs

**Requires:** Electrical Power Toolkit

Detects RMS voltage dips, swells, and interruptions, according to IEC 61000-4-30:2008. EN 50160:2007 defines the default threshold values. You also can specify custom threshold values. You can use EN 50160:2007 to evaluate voltage events that this VI detects.

This VI supports measuring single-bus single-line or multiple-bus single-line systems.

Details Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Voltage_Dips_Swells_and_Interruptions.gif']

|  | reset? specifies whether to reset the calculation process. The default is FALSE. When you run this VI for the first time, the calculation process resets automatically. |
| --- | --- |
|  | one-cycle voltage RMS specifies RMS voltage measured over one cycle. The measurement starts at fundamental zero crossing and refreshes every half-cycle. Each element of one-cycle voltage RMS corresponds to one channel or phase. The value order must be in the sequence of Phase A (L1), Phase B (L2), and Phase C (L3). This VI does not calculate Phase N. timestamps specifies the end time of each cycle. RMS values specifies the voltage RMS value for each cycle. |
|  | timestamps specifies the end time of each cycle. |
|  | RMS values specifies the voltage RMS value for each cycle. |
|  | events settings specifies settings for events. swell threshold specifies a voltage magnitude for detecting the start and end of a voltage swell. The swell threshold is a percentage of either reference voltage or the sliding reference voltage declared by the type of reference voltage. The value of swell threshold must be greater than 100% plus the hysteresis. The default is 110. On single-phase systems, a swell begins when the voltage RMS value rises above the swell threshold and ends when the voltage RMS value is equal to or below the swell threshold minus the hysteresis. On polyphase systems, a swell begins when the voltage RMS values of one or more channels are above the swell threshold and ends when the voltage RMS values on all measured channels are equal to or below the swell threshold minus the hysteresis. dip threshold specifies a voltage magnitude specified for the purpose of detecting the start and the end of a voltage dip. The dip threshold is a percentage of either reference voltage or the sliding reference voltage declared by the type of reference voltage. dip threshold must be less than 100% minus the hysteresis and greater than the interruption threshold plus the hysteresis. The default is 90. On single-phase systems, a dip begins when the voltage RMS value falls below the dip threshold and ends when the voltage RMS value is equal to or above the dip threshold plus the hysteresis. On polyphase systems, a dip begins when the voltage RMS values of one or more channels are below the dip threshold and ends when the voltage RMS values on all measured channels are equal to or above the dip threshold plus the hysteresis. interruption threshold specifies a voltage magnitude specified for the purpose of detecting the start and the end of a voltage interruption. The interruption threshold is a percentage of either reference voltage or the sliding reference voltage declared by the type of reference voltage. interruption threshold must be less than the dip threshold minus the hysteresis. The default is 1. On single-phase systems, a interruption begins when the voltage RMS value falls below the interruption threshold and ends when the voltage RMS value is equal to, or greater than, the interruption threshold plus the hysteresis. On polyphase systems, a interruption begins when the voltage RMS values of all channels fall below the interruption threshold and ends when the voltage RMS value on any one channel is equal to, or greater than, the interruption threshold plus the hysteresis. hysteresis specifies a difference in magnitude between the start and end thresholds. The hysteresis is a percentage of either reference voltage or the sliding reference voltage declared by the type of reference voltage. Valid range of hysteresis is 0.5% to 5%. The default is 2. |
|  | swell threshold specifies a voltage magnitude for detecting the start and end of a voltage swell. The swell threshold is a percentage of either reference voltage or the sliding reference voltage declared by the type of reference voltage. The value of swell threshold must be greater than 100% plus the hysteresis. The default is 110. On single-phase systems, a swell begins when the voltage RMS value rises above the swell threshold and ends when the voltage RMS value is equal to or below the swell threshold minus the hysteresis. On polyphase systems, a swell begins when the voltage RMS values of one or more channels are above the swell threshold and ends when the voltage RMS values on all measured channels are equal to or below the swell threshold minus the hysteresis. |
|  | dip threshold specifies a voltage magnitude specified for the purpose of detecting the start and the end of a voltage dip. The dip threshold is a percentage of either reference voltage or the sliding reference voltage declared by the type of reference voltage. dip threshold must be less than 100% minus the hysteresis and greater than the interruption threshold plus the hysteresis. The default is 90. On single-phase systems, a dip begins when the voltage RMS value falls below the dip threshold and ends when the voltage RMS value is equal to or above the dip threshold plus the hysteresis. On polyphase systems, a dip begins when the voltage RMS values of one or more channels are below the dip threshold and ends when the voltage RMS values on all measured channels are equal to or above the dip threshold plus the hysteresis. |
|  | interruption threshold specifies a voltage magnitude specified for the purpose of detecting the start and the end of a voltage interruption. The interruption threshold is a percentage of either reference voltage or the sliding reference voltage declared by the type of reference voltage. interruption threshold must be less than the dip threshold minus the hysteresis. The default is 1. On single-phase systems, a interruption begins when the voltage RMS value falls below the interruption threshold and ends when the voltage RMS value is equal to, or greater than, the interruption threshold plus the hysteresis. On polyphase systems, a interruption begins when the voltage RMS values of all channels fall below the interruption threshold and ends when the voltage RMS value on any one channel is equal to, or greater than, the interruption threshold plus the hysteresis. |
|  | hysteresis specifies a difference in magnitude between the start and end thresholds. The hysteresis is a percentage of either reference voltage or the sliding reference voltage declared by the type of reference voltage. Valid range of hysteresis is 0.5% to 5%. The default is 2. |
|  | polyphase only? specifies whether this VI reports voltage events for both single-phase and polyphase systems or for only polyphase systems. The default is FALSE, which means this VI reports voltage events for both single-phase and polyphase systems. A polyphase system must contain Phase A (L1), Phase B (L2), and Phase C (L3). |
|  | system info in specifies the basic configuration of the electrical power system. Use the Initialize System VI to generate the basic configuration information. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | voltage events returns voltage events information that this VI detects. event timestamp returns the start time of the voltage event. phase returns the measurement phase of the voltage event. event type returns the type of the voltage event. extreme voltage returns the greatest voltage RMS value measured during the swell, or the least voltage RMS value measured during the dip or interruption. duration returns the time difference between the begin and the end of the event in second. |
|  | event timestamp returns the start time of the voltage event. |
|  | phase returns the measurement phase of the voltage event. |
|  | event type returns the type of the voltage event. |
|  | extreme voltage returns the greatest voltage RMS value measured during the swell, or the least voltage RMS value measured during the dip or interruption. |
|  | duration returns the time difference between the begin and the end of the event in second. |
|  | voltage flags returns voltage flags of a basic measurement time interval, which is 10 or 12 cycles. Interval is flagged during a dip, swell or interruption, if any one-cycle voltage RMS value during this interval is out of dip or swell thresholds. Each element of voltage flags corresponds to one channel or phase. |
|  | system info out returns information about the basic configuration of the electrical power system. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Voltage Dips, Swells, and Interruptions Details

**Related Information**
Line and Bus Configurations


Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Voltage Dips, Swells, and Interruptions VI:

- Events Detection (DAQmx) VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (DAQmx)
- Events Detection VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)
- Events Detection VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Applications (cRIO) (SAR)
- Multiple Bus Power Quality Applications VI: labview\examples\Electrical Power\Power Quality\Multiple Line Applications (cRIO) (Delta-Sigma)

Parent topic:

Events VIs

## TOPIC 00148: Write Waveforms (SGL) VI

- bundle_id: `labview-electrical-power-toolkit-api-ref`
- source_path: `write-waveforms-sgl-vi.html`

- page_type: `leaf`
- content_type: `reference`
- source_description: Writes data (an array of single-precision waveforms) to the buffer. Examples circular buffer in specifies the data value reference to the circular buffer to operate on. new data specifies the new, continuous waveforms to append to the existing data within the circular buffer. All waveforms in new da

### Write Waveforms (SGL) VI

Writes data (an array of single-precision waveforms) to the buffer.

Examples

[IMAGE alt='image' src='images/Write_Waveforms_(SGL).gif']

|  | circular buffer in specifies the data value reference to the circular buffer to operate on. |
| --- | --- |
|  | new data specifies the new, continuous waveforms to append to the existing data within the circular buffer. All waveforms in new data should have identical timing, including t0, dt, and number of samples. t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. dt specifies the sample interval. Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | t0 specifies the timestamp of the first sample in Y. |
|  | dt specifies the sample interval. |
|  | Y specifies the measured values. |
|  | error in describes error conditions that occur before this node runs. This input provides standard error in functionality. |
|  | circular buffer out returns an identical data value reference to the circular buffer. |
|  | error out contains error information. This output provides standard error out functionality. |

Examples

Refer to the following VIs for examples of using the Write Waveforms (SGL) VI.

- Traction Inverter Acquisition and Waveform Logging (DAQmx) VI: LabVIEW\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Traction Inverter
- Traction Inverter Power and Efficiency Measurement and Logging (DAQmx) VI: LabVIEW\examples\Electrical Power\EV Power Test\Traction Inverter
